name: Brut, NV
tableside: Beau Joie comes in a hand-armored copper-wire bottle that is instantly recognizable on the table. What matters is what's inside: ripe white peach, toasted brioche, a little honeysuckle. Genuinely good Champagne that earns its presentation.
story: Beau Joie was created with presentation in mind — the hammered copper bottle is designed to stand out on an ice bucket or a table, and it works. But the wine isn't just packaging: it's a Pinot Noir-dominant blend from Grand Cru and Premier Cru vineyards with real richness and a satisfying creamy finish. For guests who want something that makes a visual statement without sacrificing quality, this is the answer.
trivia: The copper bottle acts as an insulator, keeping the wine cooler for longer once out of the ice bucket — a genuine functional benefit, not just aesthetics.
Beau Joie sources fruit exclusively from Grand Cru and Premier Cru villages, which represents the top tier of Champagne's village classification system.
The name translates loosely to 'beautiful joy' — it was conceived explicitly as a celebration wine.

name: Brut Nature, NV
tableside: Zero dosage Champagne — nothing added after fermentation is done, no sweetness, no softening of the edges. This is Champagne in its most honest form: pure fruit, pure mineral, pure acidity. For guests who find most Champagne too sweet or too soft, this is the answer.
story: Most Champagne receives a small addition of sugar dissolved in wine — called dosage — right before the final cork goes in. It softens edges, rounds out acidity, and makes the wine more immediately approachable. Brut Nature skips that entirely. What you get is the wine as it actually is: unvarnished, high-acid, and mineral-driven. It's not for everyone, but for the guest who knows exactly what they want, it's a statement.
trivia: 'Brut Nature,' 'Zero Dosage,' and 'Non-Dosé' all mean the same thing — no sugar added. The term 'Brut' on its own actually allows up to 12 grams of sugar per liter.
Without dosage to soften it, zero-dosage Champagne requires higher quality base wine — there's nowhere to hide.
The trend toward lower and zero dosage Champagnes has accelerated significantly since the 2010s as palates have moved toward drier, more mineral styles.

name: Extra Brut, 2013
tableside: A vintage Champagne from one of the great family houses — every grape from a single exceptional year, 2013, rather than a blend of multiple harvests. A decade of aging has done extraordinary things to it. This is a special occasion bottle.
story: Billecart-Salmon is a family-owned house in Mareuil-sur-Aÿ that has operated continuously since 1818 — through wars, phylloxera, the Great Depression, and every other catastrophe the 20th century threw at Champagne. Their house style is precision: lower dosage than most houses, longer aging on the lees, and a commitment to freshness over opulence. The 2013 vintage was challenging — a cold, late season — but produced wines of remarkable tension that have aged beautifully.
trivia: Founded in 1818, Billecart-Salmon is widely considered the oldest continuously family-owned Champagne house — extraordinarily rare in a region where most houses are now owned by luxury conglomerates.
Vintage Champagne must be aged on the lees for a minimum of 3 years before release; prestige cuvées are often aged 8-10 years or more. This one has had over a decade.
The 2013 harvest in Champagne was one of the latest on record — grapes were not picked until mid-October in many villages, producing wines with exceptional natural acidity.

name: 'N°2' Extra Brut, NV
tableside: Billecart's driest, most food-focused Champagne — bone-dry, mineral, built around Chardonnay. Less fruit up front, more mineral precision. It rewards attention rather than demanding none. For the guest who finds standard Brut a bit too easy.
story: The 'N°2' designation signals this is their elevated non-vintage offering, sitting above the standard Brut in both price and intent. Chardonnay-dominant with extended lees aging, it's made in the same precision-first philosophy as everything Billecart produces. The extra brut designation means dosage is kept very low — this is a wine that asks something of the drinker rather than meeting them halfway.
trivia: Chardonnay-dominant Champagnes tend to be leaner and more mineral in their youth, developing richness with age — the opposite of Pinot Noir-dominant blends, which are rounder upfront.
The 'N°2' name is an internal house designation that positions it clearly in the Billecart lineup without needing to explain the classification system to guests.
Extra Brut allows 0–6 grams of sugar per liter; Brut allows up to 12. The difference is noticeable side by side.

name: Rendez-Vous CINQ, Pinot Noir Extra Brut, NV
tableside: A Pinot Noir-dominant blanc de noirs — red grapes pressed gently to keep the juice clear, which gives it a delicate blush and a red cherry and rose character you don't find in Chardonnay-based Champagne. Elegant, perfumed, and a genuine bridge between white and red wine drinkers at the same tabightly unexpected.
story: Blanc de noirs — 'white wine from black grapes' — is one of Champagne's most interesting styles. The juice of Pinot Noir and Pinot Meunier is naturally colorless; the color in red wine comes from extended skin contact. By pressing quickly and gently, you get a white wine with all of the aromatic complexity of a red grape variety. The result is something rounder and more fragrant than a Chardonnay-based Champagne, with a faint salmon blush and a fruit-forward character.
trivia: Blanc de noirs is one of the rarer styles in Champagne — most houses blend Chardonnay and Pinot Noir together rather than making them separately.
The 'Rendez-Vous' series from Billecart is designed to highlight individual grape variety expression rather than house blending philosophy.
Pinot Noir grown in Champagne is the same grape as red Burgundy — but the climate, winemaking, and sparkling process transform it into something completely unrecognizable.

name: Rendez-Vous No.4, Chardonnay Extra Brut, NV
tableside: Blanc de blancs — 100% Chardonnay — at its most focused. Lean, electric, and full of minerality. If a guest wants to understand what Chardonnay tastes like before oak and butter get involved, this is a great place to start.
story: The companion piece to the CINQ — same series, same extra brut dosage, different grape. Where the Pinot Noir version is round and fragrant, this is all tension and precision. Blanc de blancs Champagne made from Côte des Blancs Chardonnay is about as pure an expression of that grape as exists anywhere in the world — no oak, no malolactic softening, just the fruit and the chalk it grew in.
trivia: The Côte des Blancs — the 'slope of whites' — is a ridge south of Épernay planted almost exclusively with Chardonnay. It's considered the spiritual home of blanc de blancs Champagne.
Champagne is the world's northernmost major wine region — the marginal climate means grapes rarely over-ripen, which is why the acidity is so pronounced.
Blanc de blancs ages exceptionally well — bottles from great years can evolve for 20–30 years, developing complexity while retaining freshness.

name: Blanc de Blancs, NV
tableside: Delamotte is the sister house to Salon — the most coveted blanc de blancs in all of Champagne — and they share the same vineyards in Le Mesnil-sur-Oger. In years when Salon isn't declared, that fruit goes into Delamotte. This is extraordinary Champagne at a fraction of what Salon costs.
story: Le Mesnil-sur-Oger is a Grand Cru village on the Côte des Blancs that many consider the single greatest source of Chardonnay in Champagne. The chalk here is unusually deep and pure, giving wines a minerality and tension that's almost startling. Salon, Delamotte's famous sibling, has only been produced in 37 vintages since it was first made in 1905 — the rest of the time, that fruit becomes this. For guests who know their Champagne, mention the Salon connection. It lands every time.
trivia: Salon is the only Champagne house that produces a single wine, from a single vineyard, in a single vintage. It's declared fewer than once every two years on average.
Delamotte and Salon have been under the same ownership since 1988 when Laurent-Perrier acquired both houses together.

Le Mesnil-sur-Oger's chalk soils are estimated to be over 70 million years old — remnants of an ancient seabed. The fossilized marine shells in the chalk are part of what gives the wine its distinctive mineral character.

name: Rosé, NV
tableside: Delamotte's rosé carries the same precision and minerality as their blanc de blancs, just with a layer of red fruit character from the addition of a small percentage of Pinot Noir red wine. Elegant rather than fruity — this is serious rosé Champagne.
story: Rosé Champagne can be made two ways: either by briefly macerating the skins of red grapes with the juice (saignée method), or by blending a small amount of still red Pinot Noir wine into the base white Champagne before the second fermentation. Delamotte uses the blending method, which gives more control over the final color and flavor profile. The result is a wine that's recognizably a Delamotte — restrained, mineral, precise — with a delicate blush and a red fruit lift.
trivia: Rosé Champagne is the only wine in France where blending red and white wine together is legally permitted under AOC rules.
The precise amount of red wine added to make rosé Champagne is a closely guarded secret at most houses — it typically ranges from 5–20% of the final blend.
Delamotte rosé is made primarily from Le Mesnil Chardonnay with Pinot Noir sourced from Ambonnay, one of the Montagne de Reims' most celebrated red fruit villages.

name: Brut, 2015
tableside: Dom Pérignon needs no introduction, but the 2015 vintage deserves one — it was one of the warmest and most celebrated harvests of the decade, producing wine with unusual richness and depth. This is as good as it gets for a table-opening bottle.
story: Dom Pérignon is the prestige cuvée of Moët & Chandon, produced only in declared vintages — years the chef de cave considers exceptional enough to warrant the label. The monk Dom Pérignon himself didn't invent Champagne (that's a myth), but he did advance the craft of blending significantly in the late 17th century at the Abbey of Hautvillers. The 2015 vintage was the hottest growing season Champagne had seen in years, producing wines of uncommon richness and immediacy. A bottle like this is its own event.
trivia: Dom Pérignon is only made in declared vintages — roughly 6–8 out of every 10 years. In years where it isn't declared, the fruit goes into Moët's non-vintage blend.
The wine is typically released about 8 years after harvest — by the time it's on your list, it's already had nearly a decade of development.
Dom Pérignon releases wines in three 'plénitudes': P1 (initial release), P2 (second release, ~15 years), and P3 (~25 years). Each is considered a distinct wine with its own character.

name: Blanc de Blancs 'L'Échappée Belle,' NV
tableside: A grower Champagne — meaning the person who grew the grapes also made the wine, rather than selling the fruit to a large house. Etienne Calsac farms his own vineyards in Avize, one of Champagne's great Chardonnay villages. More terroir-specific and individual than anything a large house makes.
story: Grower Champagne has become one of the most exciting categories in the wine world over the past two decades. For most of Champagne's history, small farmers sold their grapes to the big houses — Moët, Veuve Clicquot, Krug — who then blended them into house-style wines. The récoltant-manipulant (RM) designation means the farmer made the wine themselves, which tends to produce something more specific, more personal, and often more interesting. Calsac works tiny plots in Avize on the Côte des Blancs — his wines taste like exactly where they came from.
trivia: The large Champagne houses (négociant-manipulants, or NM) buy grapes from thousands of small growers — many of those growers now bottle some of their own production under the RM designation.
Avize is a Grand Cru village rated 100% in the Champagne échelle des crus system — the maximum possible classification.
Grower Champagnes rarely go through the same level of filtration and dosage as large-house wines, giving them a more individual, sometimes more rustic character.

name: Grand Cuvée 172nd Edition, NV
tableside: Krug makes one wine — this one — and they've been perfecting it since 1843. The Grand Cuvée is a blend of up to 120 different wines from as many as 10 different vintages, assembled to express the house's idea of what Champagne should taste like. The 172nd Edition means it's the 172nd time they've made it. Nothing else tastes like this.
story: Krug's philosophy is fundamentally different from every other Champagne house. Where most producers treat non-vintage Champagne as their standard offering and vintage wine as the prestige tier, Krug inverts this — the Grand Cuvée is the statement. It's aged for a minimum of six years before release (most NV Champagne ages 15 months), blended from a library of reserve wines going back decades, and made exclusively in small oak barrels for the first fermentation. The result is something so complex and layered it almost doesn't feel like the same category of wine as other Champagnes.
trivia: Krug ages all their wines — including the Grand Cuvée — in small 205-liter oak barrels for the first fermentation. Every other major house uses stainless steel. This alone sets the flavor profile apart.
The 'Edition' number on each Grand Cuvée release identifies the year of assembly and allows collectors to compare different iterations. The 172nd Edition has a different character than the 169th.
Joseph Krug founded the house in 1843 after working at Jacquesson, with a founding philosophy that non-vintage Champagne should be as serious and expressive as vintage — a radical idea at the time.

name: Grand Siècle, NV
tableside: Grand Siècle is Laurent-Perrier's answer to vintage Champagne — but rather than using a single year, they blend three exceptional vintages together. The result is something no single year could produce: a wine with the complexity of aged Champagne and the consistency of a great blender.
story: Laurent-Perrier created Grand Siècle in 1959 with a specific philosophy: the best Champagne comes not from a single year but from combining the best qualities of multiple exceptional vintages. Each edition blends three years that complement each other, with the proportions adjusted each time based on what the chef de cave is trying to achieve. It's technically non-vintage, but it's made with the same care and intention as the finest prestige cuvées.
trivia: Grand Siècle is sometimes called 'the ideal Champagne' — a phrase Laurent-Perrier has used in their marketing for decades, and one that's hard to argue with once you've tasted it.
Laurent-Perrier is also the producer behind the iconic Cuvée Rosé — one of the world's best-selling luxury rosé Champagnes — made by the rarer saignée (skin maceration) method.
The house is based in Tours-sur-Marne, one of the few villages in Champagne that has Grand Cru status for both Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.

name: Brut Impérial, NV
tableside: The world's best-selling Champagne — and for good reason. Moët Brut Impérial is the house style of the largest and most influential estate in Champagne: balanced, crowd-pleasing, consistently made, and recognizable to virtually every guest at the table.
story: Moët & Chandon owns more vineyard land in Champagne than any other house — over 1,000 hectares — and produces roughly 30 million bottles of Brut Impérial every year. That scale makes true single-vineyard expression impossible, but it also means extraordinary resources for sourcing. The house style is deliberately accessible: Pinot Noir-dominant for body, balanced dosage for approachability, and consistent year to year regardless of harvest conditions. It's not the most complex Champagne on the list, but it's the one most guests already know and love.
trivia: Moët & Chandon was founded in 1743, making it one of the oldest Champagne houses still in operation. It has supplied Champagne to some of history's most notable figures, including Napoleon Bonaparte.
The house is now part of LVMH (Louis Vuitton Moët Hennessy), the world's largest luxury goods conglomerate, which also owns Krug, Veuve Clicquot, and Dom Pérignon.
Moët held the official Formula 1 podium Champagne designation from 1966 to 1999 — one of the most visible sponsorships in sports history. The brand returned to the F1 podium in 2025.

name: Blanc de Blancs Grand Cru, NV
tableside: A grower Champagne from Le Mesnil-sur-Oger — one of the most coveted addresses in all of Champagne for Chardonnay. Pierre Moncuit has farmed the same Grand Cru plots for generations. Precise, mineral, and distinctly site-specific.
story: The Moncuit family has been growing Chardonnay in Le Mesnil-sur-Oger for generations and making Champagne from their own fruit since the 1960s. Le Mesnil is one of only a handful of villages in Champagne rated Grand Cru for Chardonnay, and the chalk here is some of the deepest and most mineral-rich in the region. This is about as direct an expression of a single Grand Cru village as you'll find on any list.
trivia: Le Mesnil-sur-Oger is home to Salon, arguably the most sought-after Champagne in the world. Pierre Moncuit and Salon sit on essentially the same chalk — the price difference reflects brand, not terroir.

Grower Champagnes from Le Mesnil are some of the best-kept secrets in the category. The village's reputation is made by Salon and Delamotte, which means small producers like Moncuit fly under the radar.
Blanc de blancs from Le Mesnil needs time — most serious examples benefit from 5–10 years of aging, far more than the average NV Champagne.

name: Brut Zero Nature, NV
tableside: Zero dosage from one of Champagne's oldest grower families, farming in Oeuilly since 1687. No sugar added, no softening, no compromise — just fruit and chalk in their purest form. Not easy drinking, but if a guest wants to understand what Champagne actually is underneath all the house style and dosage, this is the wine.
story: The Tarlant family has been growing grapes in Œuilly since 1687 — one of the oldest continuous winegrowing families in all of Champagne. They were among the early adopters of the grower-Champagne movement and have become a reference point for terroir-driven, minimal-intervention sparkling wine. The Brut Zero is their clearest statement of philosophy: no dosage, no compromise, no house style obscuring what the vineyard actually tastes like.
trivia: The Tarlant family traces their winegrowing history back to 1687, making them one of the longest-established family estates in Champagne. The current 12th generation, Benoît and Mélanie Tarlant, farm 14 hectares across dozens of individual plots in the Marne Valley.
Zero-dosage Champagne has seen a surge in popularity among sommeliers and wine professionals, who appreciate its transparency and aging potential.

Tarlant was one of Champagne's earliest independent grower-producers — great-grandfather Louis Tarlant played a key role in establishing the AOC Champagne appellation in 1927, refusing to sell a single grape to the large houses afterward.

name: Brut 1er Cru 'Grand Cellier,' NV
tableside: Vilmart is one of the few Champagne growers who ferments and ages in oak barrels — large old foudres, not new barrique — which gives the wine a richness and texture that steel-fermented Champagnes simply don't have. For guests who love the buttery, toasty side of Champagne, this is the bottle.
story: Vilmart & Cie has been farming Premier Cru vineyards in Rilly-la-Montagne on the Montagne de Reims since 1890. What sets them apart is their commitment to oak: while virtually every major house and most growers use stainless steel for fermentation, Vilmart uses large old oak foudres for both the first and second fermentations. The result is Champagne with a richness and texture that tastes almost like still Burgundy with bubbles — in the best possible way.
trivia: Vilmart uses large foudres — barrels of 600–2000 liters — rather than the small 225-liter barriques used in Burgundy. The large format means very little direct oak flavor, but the slow oxygen exchange through the wood does something important: it rounds out the acidity, builds a creamy, almost bread-like texture, and adds the toasty warmth you taste in the glass. Think of it as the difference between a wine that was made in a tank versus one that was gently breathed into shape over months.
The Grand Cellier is Vilmart's flagship non-vintage, aged on the lees for a minimum of 3 years before release — double the legal minimum.
René Champs, who took over from his parents in the 1990s, is widely credited with elevating Vilmart to international recognition by doubling down on the oak-aging approach rather than modernizing.

name: Brut 'D Millésime,' 2014
tableside: A vintage Champagne from a cooperative house that punches well above its weight. The 2014 harvest was one of the cleaner and more precise of the decade — cool, late, and mineral. This has had a decade to develop and it shows.
story: Devaux is the brand of the Union Auboise, a cooperative of over 800 growers farming more than 1,400 hectares in the Aube — the southern and often-overlooked half of the Champagne appellation. The Aube grows predominantly Pinot Noir and produces wines with a darker, earthier character than the classic Marne Valley style. The D Millésime is their flagship vintage expression, made in exceptional years only.
trivia: The Aube is sometimes called the 'other Champagne' — it sits closer to Chablis geographically than to Épernay, and the soils are Kimmeridgian limestone rather than pure chalk.
Cooperative Champagne often gets dismissed by wine enthusiasts, but the best cooperatives control extraordinary vineyard resources that no single estate could match.
The 2014 vintage was initially underrated at release but has consistently impressed tasters over time — a classic 'sleeper' vintage.

name: Blanc de Blancs, NV
tableside: Devaux's all-Chardonnay expression — lighter, crisper, and more mineral than their Pinot Noir-dominant wines. A good bridge for guests who like white Burgundy but want bubbles, or who find standard Champagne too rich.
story: Less common in the Devaux lineup than their Pinot-driven blends, this blanc de blancs shows the house working outside its Aube comfort zone. The Chardonnay here comes from higher-acid plots, producing something leaner and more mineral than the Cuvée D. For guests who've had enough buttery Champagne and want something more electric.
trivia: Blanc de blancs represents a small fraction of total Champagne production — most of the appellation's wine contains both Chardonnay and Pinot Noir or Meunier.

The Aube produces very little Chardonnay relative to the Marne Valley — sourcing 100% Chardonnay for a blanc de blancs requires either blending across regions or finding specific cooler plots within the Aube.
All-Chardonnay Champagne is typically lower in alcohol than Pinot-dominant blends due to the grape's naturally lower sugar at harvest in this climate.

name: Grand Reserve, NV
tableside: The entry point into Devaux — honest, well-made, approachable Champagne at a price that won't make anyone flinch. For tables that want bubbles without a discussion about vintages and dosage levels.
story: The Grand Reserve sits at the base of the Devaux lineup and is designed purely for accessibility. It's not trying to be complex or age-worthy — it's trying to be delicious right now, easy to pair with anything, and priced fairly. Sometimes that's exactly what a table needs.
trivia: Devaux's Grand Reserve is one of the few Champagnes at this price point made by a house that also produces serious prestige-level wines — the winemaking infrastructure and quality standards flow down through the entire lineup.
The cooperative model allows Devaux to source from over 800 growers, giving the blending team an unusually wide range of fruit to work with even for their most basic cuvée.
At this price, it competes directly with Prosecco and Cava for casual table pours — but it's made by the traditional méthode champenoise with 15+ months of aging on the lees.

name: Hill, Brut Reserve, 2016
tableside: Austrian sparkling wine made by traditional méthode champenoise — same method as Champagne, completely different grape and place. From Burgenland in the east of Austria, near the Hungarian border. Richer and more textured than most Austrian Sekt.
story: Leo Hillinger is one of Austria's most internationally recognized wine producers, known for building a stunning hillside winery in Jois overlooking Lake Neusiedl. The Hill Sekt is made by the traditional method — secondary fermentation in bottle, extended lees aging — which gives it the biscuity, textured quality usually associated with Champagne. Burgenland's slightly warmer climate produces a riper, more generous style than the cool-climate Austrian Sekt from further north.
trivia: Burgenland is home to Lake Neusiedl, a vast shallow lake that creates a unique microclimate — warm summers for ripeness, foggy autumns for botrytis-affected sweet wines. The same conditions that make it ideal for Eiswein also produce unusually ripe sparkling grapes.
Hillinger's winery was built into a hillside and is partially underground, using geothermal cooling rather than mechanical refrigeration.
Austria has a long history of sparkling wine production — the category is called Sekt — but international recognition has only grown significantly in the last 20 years.

name: Blanc de Blancs Brut, NV
tableside: From the same estate as several of our top Grüner Veltliners, but in sparkling form. Gobelsburg makes this by the traditional method from estate Grüner Veltliner grapes. It tastes like a great Kamptal white wine with bubbles — which, in the best possible way, is exactly what it is.
story: Schloss Gobelsburg has been making wine since 1171 and their sparkling program draws on the same philosophy that drives their Grüner Veltliner and Riesling: no shortcuts, terroir first. The Chardonnay for this wine grows on the same primary rock and loess soils as their celebrated still whites, which is why it has the same tightly wound mineral backbone. If a guest already loves the Gobelsburg Grüner, this is the sparkling wine to put in front of them. style. The Blanc de Blancs is made from Grüner Veltliner — not Chardonnay, as the 'blanc de blancs' terminology suggests in France. In Austria, the term simply means 'white wine from white grapes,' which opens it to any white variety.
trivia: In Champagne, blanc de blancs means 100% Chardonnay by convention. In Austria, it legally means any white grape variety — Grüner Veltliner Sekt qualifies, as does Riesling Sekt.

Gobelsburg's sparkling wine undergoes secondary fermentation in bottle using the traditional method — the same labor-intensive process used in Champagne, rather than the tank method used for Prosecco.

The estate's winemaker Michael Moosbrugger is also a master of still wines — his approach to the sparkling program borrows directly from his white wine philosophy of low intervention and site expression.

name: Brut Rosé Sekt Reserve, NV
tableside: Gobelsburg's rosé Sekt — made from Zweigelt, Austria's signature red grape, vinified to show its floral, cherry-driven character in sparkling form. Elegant and precise in the same way all Gobelsburg wines are.
story: Zweigelt is Austria's most widely planted red grape, a crossing of Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent developed in the early 20th century. In still wine it can range from light and fresh to dark and structured. In sparkling form, it shows its most charming side: floral, red-fruited, and light on its feet. Gobelsburg brings their trademark restraint to it.
trivia: Zweigelt was developed in 1922 by Dr. Fritz Zweigelt at the Klosterneuburg wine research institute — he crossed Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent to create a grape suited to Austria's northern growing conditions.

Rosé Sekt made from Zweigelt is a distinctly Austrian style with no real international equivalent — the grape's floral character gives it a personality unlike any French or Italian rosé sparkling wine.
Gobelsburg's rosé undergoes the same traditional method secondary fermentation as their still white Sekt, with extended lees aging for texture.

name: Zweigelt Sekt Reserve, 2016
tableside: A vintage Austrian rosé Sekt from Zweigelt — our most affordable sparkling option and genuinely one of the most interesting. Serve it to guests who think they know what sparkling wine is. They don't know this.
story: Steininger is a family estate in Langenlois in Kamptal — the same appellation as several of our top Grüner Veltliners. They've built a reputation for distinctive Sekt, particularly from native Austrian grape varieties. The Zweigelt Sekt is their calling card: a sparkling wine with no real international reference point, made from a grape most guests have never heard of, in a region not associated with sparkling wine. The conversation it generates is worth the pour.
trivia: Steininger produces several varietal Sekt bottlings — Grüner Veltliner, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Zweigelt — each a completely different expression of the same méthode champenoise process.
Langenlois is Kamptal's main wine town and has been a wine-producing center since at least the 12th century, with documented sales records going back to 1137.
At $58, this is one of the best value traditional-method sparkling wines on the list — comparable in quality to Champagnes costing three times as much.

name: Riesling Sekt, Heiligenstein, 2018
tableside: Sparkling Riesling from the Heiligenstein vineyard — one of Austria's most celebrated Riesling sites. The same plot that produces some of our most serious still Rieslings, but with bubbles. The minerality and fruit intensity of the site come through clearly even in sparkling form.
story: Heiligenstein is a volcanic ridge in Kamptal that produces some of Austria's most distinctive Rieslings — mineral, precise, with a beeswax and dried-wildflower complexity that develops over time. Steininger is one of very few producers who makes a single-vineyard sparkling Riesling from this site. The result is something genuinely rare: a bottle where you can taste exactly where it came from, even through the secondary fermentation.
trivia: The petrol note in aged Riesling — and this shows it even young — comes from a compound called TDN (trimethyl dihydronaphthalene), which develops naturally as the wine ages and is specific to Riesling.
Heiligenstein's volcanic soils are geologically unlike most of the surrounding Kamptal, giving its wines an unusual mineral intensity that's identifiable even to beginning wine drinkers.
Single-vineyard sparkling wines are extraordinarily rare worldwide — the blending required for most sparkling wine production makes site specificity almost impossible.

name: Sauvignon Blanc Sekt Reserve, 2020
tableside: Sparkling Sauvignon Blanc is almost unheard of outside of Austria. This is Steininger doing something genuinely unusual: taking one of the world's most aromatic white grapes and adding bubbles. The herbal, citrus-driven character of the grape comes through vividly.
story: Sauvignon Blanc as a sparkling wine is a curiosity that Austria has largely to itself. In most wine regions, Sauvignon Blanc is made still and dry — the aromatics are considered too intense and too specific for the sparkling format. Steininger disagrees, and makes this Reserve Sekt by the traditional method. It's not for everyone, but for guests who are curious about what sparkling wine can be, it's a fascinating conversation.
trivia: Austria produces more variety in sparkling wine than almost any other country — Grüner Veltliner, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Zweigelt, Pinot Blanc all appear as varietal Sekt from serious producers.
The traditional method (méthode champenoise) is used for all Steininger Sekt — each bottle undergoes secondary fermentation and is riddled and disgorged by hand.
Sauvignon Blanc's highly aromatic character means it retains varietal identity even through the process of secondary fermentation — something that more neutral grapes often lose.

name: Grüner Veltliner Sekt Reserve, 2021
tableside: Grüner Veltliner with bubbles — and the white pepper character that makes the grape so distinctive comes through even in sparkling form. This is one of the most unusual and fun pours on the list. Guests who love Grüner will be genuinely surprised.
story: Grüner Veltliner's defining characteristic — the white pepper note from the rotundone compound — is resilient enough to survive the secondary fermentation process and remain clearly identifiable in the finished wine. Steininger's sparkling Grüner is essentially a still GV with fine bubbles added, which means all the things that make still Grüner so food-friendly — the acidity, the savory herb quality, the mineral drive — are present and accounted for.
trivia: This is the only sparkling Grüner Veltliner in the world available on a restaurant wine list at serious scale — Steininger essentially invented the category commercially.
The Kamptal appellation is better known for still wines, but Steininger's Sekt program has done more to put the region on the international sparkling wine map than almost anything else.

Grüner Veltliner's high natural acidity makes it an excellent base for sparkling wine — the same quality that makes it so food-friendly in still form keeps the sparkling version refreshing rather than flabby.

name: Cab Rosé Sekt Reserve, 2023
tableside: Cabernet Sauvignon as the base for a sparkling rosé — unusual even by Austrian Sekt standards. The grape's firm structure and red fruit character translate into something elegant and slightly savory. Interesting for guests who want something they've genuinely never had before.
story: Cabernet Sauvignon in sparkling rosé form is genuinely unusual — Steininger is one of very few producers working with this combination. The grape's firm tannin structure softens in the sparkling process, leaving a wine that's floral and red-fruited with a slight savory edge. It's the newest addition to Steininger's Sekt portfolio and one of the most conversation-starting bottles in the cellar.
trivia: Cabernet Franc is increasingly being grown in Austria — originally planted for blending, it's now produced as a varietal wine in several regions and is proving particularly well-suited to the country's climate.
The 2023 vintage in Niederösterreich was warm and ripe, producing Cabernet Franc with more immediate fruit character than cooler years.

Steininger is now one of the world's most comprehensive producers of single-varietal traditional-method sparkling wines — they essentially offer a complete survey of Austrian white and red grapes in Sekt form.

name: Chardonnay Brut, NV
tableside: Brazilian sparkling wine — and genuinely good. The Geisse family came from Croatia to Brazil in the 1970s and built one of South America's most respected sparkling wine operations in the high-altitude Serra Gaúcha region. This is not a novelty pour; it's a serious wine from an unexpected place.
story: Mario Geisse came to Brazil from Croatia in 1975 and recognized that the high-altitude vineyards of Serra Gaúcha — at around 700–800 meters elevation with cool nights — had the conditions needed for fine sparkling wine. He was right. Família Geisse is now considered the benchmark for Brazilian sparkling, and international critics regularly note it favorably against entry-level Champagne. The surprise factor when you tell guests it's Brazilian is genuine every time.
trivia: The Serra Gaúcha region in southern Brazil shares a latitude with northern Argentina but sits at elevations that create a much cooler, more continental climate than most people associate with Brazil.
Brazil is the 3rd largest sparkling wine producing country in the Americas after the United States and Argentina — almost none of it is exported, which is why it's so unfamiliar internationally.
Mario Geisse's son Manuel now runs the estate and has expanded the portfolio while maintaining his father's traditional-method philosophy.

name: Brut Rosé, Willamette Valley, NV
tableside: Oregon Pinot Noir in sparkling form — and it shows. Gran Moraine is one of the Willamette Valley's most dedicated sparkling producers, and the rosé has all the earthiness and red fruit complexity that makes Oregon Pinot so compelling as a still wine.
story: Gran Moraine was established specifically to make high-quality traditional-method sparkling wine from Willamette Valley Pinot Noir — a category that barely existed in Oregon a decade ago. The estate sits in the Yamhill-Carlton AVA, which has become one of the valley's most celebrated sub-appellations for Pinot Noir. Making sparkling wine from Pinot Noir in this climate requires harvesting earlier than for still wine — before full ripeness — to preserve the acidity that makes good sparkling wine.
trivia: The Willamette Valley's cool, wet climate is frequently compared to Burgundy and Champagne — not California. Rain during harvest is a genuine concern, which has historically pushed some producers toward earlier picking — ideal for sparkling wine.
Gran Moraine is owned by the same family as Archery Summit, one of Oregon's most celebrated Pinot Noir estates — the sparkling program benefits from deep Willamette Valley farming expertise.
Oregon's sparkling wine category is growing rapidly — several dedicated producers have launched in the past decade, all working with Pinot Noir and Chardonnay in a Champagne-influenced style.

name: Chardonnay 'Prestige,' 2019
tableside: Virginia sparkling wine — and the best on our local list. Rappahannock Cellars has been making traditional-method sparkling from estate Chardonnay for over a decade. A great way to open with something local and genuinely impressive.
story: Rappahannock Cellars sits in Huntly, about 65 miles west of Washington D.C., in the Blue Ridge foothills. They make their Prestige using the same traditional Champagne method: hand-harvested Chardonnay, whole-cluster pressed, secondary fermentation in bottle, aged on the lees before disgorgement. That process takes years and costs significantly more than tank-method sparkling. They do it because the wine earns it — and the result is a Virginia sparkling with genuine complexity and a toasty, lees-driven depth that most domestic bubbly never approaches. Worth championing to guests who assume Virginia wine means red Bordeaux blends. of. The Prestige is their flagship — Chardonnay grown on the estate, secondary fermentation in bottle, disgorgement by hand. It's not trying to be Champagne; it's trying to be Virginia, and it succeeds.
trivia: Virginia has been called 'the next great East Coast wine region' so many times it's become a cliché — but the sparkling category in particular has developed faster than anyone expected, driven by Chardonnay and Viognier grown at elevation in the Blue Ridge foothills.

Thomas Jefferson spent decades trying to grow European wine grapes at Monticello and largely failed — modern Virginia winemakers have solved the disease and humidity problems he faced through better rootstocks, canopy management, and site selection.
Rappahannock Cellars sources fruit from their estate in Huntly, where the Blue Ridge Mountains create a significant rain shadow that reduces the humidity that plagues much of Virginia viticulture.

name: Merlot/Cab Franc 'Plateau' Rosé, 2024
tableside: A Virginia rosé sparkling from one of Loudoun County's most consistent producers. Walsh Family farms in Purcellville, just inside the Blue Ridge foothills, and this Merlot/Cab Franc blend has the earthy, herbal edge that distinguishes Virginia rosé from California or Provence.
story: Walsh Family Wine has been one of Loudoun County's steadiest producers since launching in the 2010s. Their Plateau Rosé sparkling is a blending exercise in classic Bordeaux varieties grown in Virginia conditions — Merlot for roundness and red fruit, Cab Franc for the herbaceous edge and structure. The 2024 vintage was an excellent year in Virginia with warm, dry conditions through harvest.
trivia: Loudoun County, Virginia, has more licensed wineries than any county in the Eastern United States — over 40 at last count — making it one of the most concentrated wine regions outside of California.

Cabernet Franc performs unusually well in Virginia's climate, retaining the herbaceous, bell pepper character that is often cooked away in warmer regions — it's considered by many to be Virginia's red grape.
Walsh Family's Purcellville operation sits at approximately 500 meters elevation, which provides meaningfully cooler temperatures than the Virginia Piedmont valleys below.

name: 'Animante' Extra Brut, Franciacorta DOCG, NV
tableside: Franciacorta is Italy's answer to Champagne — same grapes, same method, same extended lees aging, same strict production rules. Barone Pizzini is one of the region's top organic producers. Think of it as Italian Champagne made with Italian grapes and Italian discipline.
story: Franciacorta sits in Lombardy, just south of Lake Iseo, where the lake's moderating influence creates surprisingly Champagne-like growing conditions. The DOCG requires traditional-method production, 18+ months lees aging for non-vintage (25+ for vintage), and disgorgement by hand. Barone Pizzini has farmed organically since 1995 — one of the first estates in the appellation to do so — and their wines carry the precision and mineral clarity that comes from healthy soils.
trivia: Franciacorta received DOCG status in 1995 — Italy's highest wine classification — making it the only Italian sparkling wine to achieve this designation.
The name 'Animante' means 'giving life' or 'animating' in Italian — a reference to the secondary fermentation that creates the bubbles.
Barone Pizzini has been certified organic since 1995 — among the earliest organic certifications in Franciacorta and one of the oldest in Italian wine production.

name: Prosecco Brut, Veneto, NV
tableside: The real Prosecco — from one of the Valdobbiadene DOCG's historic family estates, not a mass-market brand. Lighter, more delicate, and more floral than Champagne. For guests who want something beautiful and unbothered.
story: Bisol has been farming in Valdobbiadene since 1542 — one of the oldest wine families in Veneto. Prosecco uses the Charmat method: secondary fermentation happens in a large pressurized tank rather than in the bottle. This preserves the fresh, primary fruit character — the white peach and apple blossom you smell in the glass are exactly what the grapes tasted like at harvest. The tank methodtation happens in pressurized tanks rather than individual bottles. This is a deliberate choice that preserves the fresh, floral aromatics of the Glera grape rather than developing the brioche and toasty notes of bottle fermentation. Prosecco is supposed to smell like spring — tanks keep it that way.
trivia: The Bisol family traces documented winemaking on their land to 1542, making them one of the oldest continuously operating wine families in Italy.

Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG is made from Glera grapes grown on dramatically steep hillsides — the Rive (single-village) and Cartizze (single hill) designations are the pinnacle of the category.

Prosecco's Charmat method — tank secondary fermentation — costs a fraction of the traditional method and produces results quickly. This is why Prosecco is generally priced lower than Champagne or Franciacorta.

name: Lambrusco, Emilia-Romagna, 2022
tableside: Sparkling red wine — and not the sweet, cheap version most people have encountered. Cleto Chiarli makes Lambrusco the way it was made for centuries in Emilia-Romagna: dry, earthy, slightly tannic, and built to cut through the region's rich food. This is one of the most food-friendly wines on the list.
story: Lambrusco has a terrible reputation in the United States thanks to the mass-market sweet versions that flooded the market in the 1970s and 80s. The real thing — the dry, serious Lambrusco made by old families in Modena and Reggio Emilia — is something entirely different. Cleto Chiarli is a historic Modena producer that has resisted the commercial pressures toward sweetness. Their Lambrusco is the wine people in Emilia-Romagna actually drink with their food — with prosciutto di Parma, with tortellini in brodo, with the kind of rich, fatty food that needs an acidic, slightly tannic sparkling wine to cut through it.
trivia: Emilia-Romagna produces some of Italy's richest food — Parmigiano-Reggiano, Prosciutto di Parma, Mortadella, and tagliatelle al ragù all come from this region. Dry Lambrusco evolved specifically as a foil to this food culture.
The 'Lambrusco' name refers not to one grape but to a family of native Emilian grapes — Lambrusco Grasparossa, Sorbara, and Salamino being the main varieties. Chiarli uses traditional local clones.
Cleto Chiarli was founded in 1860 and is one of the oldest continuously operating Lambrusco producers in existence.

name: Franciacorta Brut, NV
tableside: Ferghettina is one of Franciacorta's most respected estates — larger than Barone Pizzini but equally serious about quality. Traditional method, minimum lees aging, Chardonnay-dominant. A step up in richness from the Animante, slightly more in the mold of a quality NV Champagne.
story: Roberto Gatti founded Ferghettina in 1991 after leaving his position as an agronomist to pursue his own wine project. The estate has grown to over 50 hectares and produces some of Franciacorta's most consistent wines. Their Brut is the entry point to the lineup and represents everything the appellation is supposed to be: traditional method, long lees contact, and the mineral character that comes from the glacial moraine soils of Lombardy.
trivia: Franciacorta's name is thought to derive from 'Franzacurta' — a medieval designation meaning 'exempt from taxes,' referring to the monastic holdings that once dominated the area.

The glacial moraine soils of Franciacorta were deposited by glaciers retreating from the Alps during the last ice age — they are geologically similar in structure (if not in composition) to the chalky soils of Champagne.
Italy consumes roughly 75% of all Franciacorta produced — it has an intensely domestic market and relatively limited international distribution.

name: 'Concerto' Lambrusco, Emilia-Romagna, 2021
tableside: The other Lambrusco on the list — from a different producer and subzone than the Chiarli. Medici Ermete's Concerto is considered one of the reference wines for serious dry Lambrusco: earthy, structured, and built to last the entire meal. For guests open to something genuinely unexpected.
story: Medici Ermete farms in the Reggio Emilia hills — the Reggiano subzone of Lambrusco, which many consider the most structured and age-worthy of the appellation's sub-designations. The Concerto is their flagship and has won multiple international awards, doing more to rehabilitate Lambrusco's reputation internationally than almost any other wine. It's made from 100% Lambrusco Salamino di Santa Croce, one of the most structured varieties in the family.
trivia: Medici Ermete's Concerto was listed in Wine Spectator's Top 100 Wines of the Year in 2004 — a moment that single-handedly changed many American wine professionals' perception of Lambrusco.
Lambrusco Reggiano — from the Reggio Emilia hills — is generally considered more structured and age-worthy than the flatter Modena versions, due to elevation and limestone soils.
The Medici family has been farming in Reggio Emilia since 1890, but the estate's international reputation was built largely by Alberto Medici, who took over in the 1980s.

name: Willamette Valley Rosé, 2022
tableside: Oregon Pinot Noir rosé — light, precise, dry. Argyle is one of Willamette Valley's most reliable producers and this is exactly what you want rosé to be: nothing heavy, nothing sweet, just clean red-fruit freshness with the kind of acidity that makes you reach for another glass.
story: Argyle was founded in 1987 with an explicit focus on sparkling wine and cool-climate varieties. Their still rosé program emerged from the same philosophy: Pinot Noir grown in cool Willamette conditions, harvested early, handled gently. The 2022 vintage was warm and generous in Oregon, producing rosés with slightly more body than cooler years while retaining the acidity that defines Willamette wine.
trivia: Argyle was one of the first Oregon wineries to focus specifically on traditional method sparkling wine.
Willamette Valley rosé is almost exclusively made from Pinot Noir — giving it a different character from Provençal rosés made from Grenache and Mourvèdre.
Oregon's wine country sits between the Coast Range and the Cascades — the two ranges create a rain shadow producing a drier, sunnier growing season than Pacific Coast averages would suggest.

name: Rosé of Grenache, Santa Barbara County, 2022
tableside: Clementine Carter makes some of the most thoughtful wines in Santa Barbara — small production, site-specific. This Grenache rosé is closer to Bandol than anything from Provence: more structured, more mineral, with a savory herb quality that makes it genuinely food-versatile.
story: Clementine Carter is a winemaker working across small vineyards in Santa Barbara County — she sources from some of the best sites in the region. The Grenache rosé comes from old-vine material in a warm, rocky vineyard that produces the kind of concentrated, mineral-driven Grenache you find in southern France.
trivia: Grenache is the most planted red grape in the world by volume — the foundation of Châteauneuf-du-Pape, Priorat, and many of Spain's most celebrated wines.
Bandol, in Provence, is considered the gold standard for serious dry rosé — made primarily from Mourvèdre, it can age for decades.
Santa Barbara County's east-west mountain orientation is unique in California — it allows cold Pacific air to penetrate directly inland, creating some of the coolest growing conditions in the state.

name: Rosé of Nero d'Avola, Sicily, 2021
tableside: Sicilian rosé from Nero d'Avola — the island's signature red grape. Not the pale, delicate Provence style: this is bolder, more Mediterranean, with a sun-baked savory quality that makes it feel like a trip to Agrigento. Interesting and different at a very easy price.
story: Morgante is a family estate in Agrigento province, in Sicily's hot, arid interior. Nero d'Avola in rosé form translates those dark fruit and Mediterranean herb qualities into something savory and slightly rustic in the best possible way: a wine that tastes like the food it was made to accompany.
trivia: Nero d'Avola — 'black grape of Avola' — is named after the town of Avola in Syracuse province. It's been grown on the island for at least 2,000 years.
Sicily historically sold most of its production in bulk to northern Italian and French producers — a practice that has largely ended as the island built its own reputation.
The volcanic soils around Mount Etna have attracted enormous international investment — but Morgante's inland Agrigento region represents a more traditional face of Sicilian wine.

name: Secco Pinot Noir Rosé, NV
tableside: Hillinger's Secco is the lightest, most approachable sparkling on the list — lightly fizzy, strawberry-forward, bone dry. Made the same way as Prosecco but from Austrian Pinot Noir. The right pour when someone wants something pink, festive, and effortless.
story: Secco is the Austrian term for lightly sparkling wines made by the tank method — CO2 is introduced after fermentation rather than through a secondary fermentation in bottle, producing a softer, more immediately approachable fizz than traditional method sparkling. Hillinger farms organically in Burgenland and this is their most casual, crowd-friendly bottle: a rosé fizz built entirely for pleasure. The Pinot Noir brings genuine red fruit rather than the neutral base you'd find in a lot of light sparkling rosés.
trivia: The tank method — also called the Charmat method — was developed by Frédéric Charmat in France in 1907 and is the same process used for Prosecco. It preserves fresh, primary fruit flavors better than traditional method but produces softer, less persistent bubbles.
Hillinger's organic certification means no synthetic pesticides, herbicides, or fertilizers — unusual for a producer at this price and volume level.

Burgenland's warm Pannonian climate makes it one of Austria's most reliable regions for ripe, fruit-forward wines — the same warmth that produces great Blaufränkisch reds gives this rosé its generous strawberry character.

name: Zweigelt Rosé, 2024
tableside: A Wachau rosé from the Mittelbach family — six generations farming the same stone terraces that produce some of Austria's greatest Riesling and Grüner. Made from Zweigelt, Austria's signature red grape. Bright, dry, and genuinely refreshing. The most food-friendly rosé on the list.
story: Tegernseerhof is run by Martin Mittelbach — the sixth generation of his family to farm these steep Wachau terraces, which means his family has been tending these vines since before the American Civil War. The estate is a member of Vinea Wachau, the association that administers the region's strict Codex — a set of rules about farming, yields, and winemaking that goes significantly beyond what Austrian law requires. That same precision applied to Riesling and Grüner Veltliner is applied here to a simple, beautiful dry rosé from Zweigelt. It's the kind of wine that makes you realize how seriously Austrians take every bottle they make.
trivia: Zweigelt is Austria's most planted red grape — a cross of Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent created by Fritz Zweigelt at the Klosterneuburg institute in 1922.

The Vinea Wachau Codex is a voluntary but rigorous set of standards that member estates commit to — it prohibits chapitalization (adding sugar to boost alcohol), limits yields, and requires estate-grown fruit. It's one of the strictest self-regulatory codes in European wine.
The Wachau is better known for white wine, but Zweigelt rosé is a regional tradition — the grape's natural acidity and red fruit character translate beautifully into the style.

name: Brut 'Ace of Spades,' NV
tableside: The Ace of Spades. One of the most recognizable bottles in the world — the gold-lacquered metal bottle designed by Jay-Z's team has made this the default Champagne of celebration culture. The wine inside is genuinely excellent: a blend of Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Pinot Meunier from top Champagne villages, made with real care. For any table where the occasion demands a statement.
story: Armand de Brignac was created by the Cattier family, a historic Champagne house, and relaunched with bold new packaging in 2006. Jay-Z became associated with it after famously featuring the gold bottle in a music video as an implicit response to Cristal — and later acquired the brand outright in 2021. The wine itself is a prestige cuvée made from top Champagne fruit: first-press juice only, multiple vintages blended for consistency, and aged for years before release. It's easy to dismiss as a celebrity brand and wrong to do so.
trivia: Jay-Z first became associated with Armand de Brignac in 2006, acquired full ownership in 2014, and in 2021 sold a 50% stake to Moët Hennessy — making it one of the most unusual partnerships in luxury wine history.
The gold bottle is not simply painted — each one is hand-lacquered in multiple coats with a process that takes several days. The pewter spade emblem on each bottle is also applied by hand.

The Cattier family, who produce the wine, founded their Champagne house in 1763 — one of the oldest in the region. Their prestige cuvée Clos du Moulin, from a walled vineyard in Chigny-les-Roses, is one of Champagne's most respected single-vineyard expressions.

name: Brut Sekt, Kamptal — Magnum
tableside: Bründlmayer's Kamptal Sekt in magnum — the same Grüner Veltliner and Riesling-based traditional-method sparkling that anchors the Austrian section, in a format built for celebrating. A magnum of Austrian Sekt at a table is a genuine statement of individuality. For parties that want something extraordinary and want to be different.
story: Willi Bründlmayer makes some of the most respected sparkling wine in Austria — traditional method, from Kamptal fruit, aged on the lees for several years before release. In magnum, the aging process slows further, producing a wine of exceptional finesse and depth. The Practicing (PR) certification reflects the estate's commitment to minimal intervention in both vineyard and cellar.
trivia: Magnums (1.5L) age more slowly than standard bottles because the ratio of wine to oxygen entering through the cork is lower — resulting in finer, more persistent bubbles and more complex development over time.

Bründlmayer is one of only a handful of Austrian producers making serious traditional-method sparkling wine — most Austrian Sekt is tank-method. The commitment to traditional method reflects the same philosophy the estate applies to its still whites.

The Kamptal's cool climate and limestone soils produce base wines with the natural acidity and freshness that traditional-method sparkling requires — the same characteristics that make Champagne possible in northeastern France.

name: Brut 'Cuvée D,' NV — Magnum
tableside: Veuve Devaux's Cuvée D in magnum — the same prestige cuvée from the Aube that's on the regular list, in a format that turns a great Champagne into an event. The Aube is Champagne's most distinctive sub-region: Pinot Noir country, with a different soil type from the Marne heartland that gives the wines their characteristic richness.
story: Devaux is a cooperative in the Aube — the southernmost sub-region of Champagne, often overlooked in favor of the Marne heartland around Épernay and Reims. The Cuvée D is their prestige expression: a blend that prioritizes Aube Pinot Noir for its richness and depth, aged for extended time on the lees. In magnum, that depth becomes even more pronounced.
trivia: The Aube's soils are Kimmeridgian limestone — the same geological formation as Chablis, 80 kilometers to the south. This gives Aube Champagne a mineral, almost Burgundian quality that distinguishes it from the chalk-based Marne heartland.

Veuve Devaux is a cooperative — meaning it's owned collectively by the growers who supply fruit, rather than by a single family or corporation. Cooperatives produce about 10% of Champagne's total volume but often represent excellent value.

The Cuvée D name is a reference to Champagne's historical association with gastronomy — 'D' standing for 'dégustation,' or tasting. The bottle is designed to accompany a meal rather than just celebrate.

name: Ried Loiserberg
tableside: The entry point to serious Austrian Grüner — a single vineyard, high-elevation Kamptal white with snappy acidity, a stony mineral thread, and that signature white pepper finish. Clean, precise, built for food.
story: Bründlmayer is the estate you point to when a guest asks why Austrian wine matters. Their whole philosophy is restraint — no chasing ripeness, no flattering the wine with oak, just letting the vineyard speak. The Loiserberg site sits at elevation on primary rock, which is why the wine has that chiseled, almost stony quality. It's the kind of bottle that makes Chardonnay fans reconsider everything.
trivia: The white pepper note in Grüner Veltliner comes from rotundone — the same compound that makes Syrah peppery. You can't add it, you can't fake it.
Kamptal DAC rules require the wine to be completely dry and made only from Grüner Veltliner or Riesling — the appellation is built around purity.
Bründlmayer also makes one of Austria's best sparkling Rieslings from the same soils — a completely different expression worth mentioning to curious guests.

name: Ried Berg Vogelsang – 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: The 2022 Berg Vogelsang — same premier cru site as the other Berg Vogelsang expressions on the list, but kept as a separate cask selection showing slightly different character. More primary, perhaps slightly more closed, but from the same exceptional vineyard. For guests who want to explore what barrel selection does within a single vintage.
story: Berg Vogelsang — 'mountain birdsong' — is one of Kamptal's 1ÖTW classified vineyards. Having two separate cask selections from the same 2022 harvest allows a genuine comparison of how individual barrels within the same vineyard and vintage diverge. The differences are subtle but real, the kind of nuance that serious Austrian wine drinkers specifically seek out.
trivia: The 1ÖTW classification is awarded to vineyards with a documented track record of exceptional quality spanning at least 25 consecutive years — fewer than 100 vineyards in all of Austria qualify.
Berg Vogelsang faces southwest at 260–380 meters above sea level on primary rock soils — the combination of elevation, aspect, and lean soils produces wines that reward patience.
Having two cask selections from the same vintage is effectively a single-vineyard barrel comparison — an exercise typically reserved for winery tastings rather than restaurant lists.

name: Ried Berg Vogelsang 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: The 2023 Berg Vogelsang — the youngest of three consecutive Bründlmayer Berg Vogelsang vintages on the list. The most energetic, most tightly wound, with the most future ahead of it. Having 2022 and 2023 from the same premier cru site is an unusual opportunity to see what a single year does to a wine.
story: This is the third consecutive Bründlmayer Berg Vogelsang vintage on the list — 2022 and 2023 in different cask selections. Having multiple vintages of the same premier cru site allows a genuine conversation about how growing conditions shape expression without changing terroir, producer, or winemaking philosophy.
trivia: Berg Vogelsang translates to 'mountain birdsong' — the vineyard name appears in historical records dating back several centuries.
The 1ÖTW classification requires vineyards to have maintained exceptional quality for at least 25 consecutive years — fewer than 100 qualify in all of Austria.
Practicing sustainable viticulture (PR) means Bründlmayer follows organic and sustainable principles without pursuing formal certification — common among top European estates.

name: Ried Spiegel Vincent 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: Bründlmayer's Ried Spiegel Vincent is their most serious single-vineyard Grüner — a premier cru site that produces wines with more texture and depth than the Berg Vogelsang series. The 2021 is showing beautifully right now: settled, mineral, and composed.
story: The Spiegel is one of Kamptal's classified 1ÖTW vineyards and Bründlmayer's top Grüner expression. The 'Vincent' designation refers to a specific selection within the site — the best barrels from the best parcels, blended to produce the estate's most concentrated and age-worthy Grüner. At four years, the 2021 has just entered a beautiful drinking window: the primary citrus and pepper of a young Grüner have softened into something more textured and complex, while the freshness remains fully intact.
trivia: The Spiegel vineyard sits on primary rock soils at elevation, which limits yields naturally and concentrates flavor without needing to intervene in the cellar.
2021 was a cool, precise vintage in Kamptal — wines from this year are built for medium-term aging and are generally more structured than the warmer 2022 and 2023 vintages.
The 'Vincent' designation is Bründlmayer's own internal classification for their top barrel selections — it has no legal definition but signals their finest Grüner Veltliner expression.

name: Ried Renner Erste Lage 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: A mature Erste Lage Grüner from one of Kamptal's oldest estates — the Renner site, farmed by Gobelsburg for centuries. The 2016 is now a decade old and showing the integration and depth that makes aged Grüner so compelling. The acidity is still very much alive.
story: The Renner vineyard sits directly below the Schloss Gobelsburg castle on deep loess soils over primary rock — one of the estate's most historically significant sites. At nearly a decade, this 2016 is showing what Austrian Grüner does with time: the sharp white pepper and citrus of youth have given way to something more rounded and contemplative, while the firm Kamptal acidity continues to hold everything together.
trivia: Schloss Gobelsburg has been making wine since 1171 — documented in Cistercian monastic records that still exist in Austrian archives.
Loess soils produce wines with a slightly rounder, more generous texture than primary rock sites. This is why the Renner feels softer than the Steinsetz despite coming from the same estate.
The word 'Erste Lage' is Austria's designation for premier cru vineyards — the same sites as 1ÖTW, just the German language version of the classification term.

name: Ried Lamm Erste Lage 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: Gobelsburg's Ried Lamm — a different premier cru site from the Renner, and a noticeably different character. Where the Renner is round and generous, the Lamm is lean and angular. The 2021 is tight and needs a little time, but the mineral precision is already evident.
story: The Lamm vineyard sits on steeper, rockier ground than the Renner — primary rock rather than loess — which is why the wine has a sharper, more mineral personality. Having both the Renner 2016 and the Lamm 2021 on the list is an instructive comparison: same estate, same producer, but two completely different soil types producing two completely different wines. The Renner shows you what loess does; the Lamm shows you what primary rock does.
trivia: Primary rock soils — granite, gneiss, schist — drain quickly and force vine roots deep in search of water, which concentrates flavor and builds the mineral tension that defines the Lamm style.
The Lamm is one of the steeper vineyards in the Gobelsburg portfolio — steep enough that tractor work is limited and most tasks are done by hand.
2021 was a cool vintage in Kamptal, producing wines with higher natural acidity than the warmer 2022 and 2023 — the Lamm 2021 is particularly well-suited to aging.

name: Ried Lamm Erste Lage 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: The 2022 Lamm — one year later than the 881, noticeably more open and generous. The warmer vintage has given the wine more immediate fruit and texture. For guests who want the Lamm's mineral precision without the waiting.
story: Having both the 2021 and 2022 Lamm on the list is the same comparative opportunity as the Bründlmayer Berg Vogelsang series — same premier cru vineyard, different vintage personalities. The 2021 is tighter and needs time; the 2022 is more generous and drinkable now. Neither is better — they're different expressions of the same great site.
trivia: The 2022 vintage was one of the warmest in Kamptal in recent decades — but the Lamm's steep, rocky site retained enough natural acidity to keep the wine fresh.
Schloss Gobelsburg releases both the 2021 and 2022 Lamm simultaneously to allow guests and staff to compare vintages directly — an unusual and instructive choice.
The Erste Lage classification requires estates to submit their wines for blind tasting evaluation every year — it's not a permanent designation but must be earned with each vintage.

name: Ried Lamm 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: The third producer on the Lamm vineyard — Hirsch's interpretation sits between Gobelsburg's richness and Bründlmayer's precision. Biodynamic farming from one of Austria's most thoughtful winemakers. The 2021 is wound tight and built for the long term.
story: The Lamm vineyard is farmed by both Schloss Gobelsburg and Hirsch, and comparing their wines is one of the most instructive exercises on this list. Gobelsburg's Lamm tends toward more body and generosity; Hirsch's version is leaner, more mineral-driven, and more austere. Same primary rock soils, same classification, but two winemakers with very different philosophies. Johannes Hirsch farms biodynamically and intervenes minimally in the cellar, which gives his wines a particular purity that guests who pay close attention tend to notice.
trivia: Johannes Hirsch converted his estate to biodynamic farming in the early 2000s and has since become one of Austria's most vocal advocates for the practice.
The Lamm vineyard's name — meaning 'lamb' in German — has nothing to do with the animal. It's believed to derive from an old regional dialect term for the shape of the slope.
Hirsch's wines are made with minimal sulfur additions compared to most Austrian producers — a commitment to naturalness that adds complexity but requires careful handling in storage.

name: Langenlois, Ried Gaisberg 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: Hirsch's Gaisberg — a different premier cru site from the Lamm, with a slightly warmer exposure and more generous character. Still the Hirsch precision and purity, but a little more approachable on day one. A great entry point into Hirsch's single-vineyard range.
story: The Gaisberg is one of Kamptal's most celebrated premier cru sites — a south-facing slope above Langenlois on gneiss and primary rock soils. Hirsch farms it with the same biodynamic approach as the Lamm, but the warmer exposure produces a wine with more immediate fruit and a slightly softer mineral edge. For guests who want the Hirsch quality but find the Lamm too austere, the Gaisberg is the right recommendation.
trivia: Langenlois is the largest wine town in Austria — the main street is lined with wine estates and the town has been a center of Grüner Veltliner production for centuries.
The Gaisberg faces south-southwest, giving it significantly more sun exposure than north or east-facing sites — which is why the wine has more ripe fruit character than the Lamm.
Practicing sustainable viticulture (PR) is Hirsch's specific designation — they follow biodynamic principles without maintaining full Demeter certification.

name: Ried Kittmannsberg 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: Hiedler's top Grüner — the Kittmannsberg is their premier cru site and the most serious wine in their range. Precise, mineral, and built for food. The 2021 is in a good place right now: past the initial tightness of a young Grüner, with plenty of freshness still ahead.
story: Hiedler has been farming Kamptal since the 1980s — young by Austrian standards, but they've established themselves quickly as a serious producer. The Kittmannsberg is their most important site: a classified Erste Lage on primary rock that produces their most concentrated and age-worthy Grüner. The 2021 vintage gave them a precise, structured wine that sits comfortably alongside the more famous estates in the appellation.
trivia: Hiedler is one of the younger established estates in Kamptal — founded in 1979, they've been producing wines for just under 50 years compared to Gobelsburg's 800+ years.
The Kittmannsberg site faces primarily east and southeast — a classic orientation for Kamptal Grüner, giving morning sun while protecting the vines from the hottest afternoon temperatures.
Practicing sustainable viticulture at Hiedler means cover crops between vine rows, no synthetic chemicals, and vineyard management focused on encouraging biodiversity.

name: Ried Kittmannsberg 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: The older Kittmannsberg — nine years on this wine and it shows. The primary citrus and pepper have softened into something more layered and harmonious. For guests who want to taste what Kamptal Grüner does with time, this is an excellent and accessible example.
story: Having both the 2017 and 2021 Kittmannsberg on the list gives the same comparative opportunity as the Gobelsburg Lamm vintages — same site, same producer, years apart. The 2017 has had seven years to integrate and shows a softer, more harmonious character than the younger wine. Not better, just different — and a useful demonstration for guests who ask whether Austrian whites actually age.
trivia: 2017 was a warm, generous vintage in Kamptal — the wines developed more quickly than cooler years and are now at a beautiful peak of maturity.

Unlike red wines where aging is often obvious and expected, white wine aging surprises many guests — the transformation from crisp and primary to layered and complex is dramatic and worth pointing out.
Hiedler releases both current and back vintages when they feel the older wine still shows well — the 2017 being on the list in 2026 is a statement about its aging quality.

name: Ried Kefaerberg 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: A second premier cru Grüner from Hiedler — the Kefaerberg is a warmer, rounder site than the Kittmannsberg. Where the Kittmannsberg is lean and precise, this has more body and generosity. For guests who want Hiedler's quality but prefer something a little broader.
story: Hiedler farms two classified Erste Lage sites — the Kittmannsberg and the Kefaerberg — and they produce noticeably different wines despite being only a few kilometers apart. The Kefaerberg faces more toward the south and sits on deeper soils, which produces a Grüner with more weight and ripeness than the Kittmannsberg's precision. Comparing the two is an excellent way to show guests how dramatically aspect and soil type shape wine within the same small appellation.
trivia: Two premier cru sites from the same producer is unusual in Kamptal — most estates focus their top efforts on a single flagship vineyard.
The name Kefaerberg's exact etymology is uncertain — like many old Austrian vineyard names, its roots predate modern record-keeping, though it appears in documents going back several centuries.
Hiedler produces both the Kittmannsberg and Kefaerberg as premier cru expressions — tasting them side by side shows how dramatically different two classified sites from the same producer can be.

name: Terrasen
tableside: The most approachable and affordable Grüner on the list — Jurtschitsc's Terrasen is a light, fresh, everyday Kamptal white. White pepper, green apple, clean acidity. For guests who want a glass of something crisp and Austrian without committing to a premier cru.
story: Jurtschitsc is a Langenlois estate with a long history and a clear focus on making Kamptal wine accessible. The Terrasen is their entry-level expression, designed to deliver the essential character of Kamptal Grüner at a price that makes it an easy by-the-glass recommendation. Light, fresh, and unapologetically simple. Not every bottle needs to be a premier cru.
trivia: The name Terrasen — 'terraces' in German — refers to the terraced vineyard structure common throughout Kamptal's hillside sites.
Entry-level Kamptal Grüner is often blended from multiple sites across the appellation, giving the winemaker more flexibility to balance freshness and weight.
At 12.5% alcohol, this is one of the lightest wines in the Grüner section — a reflection of earlier harvesting to preserve freshness rather than chasing ripeness.

name: Ried Stein 1ÖTW
tableside: Bernhard Ott is the reference producer for Wagram Grüner — a region north of Vienna that most people overlook in favor of the more famous Kamptal and Wachau. The Ried Stein is his top site: a full-bodied, textured Grüner with serious mineral depth. A different animal from the Kamptal wines, but every bit as serious.
story: Bernhard Ott has spent decades making the case that Wagram deserves the same attention as Kamptal and Wachau, and the Ried Stein is his strongest argument. The Wagram plateau sits north of Vienna on deep loess soils that produce Grüner with more body and richness than the rockier Kamptal sites — rounder, more textured, less angular. Ott farms sustainably with very low yields, which concentrates the fruit and gives his top wines a weight and length that rivals anything in the appellation.
trivia: The Stein vineyard is classified 1ÖTW — Austria's premier cru — making it one of the very few Wagram sites to achieve this recognition. Most 1ÖTW vineyards are in Kamptal, Kremstal, or Wachau.
Wagram's deep loess soils can reach 20 meters in depth in some areas — significantly deeper than in Kamptal, providing excellent water retention and consistent vine nutrition even in dry years.
Bernhard Ott is arguably the most important ambassador for the Wagram appellation internationally — his wines have appeared on high-profile restaurant lists worldwide.

name: Alte Reben DAC
tableside: Nigl's Alte Reben — 'old vines' — is one of the most concentrated Grüners on the list. Certified biodynamic, from vines old enough to produce fruit with real depth. The 2023 is fresh and energetic but built for the long term.
story: Old vines produce less fruit than younger plantings — the vine's energy goes into fewer grapes, concentrating flavor and building the kind of complexity that young vines can't deliver. Nigl's Alte Reben draws on some of the oldest Grüner vines in Kremstal, farmed biodynamically to maximize the purity of what the site expresses. The result is one of the most concentrated Grüners on the list at any price.
trivia: Old vines — generally considered to be over 40 years old — produce smaller yields but more concentrated fruit. Some of Nigl's oldest Grüner plantings date back to the 1960s and 70s.
Certified biodynamic (Co/b) means Nigl holds Demeter certification — the most rigorous biodynamic standard, requiring a complete audit of farming practices every year.
Alte Reben is technically a Kremstal DAC wine rather than a single-vineyard expression — it blends fruit from multiple old-vine parcels across the estate.

name: 'Freiheit'
tableside: Freiheit — 'freedom' — is Nigl's most approachable and playful Grüner. Light, fresh, made for immediate drinking. Biodynamic farming at an accessible price. A great introduction to Nigl for guests not ready to commit to the Alte Reben.
story: Martin Nigl makes wines at every level of the quality and price spectrum, and the Freiheit sits at the accessible end — not because it's an afterthought, but because Nigl believes biodynamic farming and honest winemaking shouldn't be reserved for expensive bottles. The name 'Freiheit' reflects a philosophy: this is wine made freely, without the pressure of premier cru expectations. Light, fresh, and honest.
trivia: The word 'Freiheit' means 'freedom' in German — a nod to the wine's deliberately uncomplicated, approachable character.
Even at this price point, Nigl applies the same biodynamic farming principles as their top Privat wines — Demeter certification covers the entire estate, not just select vineyards.
At 12.5% alcohol, the Freiheit is one of the lightest Grüners on the list — achieved by picking earlier to preserve freshness.

name: Privat, Ried Pellingen
tableside: A decade-old biodynamic Grüner from Nigl's top site. The Privat designation means this is the estate's finest expression — made only in exceptional years. The 2016 is showing everything that makes aged Grüner so compelling: complexity, depth, and acidity that still holds everything together.
story: The Pellingen is Nigl's grand cru — a steep, rocky site in Kremstal that produces his most concentrated and age-worthy wines. The Privat designation goes further: it means this specific barrel selection was the best of the best from the best vintage. At a decade of age, the 2016 is in a beautiful place — the primary white pepper and citrus have given way to something deeper, more contemplative, and more complex.
