trivia: The Privat designation at Nigl has no legal definition — it's entirely the producer's own judgment about which wines merit the designation in a given vintage.
2016 was a balanced, precise vintage in Kremstal — cooler than recent years, with high natural acidity that has helped the wine age gracefully over the past decade.
The Pellingen vineyard name appears in Austrian wine records dating back several centuries — one of the most historically documented sites in Kremstal.

name: Ried Mühlpoint Federspiel
tableside: Federspiel is the Wachau's lightest classification — made for early drinking and food. Alzinger's Mühlpoint is one of the finest Federspiel wines in the region: elegant, mineral, and honest. For guests who want Wachau character without the weight of a Smaragd.
story: The Wachau's three-tier classification — Steinfeder (lightest), Federspiel (medium), and Smaragd (richest) — was created to help guests navigate the region's range of styles. The Federspiel, named after the lure used in falconry, is designed for immediate drinking and food pairing. Alzinger's Mühlpoint Federspiel shows that the lighter classification doesn't mean lesser quality — it means different intent: freshness over power, immediacy over aging potential.
trivia: The Federspiel classification is named after the falconer's lure used in traditional Austrian falconry — a fitting symbol for wines made to accompany a meal rather than dominate it. Maximum alcohol is typically under 12.5%, keeping the wines light and food-focused.

The Federspiel sits at the lighter end of the Wachau classification system — below Steinfeder (the lightest) and Smaragd (the most powerful). The three-tier system is unique to the Wachau and has no equivalent in any other Austrian appellation.
Alzinger farms the Mühlpoint on gentler slopes above the Danube flood plain — less steep than their top sites, which allows for slightly earlier harvesting and the characteristic lightness.

name: Ried Steinertal
tableside: Alzinger's Steinertal Grüner — a different expression of the same volcanic site we know from their Riesling. Powerful, textured, and built for the long term. The volcanic mineral character shows through the Grüner just as dramatically as in the Riesling. Worth noting: the 2023 commands a slightly higher price than the 2022 — demand for the warmer, more immediately generous vintage drives this.
story: The Steinertal site produces both Riesling and Grüner Veltliner for Alzinger, and comparing the two varieties from the same vineyard is one of the most instructive tasting exercises on this list. The Riesling emphasizes the site's acidity and mineral precision; the Grüner brings more body and spice, but the same underlying volcanic stone character runs through both. Two varieties, one voice.
trivia: Grüner Veltliner and Riesling are the two permitted varieties for Wachau DAC wines — all other varieties made in the region carry the broader Niederösterreich designation.
The volcanic rock in the Steinertal stores heat during the day and releases it slowly at night — this extended warmth allows both varieties to reach Smaragd ripeness despite the site's elevation.
Alzinger produces similar quantities of Grüner and Riesling from the Steinertal — neither variety is prioritized over the other, which is unusual for a region more associated with Riesling.

name: Ried Steinertal Smaragd
tableside: The 2023 Steinertal Smaragd — same volcanic site as the G2 2022, one year later and from a warmer vintage. More immediately generous, more open, with the volcanic mineral still very much present. For guests who want Wachau Smaragd power without waiting.
story: Two vintages of the Alzinger Steinertal Grüner side by side — the 2022 and 2023 — shows what vintage variation does at the Smaragd level. The 2022 is slightly cooler and more mineral-driven; the 2023 is warmer and more generous. Both are excellent. The choice between them is really a question of what kind of evening you want: a contemplative bottle that rewards attention, or one that makes everyone happy immediately.
trivia: 2023 was one of the warmest vintages on record in the Wachau, but the river's cooling influence and the region's granite soils kept the wines fresh despite the heat.
Smaragd wines from warm vintages like 2023 are often more immediately approachable than those from cooler years — the fruit is riper and more generous right out of the gate.
The Wachau's unique position — a narrow granite gorge carved by the Danube — creates a microclimate significantly cooler than the surrounding lowlands.

name: Federspiel
tableside: Domäne Wachau is the largest single landowner in the Wachau — a cooperative farming over 450 hectares across the region's most famous sites. Their Federspiel is the region's most accessible entry point: honest, fresh, and well-made at a price that makes it an easy recommendation.
story: Domäne Wachau was formed as a cooperative in the 1930s when small growers realized that working together was the only way to survive economically in the difficult post-war years. Today it's the largest single wine estate in Austria, farming over 450 hectares across the Wachau's most celebrated slopes. Their Federspiel is the cooperative's everyday expression — reliable, honest Wachau Grüner at a price no small estate could match.
trivia: Domäne Wachau farms over 450 hectares — by comparison, most individual Wachau estates farm between 5 and 20 hectares. Their scale gives them unmatched access to fruit from across the entire region.
The cooperative was founded in 1938 and has been instrumental in maintaining the Wachau's reputation during periods when individual estates struggled economically.

Federspiel wines from Domäne Wachau are intentionally made for early drinking — but at five years of age, this one has developed a roundness and harmony that makes it genuinely interesting. A useful conversation about how even 'simple' Wachau Grüner can evolve.

name: Ried Im Weingebirge Smaragd
tableside: Nikolaihof's Grüner from the Im Weingebirge — the same historic site we know from their legendary Riesling Vinotek wines, but in Grüner form. Biodynamic farming on the Wachau's most storied estate. The 2019 is in a beautiful place right now.
story: Nikolaihof makes both Riesling and Grüner from the Im Weingebirge, and the Grüner gets far less attention than the celebrated Rieslings — which makes it one of the best-value bottles from this estate. The same biodynamic farming, the same ancient cellar, the same no-compromise philosophy, just applied to Grüner rather than Riesling. The 2019, at six years, is showing the early stages of the complexity that Nikolaihof wines develop with bottle age.
trivia: Nikolaihof is Austria's oldest winery — built on Roman foundations with documented winemaking dating to 470 AD. The family has farmed the Im Weingebirge vineyard without interruption since that period.
The Im Weingebirge designation is one of the oldest named vineyard sites in Austria, appearing in historical documents from the early medieval period.

Nikolaihof began practicing biodynamics in 1971 and became Demeter certified in 1998 — making them one of the longest-established biodynamic wine estates in the world and Austria's first Demeter-certified winery.

name: Ried Hochrein Smaragd
tableside: Rudi Pichler is one of the Wachau's most respected producers — a perfectionist making wines with exceptional precision and aging potential. The Hochrein is his most accessible Smaragd: full Wachau power and mineral backbone, but a little earlier-drinking than the Kollmutz or Achleiten.
story: Rudi Pichler runs a small estate in Wösendorf with an almost obsessive focus on quality — low yields, late harvesting, and extended aging before release. His wines consistently appear on lists of the finest Wachau producers, often mentioned alongside F.X. Pichler despite no family relation. The Hochrein is his lightest-touch Smaragd: still powerful by most standards, but more approachable than his more famous top-site bottlings.
trivia: Despite sharing the surname, Rudi Pichler and F.X. Pichler are not related — both built their reputations entirely independently.
The Hochrein vineyard sits at the highest elevation of Rudi Pichler's main sites — the altitude contributes to the wine's fresh acidity and mineral precision.
Rudi Pichler is a member of Vinea Wachau and one of the signatories of the Codex Wachau — the voluntary set of strict production standards governing the region's best producers.

name: Ried Kollmutz Smaragd
tableside: A second Rudi Pichler Smaragd — the Kollmutz is a warmer, more south-facing site than the Hochrein and produces a richer, spicier Grüner. Same meticulous winemaking, more generous fruit. For guests who want Pichler's precision with a little more warmth.
story: Comparing the Hochrein and Kollmutz side by side is one of the best site-comparison exercises on the list. Both are Rudi Pichler Smaragd 2022 — same producer, same vintage, same grape — but the Kollmutz faces more toward the south and sits on slightly deeper soils, which produces noticeably more body and spice. The Hochrein is about precision; the Kollmutz is about generosity.
trivia: The Kollmutz faces roughly south-southwest — one of the most sun-exposed orientations in the Wachau — which is why it consistently produces riper, richer wines.
Rudi Pichler uses large-format neutral casks for aging his Smaragd wines — adding subtle texture without introducing oak flavor.
Having two Smaragd wines from the same producer and vintage at the same price is unusual — most producers price warmer-site wines higher because demand tends to be greater.

name: Ried Achleiten Smaragd
tableside: The Achleiten is one of the Wachau's most celebrated grand cru sites — a steep, complex vineyard with multiple soil types producing wines of extraordinary complexity. Rudi Pichler's Achleiten Smaragd is one of the finest Grüners on the list.
story: The Achleiten is a legendary Wachau site — a vast, amphitheater-shaped vineyard above Weissenkirchen that has been recognized as exceptional for centuries. Its complexity comes from multiple aspects and soil types within a single classified site: some parts are granite, some are gneiss, some face east and some face south. Multiple producers farm the Achleiten, and comparing their wines is one of the great exercises in Wachau terroir. Rudi Pichler's version is one of the most powerful and complex expressions available.
trivia: The Achleiten is one of the most studied vineyards in Austria — its geological complexity has been the subject of multiple academic papers and soil surveys.
Multiple prestigious producers farm the Achleiten — Franz Hirtzberger, Prager, and Rudi Pichler among the most celebrated. Each produces a noticeably different wine from the same site.
The Achleiten's amphitheater shape creates a natural heat trap — warm air collects in the bowl during the day, allowing grapes to reach full Smaragd ripeness even on the cooler northern portions.

name: Weingarten
tableside: Donabaum's single-vineyard Weingarten — a step up from the entry level, with more concentration and site definition. The 2020 has had six years to settle and is showing the structured, mineral character that Spitz Grüner is known for.
story: The Weingarten is Donabaum's primary single-vineyard expression — a site in Spitz on the granitic soils typical of this part of the Wachau. The 2020 vintage was warm in Austria but the cooler Spitz microclimate kept the acidity intact, producing a wine with real structure and the kind of mineral backbone that ages gracefully.
trivia: The name Weingarten simply means 'vineyard' in German — a common naming convention for sites that were historically just referred to as 'the vineyard' without a more specific designation.
Spitz's granitic soils produce Grüner with a particularly linear, mineral character — noticeably different from the rounder loess-grown wines of Wagram.
Donabaum ages his single-vineyard wines in large neutral casks for several months before bottling — adding subtle texture without oak flavor.

name: Weinviertel DAC
tableside: Ingrid Groiss makes biodynamic Grüner in the Weinviertel — Austria's largest wine region and the one most associated with everyday, peppery, food-friendly expressions of the variety. This is Grüner at its most honest: no premier cru pretension, no extended aging, just clean, precise, peppery white wine from certified biodynamic farming.
story: The Weinviertel DAC was created specifically to protect and promote the peppery, fresh style of Grüner that the region produces — wines that must be dry, varietal, and show that characteristic white pepper note. Ingrid Groiss farms biodynamically in the northern Weinviertel, producing wines that reflect the region's slightly cooler temperatures and the mix of loess and sand soils. At this price from a biodynamic producer, it's exceptional value.
trivia: The Weinviertel DAC was Austria's first DAC designation, created in 2002 specifically around Grüner Veltliner — a bold declaration that the region's identity is defined by a single grape.

Weinviertel — 'wine quarter' — is named for the fact that the region has been defined by viticulture for centuries. It's the largest wine region in Austria by area, accounting for roughly a third of the country's total vineyard land.
Ingrid Groiss is one of a growing number of Weinviertel producers who have shifted to biodynamic farming — meaningful in a region where volume production has historically dominated.

name: '8000' Reserve
tableside: A decade-old reserve Grüner from Setzer — the '8000' name refers to the number of vines per hectare, well above the regional average. High density forces competition between vines, lowering yields and concentrating flavor. The 2015 is showing exactly what serious Weinviertel Grüner does with age.
story: The '8000' designation refers to something specific: 8,000 vines per hectare, compared to the regional norm of around 4,000 to 5,000. High density planting forces the vines to compete for resources, producing smaller grapes with more concentrated flavors and naturally lower yields. It's a farming philosophy that prioritizes quality over volume — rare in the Weinviertel, where high-volume production has historically been the norm. At a decade, this reserve wine has developed the complexity and integration that most Weinviertel Grüner never reaches.
trivia: High-density planting — 8,000 or more vines per hectare — is common in regions like Burgundy and Champagne but unusual in most of Austria. Setzer adopted it specifically to improve quality.

The 2015 vintage was warm in the Weinviertel, producing wines with more body and ripeness than typical. Combined with low yields from high-density planting, the result is a wine of unusual concentration for the region.
Certified Sustainable (CS) at Setzer means third-party verified compliance with standards covering water use, energy, biodiversity, and social responsibility.

name: Grand Reserve
tableside: Anton Bauer's Grand Reserve — the top wine from a producer whose passing has made his bottles increasingly rare. Full-bodied, richly textured, built from Wagram's distinctive deep loess soils. This is Grüner at its most generous and weighty.
story: Anton Bauer built the Wagram appellation's international reputation almost single-handedly, and his Grand Reserve was always the wine that made the clearest case for the region. Wagram's deep loess soils produce Grüner with more body and richness than the rockier Kamptal or Wachau sites — rounder, more generous, built for the table. Bauer's recent passing means these wines are now finite — what's in circulation is what exists. Worth mentioning to guests who appreciate provenance and rarity.
trivia: Anton Bauer passed away in January 2026 at the age of 54 — a significant loss for the Wagram region. His wines are no longer in production under his direction, making every remaining bottle a finite resource.
Wagram's loess soils are among the deepest in Austria — in some areas reaching 20 meters below the surface, providing excellent water retention and consistent vine nutrition even in dry years.
Bauer was instrumental in establishing Wagram as a serious appellation — his powerful reds and structured whites were among the first Wagram wines to achieve international recognition.

name: 'Der Ott'
tableside: 'Der Ott' — literally 'The Ott' — is Bernhard Ott's flagship wine and the one most people think of when they think of Wagram Grüner. Rich, round, textured, and unmistakably Wagram. A wine that makes a confident statement about what this region can do.
story: If the Ried Stein is Ott's most serious single-vineyard expression, Der Ott is his manifesto — a wine blended from multiple sites across the estate designed to express Wagram character in its most complete form. Rounder and more textured than Kamptal, richer than most Wachau, Der Ott occupies a distinctive space in the Austrian Grüner landscape: generous, food-friendly, and immediately recognizable.
trivia: Der Ott is blended from multiple Wagram parcels — giving Ott the flexibility to balance freshness and richness across different sites and exposures.
The 2023 vintage was warm in Wagram, which suits Der Ott's naturally generous style — the riper fruit adds to the characteristic roundness rather than tipping it toward heaviness.
Bernhard Ott is regularly cited in international wine media as the best argument for taking Wagram seriously as a fine wine region.

name: 'Fass 4'
tableside: 'Fass 4' — 'Barrel 4' — is Ott's entry-level Wagram Grüner: fresh, light, and made for immediate drinking. The most approachable wine in his range and a great introduction to the Wagram style before moving up to Der Ott or the Ried Stein.
story: Ott named this wine after the specific barrel it was aged in during its first years of production — a nod to the transparency and honesty that characterizes his approach. The Fass 4 is made from younger vines and lighter parcels, designed to be fresh and accessible rather than weighty and complex. The right answer when someone wants a glass of good Austrian Grüner without committing to a premium bottle.
trivia: The name Fass 4 is an unusual choice for a wine label — most producers use vineyard names or estate names rather than barrel numbers. Ott's choice reflects his desire to be transparent about what the wine is and where it comes from.
At 12.5% alcohol, the Fass 4 is noticeably lighter than Der Ott or the Ried Stein — a deliberate choice to make an approachable everyday wine rather than a powerful statement.
The 2024 vintage is the freshest wine in the Wagram section — released recently, with all the primary freshness of a young Grüner intact.

name: Heiligenstein 1ÖTW 'Lyra' Reserve
tableside: A decade-old Riesling from the Heiligenstein — Austria's most celebrated Riesling site, grown on volcanic basalt. The 2015 has developed the deep, honeyed complexity that marks great aged Riesling — beeswax, dried wildflowers, ripe stone fruit, and a finish that won't quit.
story: The Heiligenstein is a volcanic basalt anomaly in Kamptal — a dark, heat-retaining rock formation surrounded by the region's typical primary rock and loess. The dark soils absorb heat during the day and release it slowly at night, extending the ripening season in a way no other site in the region can replicate. Bründlmayer's 'Lyra' designation refers to the unusual vine training system used — a wide, lyre-shaped canopy that increases sun exposure while reducing yields. This 2015 is now in its prime: the aged complexity has arrived — beeswax, dried wildflowers, concentrated stone fruit — and the volcanic mineral quality runs through every sip.
trivia: The complex, waxy, dried-wildflower character that develops in aged Riesling comes from a compound called TDN (trimethyl-dihydronaphthalene). Wine professionals sometimes call it 'petrol' — a term worth knowing but not worth using at a table. Tell guests it's the signature of age in great Riesling.

The Lyra training system was developed in Bordeaux in the 1980s — Bründlmayer adopted it for the Heiligenstein to control the naturally vigorous vine growth that the volcanic soil encourages — more canopy means more sun on the fruit, lower yields, and more concentration.
The Heiligenstein has been a recognized quality site since the 18th century — one of the few Austrian vineyards with a documented historical reputation predating the modern classification system.

name: Heiligenstein 1ÖTW 'Lyra' Reserve
tableside: The younger vintage of Bründlmayer's Heiligenstein flagship — same volcanic site, but 2021 is wound tighter and needs time. For guests who want to taste where great Austrian Riesling is going, not where it's been.
story: This is the same wine as the 2015 — same vineyard, same producer, same meticulous farming — but caught much earlier in its life. The 2021 vintage was cool and precise in Kamptal, producing Rieslings with high acidity and a taut, almost severe mineral quality that needs years to integrate. If the 2015 shows you where the wine ends up, the 2021 is a snapshot of the journey — tight, mineral, still becoming what it will be.
trivia: 2021 was one of the coolest growing seasons in Kamptal in recent memory — the wines are built for the long term, not for immediate pleasure.
Bründlmayer ages their reserve Rieslings in large neutral oak casks — a traditional approach that adds subtle texture without oak flavors.
The Heiligenstein's volcanic basalt is geologically unrelated to the surrounding rock — believed to be the remnant of ancient volcanic activity millions of years old.

name: Ried Zobinger Heiligenstein 1ÖTW DAC
tableside: Hirsch is the most biodynamically serious producer on the Heiligenstein — and it shows. There's a depth and precision here that reflects not just the volcanic terroir but a winemaker who has spent decades understanding exactly what this site wants to say.
story: Johannes Hirsch converted his estate to biodynamic farming before it was fashionable and has been refining his approach for decades. His Heiligenstein is widely considered one of the benchmark expressions of the site: not the most immediately generous, but the most site-true. The dark, smoky intensity of the volcanic basalt comes through clearly here — iron, stone, concentrated fruit. Not immediately generous, but extraordinary with the right dish.
trivia: Johannes Hirsch converted to biodynamic farming in the early 2000s and has since become one of Austria's most vocal advocates for the practice.
The Zobinger Heiligenstein designation specifies the Heiligenstein parcels within the village of Zöbing — further geographic precision within an already specific site.
Hirsch's wines are consistently cited in international blind tastings as among the most distinctive expressions of Austrian Riesling.

name: Heiligenstein 1ÖTW, Zöbing
tableside: A decade-old Gobelsburg Heiligenstein in a beautiful drinking window right now: the honeyed, waxy complexity of age is arriving, the fruit is still vivid, and the mineral structure from the volcanic soils frames the whole thing. For guests who want to experience aged Austrian Riesling without waiting.
story: Zöbing is the village at the foot of the Heiligenstein, and when you see it on the label it tells you you're getting Gobelsburg's Heiligenstein wine specifically — as distinct from their other Kamptal Rieslings. Schloss Gobelsburg holds multiple vintages of this wine simultaneously, and the 2015 is the one that's ready right now. Their interpretation tends toward more richness and generosity than Hirsch's more austere style — same volcanic rock, but centuries of estate tradition bring a rounder, more integrated character.
trivia: Certified Sustainable (CS) means the estate has met third-party verified standards for water use, biodiversity, and energy management.
2015 was a warm vintage in Kamptal — the wines have aged more quickly than cool vintages and are drinking at their peak now.
Schloss Gobelsburg's sustainability certification was awarded by the Austrian wine association.

name: Heiligenstein 1ÖTW
tableside: The 2019 Gobelsburg Heiligenstein — less evolved than the 2015, more energetic, the volcanic mineral front and center. A wine that's just starting to open up. For guests who want to taste potential.
story: 2019 was a warm vintage in Kamptal, but the Heiligenstein's volcanic soils and elevation kept the acidity alive. The Gobelsburg Heiligenstein at this stage shows the volcanic mineral quality most clearly — before the beeswax and dried-flower complexity of age softens it. It rewards undivided attention.
trivia: The 2019 vintage was one of the warmest on record — but the Heiligenstein's volcanic soils and elevation moderated temperatures where flat sites couldn't.
Schloss Gobelsburg holds back multiple vintages of Heiligenstein simultaneously — giving the ability to show guests the wine's evolution across a decade.
Gobelsburg's approach to Riesling is deliberately lower-intervention than their Grüner Veltliner program.

name: Heiligenstein 1ÖTW
tableside: The youngest of three Gobelsburg Heiligensteins on the list — 2021, tight and precise. The volcanic mineral is already evident but the wine is still gathering itself. Three vintages of the same wine on one list: the 2021 is all tension, the 2019 is opening up, the 2015 is at its peak.
story: Having three vintages of the same Gobelsburg Heiligenstein on the list is a live demonstration of what bottle age does to Riesling. Walking a curious guest through all three is one of the most instructive tasting experiences available.
trivia: Three vintages of the same wine in one list is unusual — most restaurants turn over inventory too quickly to offer this kind of vertical.
Vertical tastings — same wine, multiple vintages — are considered one of the most educational formats in fine wine.
The cool 2021 vintage is expected to age more slowly than the warm 2019 — they may be at similar development in 10 years despite the two-year gap.

name: Trocken, Senftenberger Pellingen Privat
tableside: A decade-old biodynamic Riesling from Nigl — one of Kremstal's most celebrated estates. The beeswax and dried wildflower complexity is in full development, the stone fruit is concentrated, and the acidity is still very much alive. One of the great aged Austrian Rieslings on the list.
story: Martin Nigl is widely considered one of Austria's finest Riesling producers — his biodynamic estate in Kremstal produces wines of extraordinary precision. The Senftenberger Pellingen is his top Riesling site: a steep, rocky slope where yields are naturally low and concentration is high. At a decade, this is showing everything that makes serious Austrian Riesling so compelling.
trivia: Nigl holds Demeter certification — the same certification used for the most rigorous biodynamic wines worldwide.
The Senftenberger Pellingen is classified in Nigl's range as 'Privat' — his designation for his top single-vineyard expressions.
Kremstal, which borders Kamptal to the east along the Krems River, produces Rieslings generally considered slightly riper and more accessible than Kamptal expressions.

name: Trocken, Hochäcker
tableside: A more accessible aged Nigl Riesling — the Hochäcker is a step below the Pellingen in concentration but still a decade-old biodynamic Kremstal from one of Austria's best producers. A great entry point for guests curious about Nigl without committing to the top tier.
story: Nigl makes Rieslings at multiple quality levels — the Hochäcker shares the same biodynamic farming and the same winemaker as the Privat Pellingen, but the site itself is less steep, less rocky, and slightly more generous in character. Where the Pellingen is austere and tightly wound, the Hochäcker has a little more give to it. At a decade of age they're both fully developed, but this one is more immediately approachable. The right recommendation when a guest wants aged Nigl without the full commitment of the top tier.
trivia: The name Hochäcker translates roughly as 'high fields' — referring to the elevated position above the Krems Valley floor.
Biodynamic farming prohibits all synthetic inputs and adds homeopathic preparations developed by philosopher Rudolf Steiner in the 1920s.
Nigl's wines consistently appear in international best-of-Austrian-Riesling lists.

name: Trocken, Ried Höhereck Smaragd
tableside: Smaragd — the Wachau's top ripeness classification. Alzinger is one of the Wachau's most celebrated estates. Big, structured, age-worthy Austrian Riesling from a region that treats the grape with the same seriousness as Burgundy treats Pinot Noir.
story: The Wachau is Austria's most dramatic wine landscape — steep granite terraces carved by the Danube gorge, where vines have been planted since the Romans. Alzinger is a small family estate that quietly produces some of the region's finest wines. The Smaragd classification — named after the emerald-green lizard that sunbathes on the terrace walls — indicates the ripest, most age-worthy style.
trivia: The Smaragd classification is named after the Emerald Lizard (Lacerta viridis) that lives on the warm stone terrace walls of Wachau vineyards.
The Wachau's terraced vineyards are among the steepest in the world — some slopes exceed 45 degrees, making all work manual.
Alzinger consistently wins top ratings from international critics while maintaining a deliberately low profile.

name: Trocken, Ried Loibenberg Smaragd
tableside: A second Alzinger Smaragd — the Loibenberg faces south, is slightly warmer, and produces Rieslings with more richness and spice than the Höhereck. For guests who want to understand how different aspects within the same village produce different wines.
story: The Loibenberg faces directly south — unusual in Wachau where most premium sites face east or northeast. This gives it a level of ripeness that neighboring vineyards can't match. Comparing the Höhereck and Loibenberg is a masterclass in how aspect shapes wine character even within the same appellation.
trivia: Loiben has been continuously inhabited for over 7,000 years — one of the oldest settlements in the Wachau.
South-facing slopes in the Wachau receive significantly more solar radiation than north-facing slopes.
Alzinger farms the Loibenberg biodynamically.

name: Trocken, Ried Steinertal Smaragd
tableside: Alzinger's third Smaragd — the Steinertal, their most mineral and site-expressive vineyard. The 2023 is young, showing the freshness of a new Smaragd. This needs time, but the foundation is extraordinary.
story: The Steinertal — 'Stone Valley' — is a steep, narrow parcel tucked into the hillside above Unterloiben. Where the Höhereck and Loibenberg sit on primary granite, the Steinertal has a higher proportion of volcanic and metamorphic rock, which gives the wine a smokier, flintier mineral quality — less round than the Loibenberg, more angular than the Höhereck. It's the most site-specific and least immediately approachable of the three Alzinger Smaragds on the list, but it has a distinctiveness that the other two don't quite match. Three Alzinger Smaragds side by side is a rare opportunity to taste how dramatically aspect and soil type shape wine even within the same small estate.
trivia: The Steinertal is the steepest and most difficult to farm of Alzinger's main sites.
2023 was a warm, early vintage in the Wachau — the wines have more immediate richness than the cooler 2022.
Alzinger releases Smaragd wines only after extended cellar aging.

name: Trocken, Ried Klostersatz
tableside: F.X. Pichler is the most famous name in the Wachau — their wines have set the benchmark for the region for decades. The Klostersatz is their most accessible Smaragd: full Wachau power and precision, a little earlier-drinking. Biodynamic farming adds purity.
story: Lucas Pichler took over from his legendary father F.X. in 2012 and has maintained the estate's position at the very top of Austrian wine. The Klostersatz sits on primary rock below the monastery walls of Dürnstein — 'Klostersatz' means 'monastery seat.' The biodynamic certification, achieved recently, has added a further dimension of purity.
trivia: F.X. Pichler — Franz Xaver Pichler — is widely considered the greatest Austrian winemaker of his generation, though the estate is now run by his son Lucas.

The monastery in Klostersatz is the Augustinian Abbey of Dürnstein, founded in 1410 — its striking blue and white Baroque tower has been one of the Wachau's most iconic landmarks since the 18th century.

Lucas Pichler made the decision in 2020 to leave the Vinea Wachau association and adopt the Wachau DAC system instead — a statement about climate change and the evolving meaning of ripeness in the region.

name: Trocken, Jungfernlese Steiner Hund
tableside: A 20-year-old biodynamic Riesling from the Wachau's oldest estate. This is a wine that has had two decades to become something extraordinary: deep beeswax, dried wildflowers, concentrated stone fruit, and a mineral backbone that is still perfectly alive. For guests who want to understand what great Riesling becomes with age, this is the bottle.
story: Nikolaihof is Austria's oldest winery — built on Roman foundations in the first century AD — and the first estate in the world to receive Demeter biodynamic certification, in 1971. The Saahs family farms with an almost philosophical commitment to the land: no chemicals of any kind for over 50 years, and wines released only when the family believes they're ready. The 2005 Steiner Hund at 20 years is still alive, complex, and nowhere near finished.
trivia: Nikolaihof was built on the site of a Roman military outpost — archaeological excavations have found wine vessels dating to the first century AD.
The estate has practiced biodynamics since 1971 and received Demeter certification in 1998, making them Austria's first Demeter-certified winery — a landmark in the history of biodynamic wine.
The Steiner Hund ('Stone Dog') vineyard is classified as grand cru equivalent by the Vinea Wachau.

name: Trocken, Klause am Berg Privat
tableside: Thirteen years old and still unfolding — Nikolaihof's Privat designation means this is the estate's top expression, made only in exceptional years. The 2012 vintage's warmth adds a glycerous richness that makes this unforgettable. One of the most profound wines on the list.
story: Nikolaihof releases 'Privat' wines only when they believe a specific vintage and site combination merits it. 2012 was a warm, exceptional vintage in the Wachau. The Saahs family maintains a strict no-compromise policy: wines are only released when they're ready, regardless of market pressure. At 13 years, this is one of the most complex wines on the list.
trivia: The Privat designation carries enormous weight among Austrian wine enthusiasts — it has no legal definition but is self-imposed with absolute seriousness.
The Saahs family has resisted every acquisition offer — they consider the estate an ongoing philosophical project.
2012 in the Wachau produced some of the most celebrated wines of the decade.

name: Trocken, Steiner Hund
tableside: The Steiner Hund at ten years — the Nikolaihof mineral signature is fully present and the beeswax and aged complexity is just arriving. A wine at the beginning of something great. Compare it to the 2005 if you want to understand what awaits.
story: Having the Steiner Hund in both 2005 and 2015 on the list is an extraordinary opportunity — ten years apart, same site, same biodynamic farming, completely different phases of development. The 2005 shows the destination; the 2015 shows the journey.
trivia: The Steiner Hund has been farmed for well over a thousand years — its history is documented in monastic records, making it one of the oldest continuously farmed vineyard sites in Austria.
Nikolaihof prunes by hand using tools and techniques that have changed very little over centuries.
The Wachau's Vinea Wachau association, which administers the Smaragd classification system, was established in 1983.

name: Trocken 'Vinotek' Im Weingebirge Smaragd
tableside: A 28-year-old biodynamic Riesling. Very few wines remain alive and compelling at this age — this is one of them. The Vinotek designation means it was released from Nikolaihof's own library. This is a once-in-a-career pour for most guests.
story: The Vinotek series is Nikolaihof's library release program — wines held back for extended aging, released only when the family believes they've reached a peak of expressiveness. The 1997 Im Weingebirge at 28 years is showing what biodynamic winemaking in the Wachau can achieve: deeply complex, honeyed, mineral, and still technically alive in a way that defies expectation.
trivia: 1997 was a warm, early vintage — initially considered good but not exceptional. 28 years of bottle aging has proved that assessment spectacularly wrong.
The Im Weingebirge ('In the wine mountains') is a historic Wachau designation that predates the modern classification system.
Nikolaihof's cellar maintains a constant 12°C year-round — naturally, without refrigeration.

name: Trocken 'Vinotek' Im Weingebirge Smaragd
tableside: Twenty-three years old and the most expensive bottle in the Riesling section. The 2002 Vinotek is Nikolaihof at their most profound. If a guest asks what to pair it with, the honest answer is: their full attention.
story: 2002 is considered one of the greatest Riesling vintages of the modern era in both Austria and Germany. The Nikolaihof 2002, held in their Vinotek library for over two decades, has become something that requires no food pairing, no context, and no explanation. It is one of the most profound wines we offer.
trivia: 2002 is widely considered alongside 1999, 2007, and 2015 as one of the four great Riesling vintages of the last quarter century.
The Vinotek aging program depends entirely on the natural cellar temperature — no technology, no manipulation, just stone walls and patience.
At $420, this is the most expensive white wine on the list. For a Riesling of this age and provenance, it is also arguably the best value.

name: Trocken, Ried Klaus Smaragd
tableside: Prager is one of the Wachau's foundational estates — making serious Smaragd before the classification existed. The Ried Klaus is their most approachable top vineyard: full Wachau power and mineral, but a little earlier-drinking. The 2017 is beautiful right now.
story: Toni and Ilse Bodenstein — Prager by inheritance — built one of the Wachau's great estates over decades of meticulous farming. The Ried Klaus is a granite-dominated site producing Rieslings with the combination of power, richness, and mineral backbone that defines the best Wachau Smaragd.
trivia: Prager was one of the founding members of the Vinea Wachau association in 1983 — the organization that created the Smaragd classification system.
The Klaus vineyard name appears in monastic records from the 14th century.
Toni Bodenstein is widely credited with helping establish the Wachau's international reputation in the 1980s.

name: Feuersbrunn
tableside: The most approachable Riesling on the list — Anton Bauer's entry-level Wagram expression. Lighter, less complex than the Wachau or Kamptal bottles, but fresh, precise, and well-made at a price that makes it an easy recommendation.
story: Anton Bauer is a Wagram estate that consistently over-delivers at its price point. Wagram — the plateau region north of Vienna — is less celebrated than Kamptal or Wachau, but produces wines with genuine character from its distinctive loess and sand soils.
trivia: The village of Wagram gave its name to the Battle of Wagram in 1809, not the other way around — the region was well-established long before Napoleon arrived. After his victory there, the name became famous across Europe.
Loess soils in Wagram are found in some of the deepest deposits in Austria, up to 20 meters thick in some areas — the same wind-deposited silt that makes some Kamptal sites so distinctive.

Anton Bauer, who built this estate into one of the Wagram's most respected, passed away recently. His wines are now a finite resource — what remains in circulation is what exists. Worth mentioning to guests who appreciate the history.

name: Ried Gamlitzberg, Südsteiermark
tableside: Styrian Sauvignon Blanc — the Austrian version of this grape most people don't know exists. Where New Zealand is tropical and the Loire is flinty, Styrian Sauvignon is herbaceous, precise, and intensely mineral. This Gamlitzberg is the entry point: approachable, aromatic, built for food.
story: Südsteiermark — South Styria — is Austria's southernmost wine region, tucked against the Slovenian border in a landscape of rolling hills. The Sauvignon Blanc grown here is dramatically different from both the Loire and New Zealand styles: more herbal than tropical, more mineral than fruity. Strauss is a reliable, quality-focused producer in the Gamlitz area, the heart of Styrian Sauvignon.
trivia: Südsteiermark is geographically closer to Zagreb than to Vienna — a border region with both Austrian and Slovenian cultural heritage.
The rolling hills of Styria are covered almost entirely in vines — one of the most visually dramatic wine regions in Central Europe.
Sauvignon Blanc was brought to Styria in the 19th century by Archduke Johann of Austria.

name: '18,' Südsteiermark DAC
tableside: The most affordable Sauvignon Blanc on the list — a joint project between two Styrian producers, made for immediate pleasure. The right answer when someone wants a glass of something clean, bright, and not expensive.
story: '18' is a collaborative cuvée between producers Strauss and Tscherne, both based in the Gamlitz area. The name refers to the collaboration number. The 2024 is the freshest wine in the Sauvignon section: primary, aromatic, built for drinking now.
trivia: Collaborative cuvées between independent producers are relatively uncommon in Austria — this one reflects the close-knit culture of the Südsteiermark wine community.

Südsteiermark DAC covers a wide range of varieties including Sauvignon Blanc, Welschriesling, Pinot Blanc, Morillon (Chardonnay), Pinot Gris, Riesling, Muskateller and Traminer — Sauvignon Blanc leads, but it is far from the only option.
The 2024 vintage in Styria was warm and abundant — wines with generous fruit and early accessibility.

name: Ried Zieregg, Südsteiermark
tableside: The Zieregg is Tement's grand cru — and arguably Austria's most celebrated Sauvignon Blanc vineyard. The 2018 is now at six years and showing the development that makes this one of the most serious expressions of the grape anywhere in the world. Nothing like New Zealand. Nothing like the Loire. Entirely its own thing.
story: Manfred Tement is one of Austria's most celebrated winemakers, and the Zieregg is his crown jewel — a single south-facing slope above Gamlitz producing Sauvignon Blanc of extraordinary intensity and mineral depth. At 14% alcohol and six years of age, this is a completely different wine from what most people think of when they think Sauvignon Blanc.
trivia: The Zieregg is consistently ranked among the top five Sauvignon Blanc vineyards in the world.
Tement converted the Zieregg to biodynamic farming in the early 2000s.
The Zieregg faces directly south — unusual in Styria where most premium sites have east or southeast exposure.

name: Ried Zieregg, Südsteiermark
tableside: The 2020 Zieregg — two years younger than the 2018, slightly more wound up, built on a cooler vintage's higher acidity. If the 2018 is Zieregg at its most generous, this is Zieregg at its most precise.
story: The 2020 vintage in Styria was cooler and more precise than 2018, giving the Zieregg a different character: tighter, more mineral-driven, built for longer aging. Comparing the 2018 and 2020 Zieregg side by side is one of the most instructive tastings on the list.
trivia: 2020 was significantly cooler than 2018 in Styria — the wines have a tighter, more nervous energy.
Tement holds back significant quantities of each Zieregg vintage for library release.
The Zieregg appeared in Jancis Robinson's list of the world's 100 greatest vineyards.

name: Ried Grassnitzberg, Südsteiermark
tableside: The Grassnitzberg is Tement's second great single-vineyard Sauvignon — less concentrated than the Zieregg, more elegant, earlier-drinking. The 2018 is in a beautiful place right now: mature, open, showing the Tement house style at its most accessible.
story: The Grassnitzberg faces southeast rather than south — a subtle difference in exposure that produces a lighter, more aromatic style. Same biodynamic farming as the Zieregg; what differs is purely the site. The right recommendation when a guest wants the Tement quality but isn't ready for the Zieregg's weight.
trivia: The Grassnitzberg has been farmed by the Tement family for three generations.
Southeast exposure gives longer morning sun and less intense afternoon heat compared to true south-facing sites.
Tement also produces wines from the Slovenian side of the border — one of the few genuinely cross-border wine estates in Europe.

name: Ried Grassnitzberg, Südsteiermark
tableside: The 2019 Grassnitzberg — a year younger than the 2018, slightly more energetic, showing the same Tement precision with a bit more grip. The most affordable Tement single-vineyard wine on the list.
story: Having both the 2018 and 2019 Grassnitzberg on the list gives the same comparative opportunity as the Zieregg — same site, different vintage character. The 2019 was warm and precise in Styria, producing Sauvignon Blancs with more body than the 2018 but excellent sustained acidity.
trivia: The 2019 vintage was one of the warmest in Styrian history — but the Grassnitzberg's southeast exposure and elevation helped retain freshness.
Tement exports primarily to Germany, Switzerland, and the United States — the estate has been instrumental in building the international reputation of Südsteiermark Sauvignon Blanc.

The Tement family has pursued biodynamic certification across their estate vineyards, including both the Zieregg and Grassnitzberg — a commitment that extends to their cross-border holdings in Slovenia.

name: 'Dissonance,' Napa Valley
tableside: California-style Sauvignon Blanc — barrel-fermented, rich, and tropical. This is Sauvignon Blanc for guests who usually drink Chardonnay. Smooth, generous, no sharp edges.
story: Blackbird Vineyards is a Napa estate better known for their red Bordeaux blends, but the Dissonance has built its own following among guests who want something white but richer than most Sauvignon Blanc delivers. Barrel fermentation adds the creamy, vanilla texture; Napa fruit brings the tropical richness.
trivia: Sauvignon Blanc barrel fermentation fundamentally changes the wine's character, adding weight and texture while suppressing herbal aromatics.
Blackbird Vineyards was founded in 2003 specifically to make Bordeaux-variety wines.
Napa Valley's warm climate produces Sauvignon Blanc with tropical fruit dominance.

name: Russian River Valley
tableside: A cooler-climate California Sauvignon Blanc from Russian River — more herbal and precise than Napa Sauvignon, closer to the Loire in spirit. A good middle ground for guests who want California freshness without the tropical richness of a warmer-climate bottle.
story: Russian River Valley is one of California's coolest growing regions — morning fog from the Pacific keeps temperatures low and ripening slow, producing wines with higher acidity and more herbal character. Cloisonné is a small-production project focused on this style of Sauvignon Blanc.
trivia: Russian River Valley's morning fog is caused by the Petaluma Wind Gap — a natural gap in the coastal mountains.
The region is far better known for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay than Sauvignon Blanc.
Russian River Valley received its own AVA designation in 1983.

name: Napa Valley
tableside: Peju is one of Napa's most guest-friendly estates — their Sauvignon Blanc is approachable, floral, and easy to recommend to anyone who wants something white, crisp, and California without being heavy.
story: Peju Province Winery was founded by Tony and Herta Peju in 1982 and remains family-owned — a rarity in Napa Valley. Their Sauvignon Blanc is made in a clean, un-oaked style that emphasizes the grape's natural freshness.
trivia: Peju Province is one of the few Napa Valley estates still fully family-owned after four decades.
The 'Province' in the name refers to Tony Peju's Provençal French heritage.
Napa Valley's Sauvignon Blanc is typically harvested in August — one of the earliest harvests in the valley calendar.

name: 'The 24,' Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre, Sonoma, 2021
tableside: Adobe Road's flagship red blend — Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvèdre from Sonoma County in the southern Rhône tradition. The 24 is named for the race car number of Kevin Buckler, Adobe Road's founder. Rich, spiced, and generously fruited. For guests who want something bold and Rhône-inspired without going to France.
story: Adobe Road is built around Kevin Buckler's dual passions — motorsport and wine. The 24 references his race car number and his approach to winemaking: precision, performance, and no compromise. The GSM blend draws on Grenache for fruit and body, Syrah for structure and spice, and Mourvèdre for depth and earthy complexity.
trivia: Kevin Buckler is a professional endurance racing driver who has competed at Le Mans multiple times — the same discipline and attention to detail that defines elite motorsport is applied to every aspect of Adobe Road's winemaking.

The GSM blend — Grenache, Syrah, Mourvèdre — is the backbone of southern Rhône wines like Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Gigondas. California producers have embraced the combination as a natural fit for the state's warm, Mediterranean-influenced climate.
Sonoma County's diversity of sub-appellations — from the cool Petaluma Gap to the warmer Dry Creek Valley — allows Adobe Road to source GSM components from the ideal climate for each variety.

name: Bordeaux Blanc
tableside: La Louvière is a classified Pessac-Léognan estate — their white is aged in oak, giving it a toasty, textured character closer to white Burgundy than anything New World. A wine for guests who take their whites seriously.
story: The blend at La Louvière is approximately 85% Sauvignon Blanc and 15% Sémillon — a ratio that emphasizes freshness while the Sémillon adds the characteristic waxy texture. The 2018 vintage in Bordeaux was warm and generous, producing whites with unusual richness and depth.
trivia: Château La Louvière is classified as a historical monument — the 18th century château itself is considered architecturally significant.
André Lurton is credited with reviving Pessac-Léognan's white wine reputation in the 1960s and 70s through a series of high-profile tastings.

The waxy texture you get from Sémillon is unlike anything else in wine — it's a lanolin-like quality, almost like the coating on the skin of a ripe pear, that comes from the grape's naturally high glycerol content. It coats the palate gently and lingers. Guests who notice it often describe it as richness without weight — and once they know to look for it, they tend to love it.

name: Pouilly-Fumé, Loire Valley
tableside: Classic Pouilly-Fumé from one of the Loire's most respected estates. The 'fumé' — smoky — in the name comes from the gunflint soils, and you can taste it: Sauvignon Blanc at its most mineral and precise.
story: Henri Bourgeois is a multi-generational Loire family that farms both in Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé — the two great Sauvignon Blanc appellations on opposite banks of the Loire River. Pouilly-Fumé tends toward more immediate accessibility and a more pronounced smoky, flinty mineral quality from the region's famous silex (flint) soils.
trivia: The 'Fumé' in Pouilly-Fumé refers to the smoke-like character of the wine, which comes from the silex (flint) soils rather than any winemaking technique.
Pouilly-Fumé and Sancerre face each other across the Loire River — only a few kilometers apart but with different soil compositions.
Henri Bourgeois also makes wines in Marlborough, New Zealand under the Clos Henri label.

name: Sancerre, Loire Valley
tableside: Sancerre — the world's most famous Sauvignon Blanc appellation. Lauverjat is a reliable, quality-focused producer, and the 2025 is the freshest vintage on the list: all primary citrus, chalk mineral, and the clean precision that made Sancerre famous.
story: Sancerre sits on the eastern Loire where the hills rise sharply from the floodplain. The soils here — primarily Kimmeridgian limestone and silex — produce Sauvignon Blanc with a precision and mineral clarity that no other region can replicate.
trivia: Sancerre has been made famous in part by its association with Crottin de Chavignol — the aged goat cheese from the village of Chavignol within the appellation. One of the great French regional food-and-wine pairings.
The 2025 vintage is the most recent on the entire list — these wines were harvested barely months before landing on the table.
Sancerre's reputation was built almost entirely in the second half of the 20th century.

name: 'Fosilni Berg,' Ciringa
tableside: Tement's Slovenian expression — their vineyards cross the border and this comes from the Slovenian side. Same biodynamic farming, same producer, completely different appellation. A remarkable value for a Tement wine and a great conversation piece.
story: Tement's vineyards don't stop at the Austrian border — the Ciringa appellation in Slovenia sits directly adjacent to their Südsteiermark parcels, and Manfred Tement farms both sides with the same biodynamic approach. The Fosilni Berg ('Fossil Hill') is named after the marine fossils in the limestone soils. At $48, it's the most affordable Tement wine on the list.
trivia: The Austrian-Slovenian wine border is one of the most unusual in the world — the same producer can hold certified organic vineyards in two countries.
Ciringa sits in the Brda region, home to some of Slovenia's most celebrated producers.
The fossilized marine sediments in Ciringa's soils date from when the area was covered by the ancient Tethys Sea.

name: 'Pruh,' Ciringa
tableside: Tement's top Slovenian bottling — the Pruh is to Ciringa what the Zieregg is to Südsteiermark. Six years of age, biodynamic farming, and the same meticulous approach that defines everything Tement makes.
story: The Pruh is Tement's premier cru expression from their Slovenian parcels — a single-vineyard wine from the best site on the Ciringa side. At six years, it's showing the same development that makes the Grassnitzberg and Zieregg so compelling.
trivia: 'Pruh' means 'stripe' in Slovenian — referring to the distinctive bands of limestone and marl in the vineyard's soil profile.
Manfred Tement is one of very few winemakers considered a reference point for the same grape variety in two different countries simultaneously.
The Brda region of Slovenia was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire until 1918, then Italian territory until 1947.

name: Alto Adige
tableside: Alto Adige Sauvignon Blanc — Italian in address, Austrian in soul. Elena Walch is one of the region's most celebrated producers: aromatic, precise, with the altitude-driven freshness that makes Alto Adige wines so distinctive.
story: Elena Walch is a remarkable story: a Vienna-born architect who married into one of Alto Adige's great wine families and transformed the estate into one of the region's leading producers. Alto Adige sits in the northern Italian Alps, where vineyards at 300 to 900 meters produce whites with an Alpine freshness that no warmer Italian region can match.
trivia: Alto Adige is Italy's most Germanic wine region — German is an official language and the wine culture reflects both Italian and Austrian influences.
Each 100 meters of altitude reduces average temperature by approximately 0.6°C — giving winemakers extraordinary flexibility.
The region was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire until 1919.

name: 'Old Stones,' Willamette Valley
tableside: Oregon Chardonnay in the Burgundy mold — cool, mineral, and restrained where California would add richness. Bergström is one of the Willamette Valley's most respected producers. For guests who find California Chardonnay too buttery, this is the answer.
story: Bergström farms biodynamically in the Willamette Valley and has built a reputation primarily for Pinot Noir, but their Chardonnay program is equally serious. Old Stones is a multi-vineyard blend designed to show what Oregon Chardonnay can do at its most elegant: restrained oak, bright acidity, and a mineral thread that comes from the volcanic Jory soils that define the best Willamette sites. At five years, the 2020 is showing more complexity than it did on release.
trivia: Bergström holds Demeter biodynamic certification — the same rigorous standard as Nikolaihof and Nigl in Austria.

The Jory soil that dominates the best Willamette Valley sites is a volcanic clay loam derived from ancient basalt flows — it drains well and warms quickly, extending the growing season in a naturally cool region.

Oregon Chardonnay has historically been overshadowed by Oregon Pinot Noir, but a growing number of producers are making the case that the Willamette Valley is one of America's finest Chardonnay regions.

name: Sonoma Coast
tableside: A Sonoma Coast Chardonnay from a producer committed to sustainable farming. The coast keeps the style leaner than Napa or Carneros — more citrus, less butter — but with the warmth and generosity that makes California Chardonnay so approachable. A solid crowd-pleaser at a reasonable price.
story: The Calling focuses on cool-climate California appellations — Sonoma Coast, Russian River, the Santa Rita Hills — where fog and wind from the Pacific push ripening slow and preserve acidity. Their Chardonnay program is made in a style that bridges the gap between the leaner, mineral end of the spectrum and the richer, more opulent California style most guests are familiar with. Practicing sustainable viticulture means no synthetic chemicals in the vineyard.
trivia: Sonoma Coast is one of California's largest and most variable AVAs — it covers everything from warm inland sites to genuinely cold coastal vineyards only a few miles from the Pacific.
The 2019 vintage in Sonoma was considered one of the finest in recent memory — a long, cool growing season produced wines with exceptional balance and aging potential.
Practicing sustainable viticulture (PR) at The Calling means third-party verified compliance with water conservation, soil health, and biodiversity standards.

name: Calistoga, Napa Valley
tableside: Château Montelena is the winery that won the 1976 Paris Tasting — blind against the best Burgundies, their Chardonnay came first. That result changed how the world thought about California wine. This is a full, rich, classically Californian style: buttery, oaky, generous. For guests who know exactly what they want.
story: The 1976 Paris Tasting — organized by British wine merchant Steven Spurrier — is the single most consequential event in the history of American wine. French judges, tasting blind, ranked Château Montelena's 1973 Chardonnay above every white Burgundy on the table. The result was so shocking that French wine authorities initially refused to believe it. The winery has been trading on that reputation ever since — justifiably, because they've never stopped making excellent wine.
trivia: The 1976 Paris Tasting result was initially suppressed — the French organizer was pressured not to publish the results. When they were eventually revealed, they made front-page news globally.
The event was dramatized in the 2008 film Bottle Shock, though the film takes considerable creative liberties with the actual story.

Calistoga, at the northern end of Napa Valley, is one of the warmest sub-appellations — the combination of heat and the estate's cooler hillside elevation gives Montelena's Chardonnay its characteristic richness balanced by surprising freshness.

name: Our Lady of Guadalupe Vineyard, Santa Barbara
tableside: Dave Phinney is best known for The Prisoner, the cult Napa red blend. His Santa Barbara Chardonnay is a different animal entirely — cool-climate, mineral, built more in the European style than the classic California mold. From a single vineyard in one of the state's coolest growing regions. Unusual and worth exploring.
story: Dave Phinney has built multiple cult wine brands over his career, but the Santa Barbara projects are where he does his most personal work. The Our Lady of Guadalupe Vineyard sits in a cool coastal pocket of Santa Barbara County where marine air keeps temperatures low and ripening slow — producing Chardonnay with a nervous, mineral energy that's closer to Chablis than Napa. At $195 it's the most expensive Chardonnay on the list after the top Burgundies, and it earns it.
trivia: Santa Barbara County's east-west mountain orientation is unique in California — it allows cold Pacific air to penetrate directly inland, creating growing conditions cooler than anything on the northern California coast at the same latitude.

Dave Phinney founded The Prisoner Wine Company in 2000 and sold it in 2010 for a reported $40 million. He has since built several other brands including Orin Swift and various vineyard-specific projects.
Single-vineyard Chardonnay at this level is still relatively rare in Santa Barbara — the region is better known for Pinot Noir, making Phinney's commitment to this site all the more noteworthy.

name: Napa Valley
tableside: Darioush is an Iranian-American producer who has built one of Napa's most distinctive estates — a Persian-inspired winery that makes no apologies for being bold and opulent. Their Chardonnay is exactly that: rich, tropical, oak-forward. For guests who want a statement white wine.
story: Darioush Khaledi fled Iran during the revolution and built his winery in Napa with an explicitly Persian aesthetic — the tasting room looks like Persepolis transported to the Silverado Trail. The wines match the architecture: full-bodied, generous, and luxurious. Their Chardonnay is aged in French oak for ten months, which gives it the toasty, vanilla-rich character that guests who love Napa whites tend to seek out.
trivia: Darioush Khaledi came to California with almost nothing after the Iranian revolution and built his wine empire through a chain of grocery stores before turning to winemaking full-time.
The winery building features 16 monolithic travertine columns inspired by the ancient Persian palace at Persepolis — it is one of the most architecturally distinctive wineries in Napa Valley.
Darioush was one of the first Napa producers to commit to estate farming across all his white wine vineyards — giving him control over every aspect of the fruit that ends up in the bottle.

name: Reserve, Russian River Valley
tableside: DuMOL is one of Sonoma County's most respected producers — their Chardonnay program is built around Russian River Valley fruit that delivers the richness California is known for but with the cool-climate precision that makes it genuinely interesting. The Reserve is their fullest expression of the variety.
story: DuMOL was founded by two Australian businessmen who fell in love with Russian River Valley fruit and set out to make the finest possible expressions of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir from the region. The Reserve Chardonnay is sourced from their best Russian River parcels and aged in a higher proportion of new French oak than their standard bottling — giving it more richness and complexity while the cool-climate fruit keeps it from tipping into excess.
trivia: DuMOL's name is a contraction of the founders' surnames — Duncan and Moloney — a naming convention borrowed from their background in Australian business.

Russian River Valley receives morning fog from the Pacific Ocean almost every day during the growing season — the fog burns off by midday but cools the vineyards enough at night to preserve the acidity that makes Chardonnay from here so lively.

The 2020 vintage in Russian River was technically challenging — a series of wildfires in Northern California created smoke exposure concerns, but Russian River's proximity to the coast meant the smoke was less concentrated there than further inland.

name: Finch Hollow, Napa Valley
tableside: Jarvis is one of Napa's most unusual estates — the entire winery is built underground, carved into the hillside, where the cave maintains a constant temperature year-round. The Finch Hollow Chardonnay is rich, floral, and generously built. For guests who want something genuinely opulent.
story: William Jarvis spent 25 years and considerable personal fortune blasting a 45,000 square-foot underground winery into the Vaca Mountains above the Silverado Trail. The result is one of Napa's most extraordinary production facilities — temperature-stable at 58°F year-round, which means no refrigeration is needed for any stage of production. The wines reflect the estate's commitment to extravagance: rich, textured, and built to impress.
trivia: The Jarvis winery cave is entirely self-sufficient — it maintains a constant 58°F without mechanical refrigeration, which Jarvis believes produces more stable fermentations and aging conditions.
Jarvis's underground stream — which runs through the cave — is used for irrigation and temperature management, making it one of the most sustainably cooled wineries in Napa.
Finch Hollow refers to a specific stream-fed hollow on the estate property, where the vines benefit from additional moisture and cooler air drainage at night.

name: Napa Valley
tableside: Jayson is the second label of Pahlmeyer — one of Napa's most celebrated estates. Same fruit sources, same winemaking approach, lower price. A rich, generous Napa Chardonnay that over-delivers for what it costs. The right answer when a guest wants something impressive without the flagship price.
story: Pahlmeyer built his reputation on full-throttle Napa reds, but the whites — made under the direction of noted winemaker Erin Green — are equally serious. The Jayson is designed as the accessible entry into the Pahlmeyer style: same meticulous sourcing from Napa hillside and benchland vineyards, but priced to encourage discovery rather than commitment. At $98 it's one of the best-value full-bodied Napa Chardonnays available.
trivia: Pahlmeyer founder Jayson Pahlmeyer was a Berkeley lawyer who fell in love with wine on a trip to Bordeaux and decided to make Napa wine his second career — the second label is named after him.

Erin Green, who makes the Pahlmeyer whites, is one of Napa's most respected white wine specialists — she trained in Burgundy and brings a more restrained, terroir-focused approach than the old-school Napa style.

At the flagship Pahlmeyer level, the same Chardonnay retails for significantly more — the Jayson represents genuine value for guests who want to explore the producer's philosophy without the full investment.

name: Carneros
tableside: Carneros Chardonnay from Joseph Carr — a producer who focuses on vineyard-specific wines from some of California's best appellations. Carneros sits at the southern end of Napa and Sonoma, where San Pablo Bay cooling produces wines with more freshness than the warmer inland sites. Rich but not heavy.
story: Carneros is bisected by the Napa-Sonoma county line, making it one of California's few AVAs that sits in two different counties simultaneously. The proximity to San Pablo Bay creates a natural air conditioning effect — cold marine air funnels up the bay every afternoon, dropping temperatures dramatically and preserving the acidity that prevents Chardonnay here from becoming flat. Joseph Carr sources from estate vineyards in the heart of the appellation.
trivia: Carneros was the first California sub-appellation defined by climate rather than county lines — its boundaries were drawn around the area affected by San Pablo Bay cooling rather than following any political border.
The name Carneros means 'rams' in Spanish — the rolling hills were historically used for sheep grazing before vineyards took over in the late 19th century.
Carneros produces both Chardonnay and Pinot Noir primarily — the same combination that defines Burgundy and Champagne, which is not a coincidence given the similar cool-climate conditions.

name: 'Sonoma Mountain,' Sonoma Coast
tableside: Kistler is the name California Chardonnay lovers point to when they want to justify the category. Their wines are benchmarks — rich, Burgundian in ambition, built on single-vineyard fruit from Sonoma's finest sites. The Sonoma Mountain expression shows the estate at its most generous.
story: Steve Kistler started making Chardonnay and Pinot Noir in Sonoma in 1978, at a time when single-vineyard California white wine was almost unheard of. He was inspired by white Burgundy and set out to prove that California could produce wines of equivalent complexity and longevity. The result is a range of single-vineyard Chardonnays that have been cited on best-of lists for decades. The Sonoma Mountain expression is one of his richest and most opulent — a full-throttle California Chardonnay that never loses its identity.
trivia: Steve Kistler was one of the first California winemakers to deliberately make single-vineyard, Burgundy-inspired Chardonnay — a practice that is now standard among quality producers but was radical in the late 1970s.

Kistler ferments in small French oak barrels and ages on the lees for up to eighteen months — techniques borrowed directly from Burgundy that add the hazelnut and brioche complexity that defines the house style.

The Sonoma Mountain AVA sits above the fog line on the eastern slope of Sonoma Mountain — warmer than the Sonoma Coast but cooler than the Valley floor, which gives Kistler's wines from this site their particular richness.

name: Howell Mountain Estate, Napa Valley
tableside: La Jota farms on Howell Mountain — one of Napa's highest and most rugged appellations. Mountain Chardonnay is different from valley floor: leaner, more structured, with a mineral edge that makes it genuinely age-worthy. The 2018 has had eight years and is showing real complexity.
story: Howell Mountain sits above the fog line on the eastern slopes of the Mayacamas Mountains at elevations up to 2,200 feet. Most of Howell Mountain's fame comes from its Cabernet Sauvignon, but La Jota has been making a compelling case for the appellation's Chardonnay since the 1980s. The volcanic soils, high elevation, and significant diurnal temperature swings produce a Chardonnay that is simultaneously powerful and precise — a combination that's rare in California.
trivia: Howell Mountain was one of the first sub-appellations within Napa Valley to be approved — receiving its designation in December 1983 alongside Los Carneros, ahead of Stags Leap, Oakville, and Rutherford.

Volcanic soils on Howell Mountain — derived from ancient ash deposits — drain extremely well and are poor in nutrients, which naturally limits vine vigor and concentrates flavor in the resulting fruit.
La Jota was founded in 1898 and is one of the oldest continuously operating estates on Howell Mountain — though there was a gap during Prohibition.

name: Sta. Lucia Highlands, Monterey
tableside: The most affordable Chardonnay on the list and a genuine over-achiever. The Sta. Lucia Highlands is one of California's coldest growing regions — the Monterey gap funnels wind off the Pacific so aggressively that candles won't stay lit in the vineyards during harvest. Bright, fresh, and well-made.
story: The Sta. Lucia Highlands appellation runs along the western edge of the Santa Lucia mountain range, where afternoon winds from the Pacific cool the vineyards to temperatures that California's warmer regions never see. The result is Chardonnay with natural acidity that requires no winemaking tricks to stay fresh. Luli makes approachable wines from this exceptional appellation at prices that make them genuinely accessible — a rare combination.
trivia: The Monterey gap is a natural break in the Coast Ranges that acts as a wind tunnel, channeling cold Pacific air directly into the Salinas Valley and the Sta. Lucia Highlands every afternoon during the growing season.
Wind speeds in the Sta. Lucia Highlands during harvest can exceed 40mph — growers joke that you need to hold your hat on during picking season.
Despite its exceptional growing conditions, Sta. Lucia Highlands remains relatively unknown compared to Sonoma or Napa — which is part of why quality wines from the region are often underpriced.

name: 'Lady Marjorie,' Sonoma Coast
tableside: RAEN is a small, serious producer on the Sonoma Coast making Chardonnay and Pinot Noir that consistently ranks among the finest in California. Lady Marjorie is their single-vineyard Chardonnay: cool, mineral, and restrained in a way that sets it apart from nearly everything else in the California section.
story: RAEN is a project built around extreme cool-climate farming on the true Sonoma Coast — not the warmer inland sites that technically qualify for the appellation, but the cold, windy parcels that sit within a few miles of the Pacific. The winemaking philosophy is Burgundian: minimal intervention, indigenous yeasts, and a commitment to letting the site speak rather than the winemaker. Lady Marjorie comes from a single vineyard that consistently produces one of the most mineral and precise California Chardonnays available.
trivia: RAEN's founders, brothers Carl and Jesse Klotz, grew up in a winemaking family in Napa but deliberately chose the challenging Sonoma Coast because they wanted to make site-specific wine rather than appellation blends.

The true Sonoma Coast — as opposed to the broader AVA — sits so close to the ocean that fog is present for most of the morning every day of the growing season, keeping temperatures dramatically lower than just a few miles inland.

RAEN stands for Research in Agriculture and Enology Naturally — but the spelling also nods to rain, the maritime moisture that defines their coastal vineyards. The name captures both the scientific rigor and the elemental conditions that shape every bottle.

name: Carneros
tableside: One of California's most recognized Chardonnay labels — full malolactic fermentation, generous oak, tropical and stone fruit in abundance. Full malolactic fermentation, generous oak, tropical fruit. If a guest says 'I'll have a Chardonnay' and doesn't specify further, this is the safe read. Rich, immediately satisfying, and consistent.
story: Rombauer became a phenomenon not by accident but by design: they deliberately make wine for guests who want immediate pleasure over contemplation, using full malolactic fermentation to convert all acidity to the softer lactic acid and aging in a high proportion of new French and American oak. The result is the definition of a crowd-pleasing California Chardonnay — butter, vanilla, tropical fruit, and a finish that disappears gently. It is not a subtle wine. It is not trying to be. And it works.
trivia: Malolactic fermentation — the conversion of sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid — is what gives California Chardonnay its characteristic buttery texture. Rombauer does full malolactic on every barrel.
Rombauer Vineyards was founded in 1982 by Koerner Rombauer, a relative of Irma Rombauer who wrote The Joy of Cooking — the winery frequently acknowledges this connection in their marketing.
The Joy of Cooking connection is apt: like the cookbook, Rombauer Chardonnay is about accessible pleasure and repeatability rather than intellectual challenge.

name: 'Special Selection,' Carneros
tableside: Rombauer's top Chardonnay — everything the standard Carneros is, but more of it. More concentration, more oak influence, more time in barrel, more everything. For guests who love the Rombauer style and want to step up.
story: The Special Selection is made from Rombauer's best Carneros vineyard blocks — lower yielding, older vines, and longer time in new French oak. The result is the Rombauer style taken to its logical conclusion: a full-throttle California Chardonnay of extraordinary richness and intensity. It's polarizing by design — guests who love it really love it.
trivia: The Special Selection is produced in significantly smaller quantities than the standard Carneros — it's a genuine step up in sourcing and production rather than just a marketing label.

Carneros's proximity to San Pablo Bay means the vineyards here receive significantly more cooling maritime influence than most of Napa and Sonoma — which is why even the richest Rombauer Special Selection retains some freshness.
The use of American oak in some barrels — alongside French — adds a distinctive vanilla and coconut note that is considered a hallmark of the Rombauer style.

name: Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: Sea Smoke is best known for Pinot Noir but their Chardonnay is equally serious — cool-climate, precise, with a mineral backbone that makes it one of the finest California Chardonnays on the list. The Sta. Rita Hills brings restraint to what could be an over-ripe style.
story: The Sta. Rita Hills AVA sits at the western end of the Santa Ynez Valley, where the valley opens toward the Pacific and cold marine air pours in almost continuously. Sea Smoke farms estate vineyards in this cold pocket and produces Chardonnay with a mineral precision that's more reminiscent of Chablis than Napa. The same volcanic and diatomaceous earth soils that give their Pinot Noir such distinctive character inform the Chardonnay with an unusual stony quality.
trivia: The Sta. Rita Hills was officially renamed from Santa Rita Hills to avoid confusion with a Chilean winery — making it one of the few American appellations to change its name after a legal dispute.

Diatomaceous earth — fossilized remains of ancient microscopic algae — is found in significant quantities in Sta. Rita Hills soils. The chalky, porous nature of this soil drains well and contributes to the wines' distinctive minerality.
Sea Smoke's Chardonnay program is much smaller than their Pinot Noir production — making their white wines genuinely difficult to find outside of restaurant allocations.

name: Le Finage, Burgundy
tableside: Chablis at its most honest — La Chablisienne is the region's most respected cooperative, and Le Finage is their village-level expression. Completely unoaked, completely dry, and entirely about the Kimmeridgian chalk soils that make Chablis the purest expression of Chardonnay on earth. For guests who ask for Chardonnay but actually want something clean and mineral.
story: La Chablisienne is a cooperative of over 300 growers who collectively farm nearly a third of all Chablis vineyards. Their scale gives them access to fruit from across the appellation's best sites, and their winemaking philosophy is deliberately minimal: no oak, no malolactic fermentation on most wines, and a fierce commitment to expressing the Kimmeridgian limestone terroir that defines Chablis. Le Finage is the gateway: village-level Chablis that shows the style clearly and accessibly.
trivia: Kimmeridgian limestone — the soil that defines the best Chablis vineyards — is also found in the English Channel, in parts of Burgundy, and in the Aube subregion of Champagne. It is named after Kimmeridge Bay in Dorset, England.

Chablis is legally permitted to be labeled Burgundy despite being 100 miles northwest of the Côte d'Or — it is technically the northernmost part of the Burgundy wine region, though it feels like an entirely different world.
