Unoaked Chardonnay was considered old-fashioned in many markets during the 1980s and 90s when oak-aged California Chardonnay dominated. Chablis refused to change and has since been fully vindicated by shifting consumer preferences toward freshness.

name: 'Plaisance,' Pouilly-Fuissé
tableside: Pouilly-Fuissé is the southern Burgundy appellation that most people have heard of but rarely order — which makes it one of the best value plays in the Chardonnay section. Floral, approachable, with enough body to feel serious. A great step up from entry-level Burgundy.
story: Pouilly-Fuissé sits in the Mâconnais, the southernmost section of Burgundy, where the climate is warmer and the wines are generally richer and more accessible than the cooler Côte de Beaune. Thierry Drouin is a quality-focused family producer whose Plaisance — meaning 'pleasure' — lives up to its name: a genuinely enjoyable village-level white Burgundy at a price that makes it an easy recommendation.
trivia: Pouilly-Fuissé received its own premier cru classification in 2020 — the most recent major change to the Burgundy classification system. The appellation had been arguing for the designation for decades.

The dramatic rock formation of Solutré and Vergisson dominates the Pouilly-Fuissé landscape — they served as hunting grounds for prehistoric humans, and archaeological finds around the base suggest the area has been continuously inhabited for 40,000 years.

Mâconnais Chardonnay is often considered the best value white Burgundy — the soils are limestone-based like the Côte de Beaune, but the lower land prices mean comparable quality at significantly lower cost.

name: Chablis Grand Cru, Valmur
tableside: Grand Cru Chablis — the top tier of the appellation, from one of only seven classified grand cru vineyards. The Valmur is considered one of the most elegant and age-worthy of the seven. The 2019 has had six years and is showing the complexity that makes grand cru Chablis one of the most intellectually rewarding white wines in the world.
story: There are only seven grand cru vineyards in Chablis, all on the same southwest-facing slope above the town. The Valmur is considered by many to be the most elegant and complex of the seven — less immediately powerful than Vaudésir or Les Clos, more refined and mineral-driven. Domaine Louis Moreau is a generational family estate with holdings across multiple Chablis appellations; their grand cru wines are consistently among the finest in the region.
trivia: All seven Chablis grand cru vineyards are on a single continuous slope above the town — they face southwest toward the Serein River, which moderates temperature and increases sun exposure.
The seven grand crus — Blanchot, Bougros, Les Clos, Grenouilles, Les Preuses, Valmur, and Vaudésir — collectively cover only about 100 hectares, making grand cru Chablis genuinely rare.
Despite its status, grand cru Chablis is often overlooked in favor of grand cru Côte de Beaune — which means it's frequently better value for comparable quality.

name: Chablis Grand Cru, Les Clos
tableside: The second Louis Moreau grand cru on the list — Les Clos is the most powerful and age-worthy of all seven grand cru vineyards. Where Valmur is elegant, Les Clos is monumental. Same producer, same vintage, completely different personalities from adjacent vineyards. A remarkable comparison for the right guest.
story: Les Clos is Chablis's largest and most celebrated grand cru — the name means 'the enclosed plots,' referring to the medieval walls that once separated the vineyards. It produces the most powerful and structured of the grand crus, with a mineral intensity and aging potential that rivals the finest white Burgundies from the Côte de Beaune. Having both the Valmur and Les Clos from the same producer and vintage on the list is an extraordinary comparative opportunity.
trivia: Les Clos is the largest of the seven grand cru vineyards at approximately 26 hectares — about twice the size of Valmur.
The wine from Les Clos typically needs more time than other grand crus — its power and intensity in youth can be almost austere, with the complexity emerging only after five or more years of aging.

Tasting Valmur and Les Clos side by side from the same producer and vintage is a classic Chablis education: same appellation, same soil type, same winemaker, but two completely different characters from adjacent hillsides.

name: Puligny-Montrachet, Côte de Beaune
tableside: Puligny-Montrachet is the most celebrated white wine village in the world. Louis Latour is one of Burgundy's great négociant houses. This is village-level Puligny — the entry point to one of the most revered appellations in wine, and one of the most important white wines on the list.
story: Puligny-Montrachet sits at the heart of the Côte de Beaune and is home to two of the world's most legendary white wines: Le Montrachet and Chevalier-Montrachet, both grand crus of extraordinary rarity and price. Village-level Puligny — wine that comes from the commune but not from a classified premier or grand cru vineyard — gives guests a genuine taste of this terroir at a fraction of the grand cru price. Louis Latour has been making Burgundy since 1797 and their Puligny is a reliable, elegant expression of what the village does.
trivia: Le Montrachet — the grand cru at the heart of Puligny — is widely considered the greatest dry white wine in the world. A single bottle can fetch thousands of dollars at auction.
Louis Latour was founded in 1797 and is one of the few Burgundy négociants still family-owned after more than two centuries of continuous operation.

Puligny-Montrachet added 'Montrachet' to its name in 1879 — the same year that Chassagne added it to theirs — a practice that became common across Burgundy as villages sought to associate themselves with their most famous vineyards.

name: Savigny-lès-Beaune Blanc, Burgundy
tableside: Savigny-lès-Beaune is better known for red than white Burgundy — which makes Nicolas Potel's blanc a genuine discovery for guests who want village Burgundy quality without the Puligny or Meursault price. Elegant, mineral, and under the radar.
story: Nicolas Potel is a Burgundy négociant and domaine owner who has built a reputation for quality across the appellation. Savigny-lès-Beaune is a village on the northern edge of the Côte de Beaune where red wine dominates the vineyards — the few whites made there come from a handful of well-sited parcels and are rarely seen outside specialist wine lists. This is exactly the kind of discovery bottle that rewards a curious guest.
trivia: Savigny-lès-Beaune produces about 85% red wine and only 15% white — making the blanc a genuine rarity within the appellation.

The village is bisected by the Rhoin stream, which divides the vineyards into two distinct soil types — the northern side tends toward rounder, richer wines; the southern side toward leaner, more mineral expressions.

Nicolas Potel — who passed away in 2025 — built his négociant career after leaving Domaine de la Pousse d'Or following his father's death in 1997. He later founded Domaine de Bellene and Maison Roche de Bellene, whose wines are considered among the finest in the Burgundy négociant category.

name: Chablis, Burgundy
tableside: Village-level Chablis from Simonnet-Febvre — one of the region's oldest houses. The most approachable and affordable white Burgundy on the list. Clean, dry, and entirely mineral. For guests who want a proper Chablis without the grand cru commitment.
story: Simonnet-Febvre was founded in 1840 and is one of the oldest wine houses in Chablis. Their village-level wine is made in the classic unoaked Chablis style — stainless steel fermentation, no malolactic conversion, and a focus entirely on the chalk and limestone terroir of the appellation. It's the right answer when a guest asks for something light, dry, and French.
trivia: Simonnet-Febvre was founded in 1840, making it one of the oldest wine houses in Chablis — it was producing wine before the phylloxera crisis wiped out European viticulture in the 1870s and 80s.
Village Chablis — the appellation's entry level — accounts for the majority of the region's production. Premier cru and grand cru together represent only about 30% of total output.

The term 'unoaked Chardonnay' has become a marketing concept in many New World regions, but in Chablis it has always been the traditional approach — the wine's character comes entirely from the soil, not the cellar.

name: Ried Steinberg Reserve, Kamptal
tableside: Bründlmayer also makes Chardonnay — from the same Kamptal soils that produce their celebrated Grüner and Riesling. The Steinberg Reserve shows what happens when Austrian precision meets Burgundian variety: mineral, structured, and genuinely distinctive from anything in the California section.
story: Chardonnay in Austria is a relative newcomer — most estates built their reputations on indigenous varieties. Bründlmayer's decision to grow Chardonnay on their Ried Steinberg site reflects a desire to see how Kamptal's soils and climate interact with the world's most planted white variety. The result is not a Burgundy imitation but something genuinely Austrian: the same mineral precision and acidity that defines their Grüner, applied to a different grape.
trivia: Chardonnay accounts for less than 5% of Austrian wine production — it is dwarfed by Grüner Veltliner and Riesling in the regions where it appears.
The Ried Steinberg site gets its name from the German word for 'stone mountain' — the rocky limestone and primary rock soils of the site are reflected in the wine's mineral character.
Bründlmayer ages the Steinberg Reserve in large neutral oak casks — traditional Austrian practice that adds texture without obvious oak flavor.

name: Ried Lores, Tattendorf, Thermenregion
tableside: Familie Reinisch makes Chardonnay in the Thermenregion — a warm, lesser-known Austrian appellation south of Vienna. The Lores site produces a richer, more approachable style than the Kamptal expression: more fruit, more texture, less austerity. A good entry into Austrian Chardonnay for guests who find the mineral style challenging.
story: The Thermenregion sits south of Vienna between the hills of the Vienna Woods and the Pannonian Plain — a warmer region than Kamptal or the Wachau, where Chardonnay achieves fuller ripeness and more generous fruit character. Familie Reinisch is one of the region's leading estates, farming biodynamically and producing wines that reflect the warmer character of the appellation without losing freshness.
trivia: The Thermenregion is named after the thermal springs that run through the area — the same springs that feed the famous Roman baths and modern spa resorts in the region.
Familie Reinisch is the leading proponent of the Tattendorf sub-appellation within the Thermenregion — a small area they have worked to establish as a distinct identity within the broader region.
The Thermenregion is one of the few Austrian regions where indigenous varieties like Zierfandler and Rotgipfler are grown — varieties found almost nowhere else in the world.

name: Schützen Ried Sinner, Leithaberg
tableside: Prieler's Chardonnay from the Leithaberg — a cool, mineral-driven expression from a producer better known for Blaufränkisch. The Ried Sinner brings freshness and limestone character that makes it one of the most restrained and food-friendly Austrian Chardonnays on the list.
story: Prieler is primarily celebrated for their Blaufränkisch reds from Burgenland's Leithaberg DAC, but they also make a small amount of Chardonnay from their Schützen estate. The Leithaberg's limestone and schist soils produce a markedly different Chardonnay from the warmer loess-based Wagram or Thermenregion expressions — leaner, more mineral, with the kind of precision you associate with cool-climate viticulture.
trivia: The Leithaberg DAC designation is one of Austria's newest and most mineral-focused appellations — it covers both red (Blaufränkisch) and white (Chardonnay and Pinot Blanc) wines from a specific limestone and schist ridge above Lake Neusiedl.

Prieler's decision to make Chardonnay reflects the Leithaberg's unusual geology — the limestone soils are similar to parts of Burgundy, which the family believes makes Chardonnay a natural choice for the site.
The 2024 vintage is the youngest wine in the Austrian Chardonnay section — released recently and built for early drinking rather than long aging.

name: Morillon 'Muschelkalk,' Styria
tableside: Morillon is the Austrian name for Chardonnay — a local designation that reflects the variety's long history in Styria. Tement's Muschelkalk is named after the fossilized shell limestone soils where the vines grow. At seven years, it's showing real character: mineral, textured, and distinctly Austrian.
story: In Styria, Chardonnay has historically been called Morillon — a local name that predates the variety's international fame. Tement farms Morillon in their Muschelkalk — 'shell limestone' — vineyard, a site where ancient marine sediment creates a particularly mineral, chalky character in the wines. At $59 it's one of the best-value bottles in the Chardonnay section, and it shows a completely different face of the variety from anything in the California or French sections.
trivia: Morillon — the Styrian name for Chardonnay — is believed to derive from a medieval French term for the variety that was preserved in Styria long after it fell out of use elsewhere.

Muschelkalk is a specific type of limestone formed from the fossilized shells of ancient marine organisms — the same geological formation gives wines their characteristic chalky, mineral quality in parts of Germany and Alsace as well.
Tement is better known for Sauvignon Blanc, but their Morillon program shows how the same biodynamic farming philosophy and mineral-rich soils produce compelling results with Chardonnay.

name: Morillon, Ried Zieregg, Styria
tableside: Tement's grand cru Chardonnay — from the same Zieregg site as their celebrated Sauvignon Blanc. This is what happens when you apply the same biodynamic farming and meticulous winemaking to Chardonnay: complex, mineral, and genuinely extraordinary. One of the most unusual and impressive bottles on the list.
story: The Zieregg is Tement's most celebrated site and they grow both Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay there — a rare choice, since most producers commit their best sites to a single variety. The Chardonnay is aged in French oak and aged for longer than the Sauvignon Blanc, giving it a more complex, layered character. At six years, the 2018 is showing the depth that comes from world-class biodynamic farming on exceptional limestone soils. One of the most unusual and impressive bottles in the entire guide.
trivia: The fact that Tement grows both Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay on the Zieregg is unusual — most grand cru-equivalent sites in the world are committed to a single variety. Tement's decision reflects a belief that the soil quality matters more than the variety planted on it.

The Zieregg Chardonnay is aged in French oak — specifically in a higher proportion of new barrels than the Sauvignon Blanc — which adds the hazelnut and toasted complexity that distinguishes it from the pure mineral expression of the Sauvignon Blanc.

This wine appears on very few restaurant lists outside Austria — its combination of Tement's reputation and Chardonnay's relative obscurity in Styria makes it a genuine discovery for wine-focused guests.

name: Zierfandler, Ried Modler
tableside: Zierfandler is a white grape found almost exclusively in the Thermenregion south of Vienna — you will not find it anywhere else in the world. This is a 2012, fourteen years old and still alive. Gebeshuber is the benchmark producer. For the guest who wants something genuinely rare.
story: Zierfandler is one of Austria's most obscure indigenous white varieties — grown only in the Thermenregion, a warm region south of Vienna between the Vienna Woods and the Pannonian Plain. At its best it produces wines of extraordinary longevity and complexity, with a warm spicy character that has no parallel in any other variety. Gebeshuber is the producer most associated with serious Zierfandler, and the 2012 at fourteen years is a remarkable demonstration of what the variety can do with age.
trivia: Zierfandler is grown commercially only in the Thermenregion — it is one of the rarest wine grapes in active production anywhere in the world.
The variety was nearly extinct by the mid-20th century when volume producers abandoned it in favor of easier grapes. A small number of quality-focused estates like Gebeshuber kept it alive.
Despite the name, Zierfandler has no connection to Zinfandel — the similar-sounding names are a coincidence of etymology.

name: Rotgipfler 'Liam'
tableside: Rotgipfler is Zierfandler's equally obscure Thermenregion partner — two grapes found almost nowhere else on earth, grown side by side for centuries. At fourteen years this 'Liam' cuvée is showing exactly what rare Austrian whites do with time: warm, complex, and genuinely contemplative.
story: Rotgipfler and Zierfandler are often spoken of as a pair — two indigenous Thermenregion varieties that have coexisted in the same vineyards for centuries and are sometimes blended together. Rotgipfler tends toward more body and spice than Zierfandler, with a warmth that reflects the Pannonian influence on the region's climate. Gebeshuber's 'Liam' designation refers to a specific barrel selection named for a family member — their most serious Rotgipfler expression.
trivia: Rotgipfler's name means 'red tip' in German — a reference to the reddish tinge on the tips of the grape clusters at harvest.

Like Zierfandler, Rotgipfler was nearly abandoned in the 20th century when producers switched to more productive varieties. Its survival is largely due to a handful of committed Thermenregion estates.
Thermenregion wines were popular in Vienna's coffee houses and restaurants for centuries before the region's reputation declined — a revival is now underway driven by producers like Gebeshuber.

name: Weissburgunder, Burgenland
tableside: Weissburgunder is Pinot Blanc — a variety that Austria does particularly well. Heidi Schröck is one of Burgenland's most celebrated producers, better known for her natural wines but equally serious across her whole range. Fresh, floral, and quietly excellent.
story: Heidi Schröck farms biodynamically in Rust, on the western shore of Lake Neusiedl in Burgenland — a region better known for rich reds and Trockenbeerenauslese dessert wines than fresh whites. Her Weissburgunder is a counterpoint to the region's typical richness: clean, precise, and built for food rather than contemplation. Pinot Blanc in Austrian hands tends to have more body and texture than the Alsatian style, but without the oak that defines it in Burgundy.
trivia: Weissburgunder — Pinot Blanc — is believed to be a natural mutation of Pinot Gris, which is itself a mutation of Pinot Noir. The three varieties are genetically nearly identical.

Rust, where Heidi Schröck farms, is one of Austria's most historic wine towns — it has been a free royal city since 1681, when the town paid the Habsburg emperor 60,000 guilders and 30,000 liters of wine for the privilege of putting the letter R on its barrels.
Schröck is one of the few producers who has successfully built an international reputation for both natural wines and conventionally made wines under the same label.

name: Neuburger, Niederösterreich
tableside: Neuburger is another Austrian indigenous variety — a grape found almost exclusively along the Danube and in pockets of Niederösterreich. At eleven years and biodynamically farmed, this Donabaum is a serious and unusual bottle. Worth pulling out for guests who ask what makes Austrian wine different.
story: Neuburger is a crossing of Roter Veltliner and Silvaner that produces wines of unusual richness and texture for a cool-climate white variety. It's rarely seen outside Austria and virtually unknown to most wine drinkers internationally — which makes it one of the most compelling discovery bottles on the list. Donabaum farms biodynamically in Spitz in the Wachau and applies the same rigor to his Neuburger as to his Grüner and Riesling.
trivia: Neuburger is believed to have originated as a natural crossing in the Wachau — the same gorge where Donabaum farms today.
The variety is rich in glycerol, which gives it its characteristic round, almost oily texture — quite different from the crisp, mineral character of Grüner Veltliner from the same region.
Biodynamic certification (Co/b) from Donabaum is notable — Demeter-certified estates in the Wachau are still relatively rare despite the region's quality focus.

name: Gelber Muskateller 'Sand & Schiefer,' Styria
tableside: Gelber Muskateller is the Austrian name for Muscat Blanc — but where most Muscat is sweet, this is bone dry and intensely floral. Tement's version comes from sand and slate soils in Styria, which give it a stony mineral quality that keeps the aromatics in check. For guests who want something floral without sweetness.
story: Gelber Muskateller — Yellow Muscat — is one of Austria's most aromatic white varieties, producing wines that smell like a flower market but taste dry and precise. Tement's Sand & Schiefer ('sand and slate') cuvée is named after the specific soil types of the vineyard: the sandy soils contribute delicacy; the slate brings the mineral tension that prevents the wine from becoming cloying. At $59 it's one of the best-value aromatic whites on the list.
trivia: Muscat Blanc à Petits Grains — the variety behind Gelber Muskateller — is one of the oldest cultivated grape varieties in the world, believed to have been grown in ancient Greece and Rome.

The characteristic floral aroma of Muscat comes from a group of compounds called monoterpenes, particularly linalool and geraniol. These compounds are present in high concentrations in the grape skins.

Despite its intense aromatics, dry Muscat is one of the most food-friendly white wines — the floral character complements asparagus, artichokes, and light fish in ways that more neutral varieties can't.

name: Semillon/Chenin Blanc/Pedro Gimenez, Mendoza
tableside: An unusual blend from Argentina — Semillon, Chenin Blanc, and Pedro Gimenez, three varieties rarely seen together anywhere. Altos las Hormigas is better known for Malbec but their white program is genuinely interesting. Approachable, aromatic, and a great conversation starter.
story: Altos las Hormigas — 'the heights of the ants' — is one of Mendoza's most thoughtful wine estates, better known internationally for their Malbec but equally serious about expressing what high-altitude Argentina can do with white grapes. The blend of Semillon, Chenin Blanc, and Pedro Gimenez is unusual anywhere and nearly unheard of in South America — a deliberate experiment in combining old-vine Argentine varieties that are rarely celebrated on their own.
trivia: Pedro Gimenez — sometimes written Giménez — is an Argentine white variety that has no relation to Spain's Pedro Ximénez despite the similar name. It's believed to have developed as a distinct variety in Argentina over several centuries.
Altos las Hormigas was founded in part by Alberto Antonini, one of Italy's most respected flying winemakers, who brought a European sensibility to Argentine viticulture.

Mendoza's high altitude — most vineyards sit between 900 and 1,500 meters — means cool nights even in summer, which preserves the natural acidity that makes white wine viable in what is otherwise a warm, dry region.

name: Roussanne, Santa Barbara County
tableside: Roussanne is a Rhône variety that almost no one makes in California — which is exactly why Clementine Carter makes it. Rich, textured, floral, and genuinely distinctive. For guests who want something California but don't want Chardonnay.
story: Roussanne is one of the great white varieties of the northern Rhône — the grape behind white Hermitage and Crozes-Hermitage — but it's rarely grown in California because it's difficult to farm and unpredictable in warm climates. Clementine Carter sources from a specific cool-climate Santa Barbara vineyard where the marine influence keeps Roussanne's natural tendency toward flabbiness in check. The result is something genuinely unusual: a rich, floral, textured white that has no equivalent anywhere else on the list.
trivia: Roussanne is one of only two permitted varieties for white Hermitage in the northern Rhône — the other being Marsanne. Together they produce some of the most age-worthy white wines in the world.
The variety's name is believed to derive from the French word for 'rust' — rousse — a reference to the reddish-brown color the grape skins develop at full maturity.
Roussanne is notoriously prone to oxidation and difficult to work with in the cellar, which is part of why so few California producers attempt it.

name: Côtes du Rhône Blanc
tableside: A southern Rhône white — Grenache Blanc, Clairette, and Viognier — from a small family estate in the Rhône Valley. Light, floral, and genuinely food-friendly. The most affordable French white on the list and one of the most versatile.
story: Southern Rhône whites are one of wine's great undiscovered categories — made from a rotating cast of indigenous varieties including Grenache Blanc, Clairette, Bourboulenc, Roussanne, and Viognier, they produce wines of genuine character that most guests have never encountered. Domaine Berthet-Rayne is a small family estate in Cairanne, one of the Rhône's most reputable villages, farming with minimal intervention and making wines that reflect the garrigue-scented landscape of Provence.
trivia: The southern Rhône is permitted to use up to 18 different grape varieties in its white wines — one of the most complex blending palettes of any appellation in the world.
Garrigue — the aromatic scrubland of wild herbs, lavender, and rosemary that covers the Rhône's hillsides — is believed by many producers to directly influence the character of wines grown nearby.
Cairanne was elevated to its own cru status in 2016, separating it from the broader Côtes du Rhône Villages appellation — a recognition of the village's consistently superior wine quality.

name: Pinot Gris, Alsace
tableside: Domaine Weinbach is one of Alsace's greatest estates — a biodynamic family domaine that has been making wine since the 17th century. Their Pinot Gris is the richest, most opulent white on the list: honeyed, spiced, and built for serious food. Nothing else on the list tastes like it.
story: Alsace Pinot Gris is a completely different wine from Italian Pinot Grigio — where Pinot Grigio is light, neutral, and dry, Alsatian Pinot Gris is rich, aromatic, and often off-dry. Domaine Weinbach, founded in 1612 by Capuchin monks and run by the Faller family since 1898, makes wines of extraordinary richness and complexity. Their Pinot Gris has the weight and intensity to accompany the richest dishes on the menu — foie gras, roasted duck, aged cheeses.
trivia: Domaine Weinbach began transitioning to biodynamic farming in 1998 and achieved full Demeter certification in 2005 — one of the most committed estates in Alsace.
The Faller family's domaine is built around the Clos des Capucins, a walled vineyard that has been continuously cultivated since the 17th century.
Pinot Gris and Pinot Grigio are genetically identical — the dramatic difference between Alsatian Pinot Gris and Italian Pinot Grigio comes entirely from climate, viticulture, and winemaking choices.

name: Gewürztraminer, Alsace
tableside: Gewürztraminer is one of the most distinctive wines in the world — intensely aromatic, floral, and spiced in a way that no other variety replicates. This Mosbach is certified organic and one of the most affordable examples of a serious Alsatian Gewürz available. For guests who want something that will surprise them.
story: Gewürztraminer — 'spiced Traminer' — has been grown in Alsace for centuries and produces wines that are immediately recognizable to anyone who has encountered them: lychee, rose, ginger, and a headiness that makes them unlike anything else. Mosbach is a small organic producer in the northern Alsace village of Marlenheim, making wines with minimal intervention and a commitment to organic viticulture that has recently been recognized with Demeter certification.
trivia: The name Gewürztraminer translates roughly as 'spiced grape from Tramin' — Tramin (or Termeno) is a village in Alto Adige, Italy, where the variety is believed to have originated.

Gewürztraminer's characteristic lychee aroma comes from a compound called rose oxide, while the rose petal note comes from geraniol — both are monoterpenes present in unusually high concentrations in the variety.

Alsace is the only French wine region where the grape variety is always listed on the label — a result of the region's Germanic wine culture, which has always prioritized varietal labeling over appellation.

name: Assyrtiko, Santorini
tableside: Assyrtiko from Santorini — a volcanic island in the Aegean where vines have been grown on ancient lava rock for 3,500 years. The most mineral and saline white on the list. For guests who want to taste somewhere genuinely unlike anywhere else.
story: Santorini is one of wine's most extraordinary places: a volcanic island where vines grow in basket-shaped formations called kouloura to protect against the fierce Aegean winds, planted in ashy volcanic soil that has never seen phylloxera — the louse that destroyed most of Europe's vineyards in the 19th century. Santorini's vines were spared because the volcanic ash and sandy soil don't support the pest. Some of the island's vines are believed to be over 200 years old. Assyrtiko, the grape variety, absorbs the island's volcanic minerality and produces wines of startling precision and salinity.
trivia: Santorini's vineyards have never been replanted after phylloxera because the volcanic ash soil is inhospitable to the pest — many vines are estimated to be 150 to 200 years old, with some root systems believed to be far older.
The kouloura — basket training system used in Santorini — was developed specifically to protect the vines from the fierce Meltemi winds that blow across the island from the north every summer.
Assyrtiko can maintain unusually high natural acidity even in Santorini's hot, dry climate — a characteristic that makes it one of the few Mediterranean white varieties that can age for decades.

name: Vermentino, Sardinia
tableside: Sardinian Vermentino — one of Italy's most characterful coastal whites. Aromatic, herbal, slightly bitter on the finish in the best possible way. For guests who want something Mediterranean and distinctive at a price that makes it easy to recommend.
story: Cantina Santadi is one of Sardinia's most respected cooperatives — a model of what cooperative winemaking can achieve when quality is prioritized over volume. Their Vermentino comes from vineyards in the Sulcis region of southwestern Sardinia, where the Mediterranean climate and granite soils produce whites with aromatic intensity and a characteristic bitter almond finish that is entirely the variety's own.
trivia: Vermentino is grown along the entire Mediterranean coast from Sardinia to Liguria to Provence (where it's called Rolle) and Corsica — each location produces a distinctly different expression of the same variety.

The characteristic bitter finish of Sardinian Vermentino is considered a mark of quality by local winemakers — it reflects the variety's natural terpene content and is a sign that the grapes were harvested at the right moment.
Cantina Santadi was founded in 1960 and has been instrumental in raising Sardinia's wine reputation internationally — before their efforts, most Sardinian wine was sold in bulk to mainland Italy.

name: Falanghina 'Preto,' Campania
tableside: Falanghina is one of Campania's signature white varieties — an ancient grape that the Romans prized highly and that nearly disappeared in the 20th century before a revival by quality-focused Campanian producers. Aromatic, food-friendly, and genuinely Italian.
story: Falanghina is believed to be one of the oldest cultivated grapes in Italy — some historians argue it was the basis for Falernum, the wine that ancient Romans considered their finest. After centuries of neglect and near-extinction, a revival began in the 1980s when Campanian producers recognized the variety's quality potential. Capolino is a family estate in the Sannio area of Campania farming with minimal intervention and producing wines that reflect the volcanic and limestone soils of the region.
trivia: Falernum — the wine prized above all others in ancient Rome — is believed by some historians to have been made primarily from Falanghina grown on the slopes of Monte Massico in northern Campania.
The name Falanghina may derive from the Latin word phalanga, meaning 'pole' — a reference to the stakes used to train the vines in the ancient Roman system.

Campania has an extraordinary diversity of indigenous grape varieties — over 100 different native varieties have been identified in the region, more than almost any other comparably sized wine area in Italy.

name: Greco 'Vento,' Campania
tableside: Greco is Falanghina's richer, more mineral neighbor — the grape behind the famous Greco di Tufo DOCG. Where the Falanghina is fresh and floral, the Greco is more structured and stony. Two bottles from the same producer showing two completely different indigenous Campanian varieties.
story: Greco di Tufo — the appellation this variety is most associated with — gets its name from the tufo (volcanic rock) soils of the town of Tufo in Avellino province. The volcanic and clay soils give Greco a stony, almost smoky mineral quality that distinguishes it from lighter coastal whites. Capolino's Vento is named for the wind that sweeps through the Campanian hills — a wine that reflects the elemental character of the landscape.
trivia: Greco di Tufo received DOCG status in 2003 — the highest classification in Italian wine — in recognition of the variety's quality potential in the volcanic soils of Avellino province.

The tufo rock that gives the appellation its name is a type of volcanic tuff formed from consolidated volcanic ash — it drains well, retains heat, and imparts the characteristic mineral quality to the wines grown on it.
Greco is believed to have been brought to southern Italy by ancient Greek colonists — the 'Greco' in the name is a direct reference to this origin.

name: Cavallo delle Fate/Grillo, Sicily
tableside: Sicilian white from Regaleali — one of the island's most historic estates, farming in the elevated interior where altitude tempers the Mediterranean heat. Grillo is the indigenous variety; Cavallo delle Fate ('fairy horse') is their light, aromatic expression. Fresh, distinctive, and well-priced.
story: Tenuta Regaleali has been farming in the Sicilian interior since the 19th century and is one of the island's most storied wine estates. Their vineyards sit at 400 to 900 meters elevation in the heart of Sicily — significantly cooler than the coastal sites — which allows white varieties to retain freshness that would be impossible at lower elevations. Grillo is one of Sicily's most interesting indigenous whites, producing wines with a distinctive herbal and floral character.
trivia: Tenuta Regaleali is the estate behind the Tasca d'Almerita brand — one of Sicily's most celebrated wine families, who have been influential in raising the island's wine reputation internationally since the 1950s.

Grillo was originally developed as a blending variety for Marsala — the fortified wine for which Sicily is historically famous. As the market for Marsala declined, producers began making unfortified still wine from Grillo and discovered its quality potential.

Sicily is now considered one of Italy's most exciting wine regions — the combination of ancient indigenous varieties, volcanic soils, and improving viticulture has attracted significant international attention over the last two decades.

name: Chardonnay/Cataratto/Inzolia/Grecanico, Sicily
tableside: A Sicilian white blend — Chardonnay with three indigenous varieties, Cataratto, Inzolia, and Grecanico. The indigenous grapes bring aromatics and a Sicilian character that lifts the whole blend beyond what Chardonnay would do alone. Generous, approachable, and interesting.
story: This blend is Regaleali's argument that Sicily's indigenous varieties can enhance even the world's most planted grape. Cataratto brings texture; Inzolia brings aromatics; Grecanico brings freshness; Chardonnay provides structure. The result has a distinctly Sicilian personality despite the Chardonnay base — sun-drenched, aromatic, and generous.
trivia: Cataratto is Sicily's most planted white variety by far — it accounts for roughly 20% of all the island's vineyards. Despite its ubiquity it is rarely seen on labels outside of blends.
Inzolia — sometimes called Ansonica on the Tuscan coast — produces wines with a characteristic nutty, herbal quality that adds complexity to blends.
Sicily produces more wine than any other Italian region — though much of it has historically been sold for blending rather than bottled under a Sicilian label.

name: Arneis, Langhe
tableside: Arneis is Piedmont's signature white variety — 'little rascal' in the local dialect, named for how difficult it is to grow. Where Barolo and Barbaresco get all the attention, Arneis produces a delicate, floral white that is the perfect foil to the region's rich food.
story: Arneis was nearly extinct by the 1970s when the variety had declined to only a few hectares in the Roero district across the Tanaro River from Barolo. Giacomo Bologna of Braida and Bruno Giacosa are credited with its revival — recognizing that Piedmont needed a quality white variety to balance its powerful reds. Today Arneis is the standard aperitif white in the region, served everywhere from trattorias to Michelin-starred restaurants.
trivia: Arneis means 'little rascal' or 'rascal' in the Piedmontese dialect — a reference to the variety's difficulty in the vineyard. It ripens unevenly, is prone to oxidation, and requires careful handling.

Before the Arneis revival, the variety was historically planted in Nebbiolo vineyards to attract birds away from the precious Nebbiolo grapes — a sacrificial planting that protected the more valuable crop.
Terre del Barolo is a cooperative in Castiglione Falletto — one of the Barolo villages — that produces wine from member growers across the entire Barolo zone.

name: Sauvignon/Pinot Blanc 'Upupa,' Alto Adige
tableside: A blend of Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Blanc from Alto Adige — two varieties that rarely appear together but make perfect sense in this Alpine context. Weingut Abraham is a small family producer making wines of real elegance from high-altitude vineyards. Floral, precise, and genuinely Alpine.
story: Weingut Abraham farms in the Vinschgau valley in Alto Adige at elevations between 700 and 1,000 meters — among the highest vineyards in the entire region. At this altitude, growing seasons are long and cool, producing wines with exceptional freshness and delicate aromatics. The Upupa — named after the hoopoe bird that nests in their vineyards — blends Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Blanc to create something that is more elegant than either variety produces alone at this elevation.
trivia: The Vinschgau valley in western Alto Adige is one of the highest wine-producing valleys in Europe — vineyards here sit at elevations that would be impossible in warmer regions.
The Upupa bird — hoopoe — is considered a good omen in many European traditions and is also listed as unclean in Leviticus. Its striking orange and black plumage makes it immediately recognizable.
Alto Adige produces wine in both German and Italian naming conventions — the region is officially bilingual, and most producers use both Südtirol and Alto Adige on their labels.

name: Pinot Blanc 'In Der Lamm,' Alto Adige
tableside: Abraham's single-vineyard Pinot Blanc — the In Der Lamm site is their most important white wine parcel. More concentrated and textured than the Upupa blend, with the same Alpine precision. One of the finest Pinot Blancs on the list.
story: The In Der Lamm vineyard is Weingut Abraham's most valued white wine site — a single parcel in the Vinschgau at high elevation on a combination of porphyry and sand soils that produce fruit with unusual concentration and mineral character. Pinot Blanc at this altitude has a different personality from lowland expressions: crisper, more focused, with a stony quality that reflects the vineyard rather than the winery.
trivia: Porphyry is a volcanic rock type that appears in specific parts of Alto Adige — it creates wines with a distinctive reddish mineral character quite different from the limestone soils found in other parts of the region.
The name 'In Der Lamm' — 'in the lamb' — is a traditional Südtirol naming convention referring to the shape of the parcel. Similar lamb-shaped vineyard names appear throughout the region.

Abraham is one of a new generation of small Alto Adige producers who have moved away from the cooperative model — most production in the region goes through one of several powerful cooperatives, making independent estates like Abraham genuinely rare.

name: Pinot Blanc 'Vom Muschelkalk,' Alto Adige
tableside: A second Abraham Pinot Blanc — the Vom Muschelkalk ('from the shell limestone') shows what a different soil type does to the same variety from the same producer. Where the In Der Lamm has texture and richness from porphyry, this one is more linear and mineral from limestone. Same winemaker, same philosophy, completely different personality.
story: Having two single-vineyard Pinot Blancs from the same producer in the same vintage — the In Der Lamm and the Vom Muschelkalk — gives the same comparative opportunity as the multiple Riesling vineyards in the Wachau section. Porphyry versus shell limestone, even within the same small family estate, produces noticeably different wines. The Muschelkalk is the more mineral and austere of the two.
trivia: Muschelkalk — shell limestone — is the same geological formation found in Tement's Styrian vineyards and in parts of Germany and Alsace. Wherever it appears, it tends to produce wines with a distinctive chalky mineral quality.

Alto Adige has one of the most complex geological landscapes of any wine region in Europe — the result of millions of years of Alpine tectonic activity that left different rock types in close proximity.
Weingut Abraham produces wines in quantities so small that they are rarely available outside specialist wine shops and restaurant lists — finding three separate bottlings on a single list is unusual.

name: Chenin Blanc 'Mary Delany,' Citrusdal Mountains
tableside: South African Chenin Blanc — a country that has more old-vine Chenin than France itself. Botanica's Mary Delany is from the Citrusdal Mountains, a cool, remote growing area where old bush vines produce fruit of extraordinary concentration. For guests who think South African wine is just Pinotage, this is the revelation.
story: South Africa planted Chenin Blanc — locally called Steen — in enormous quantities during the 20th century, mostly for bulk wine and brandy. When the market shifted, many of these old vines were simply left in place rather than replanted, creating a resource of ancient, low-yielding bush vines that produces some of the most concentrated Chenin Blanc in the world. Botanica's Mary Delany comes from such vines in the Citrusdal Mountains — a remote, cool growing area north of the Olifants River where the combination of altitude and ocean influence produces white wines of genuine complexity.
trivia: South Africa has more old-vine Chenin Blanc than the Loire Valley in France — the country where Chenin originated. This is a result of mass plantings in the 1960s and 70s that were never replaced.
Mary Delany was an 18th century botanical artist who created extraordinarily detailed paper flower mosaics — Botanica named their wine range after her as a tribute to botanical art.
The Citrusdal Mountains receive significantly more rainfall than Stellenbosch or Franschhoek, which allows dry farming without irrigation — a key factor in the concentration of old bush vine fruit.

name: Airen, Alejairen, La Mancha
tableside: Airen is the most planted white grape in Spain — and possibly in the world — yet almost no one has heard of it. El Vinculo, part of the Alejandro Fernandez group that makes Pesquera, takes this humble variety seriously. A genuinely unusual bottle that rewards curious guests.
story: Airen covers more vineyard land in Spain than any other variety — roughly 25% of all Spanish vineyards — yet it is almost invisible on wine lists because it has historically been used for bulk wine and distillation rather than quality bottling. Alejandro Fernandez, who built his reputation on Ribera del Duero Tempranillo with Pesquera, saw an opportunity to show what Airen could be with serious viticulture and winemaking. The result is one of the most unexpected bottles on the list: a quality wine from a variety that almost no one treats with respect.
trivia: Airen is believed to be the most widely planted white grape variety in the world by total hectares — yet it appears on almost no quality wine lists because producers have historically treated it as a bulk commodity.
La Mancha, where Airen dominates, is the largest wine appellation in the world by area — it covers roughly a third of all Spanish vineyards.

Alejandro Fernandez — the force behind El Vinculo — is one of Spain's most celebrated winemakers, known primarily for Tinto Pesquera in Ribera del Duero. His decision to make serious wine from Airen is a deliberate provocation.

name: Viura/Garnacha Blanca/Maturana Blanca Gran Reserva Blanco, Rioja
tableside: A white Rioja Gran Reserva — a category that barely exists and that most guests have never considered. White Rioja at this level is oak-aged and built for the long term, more like white Burgundy than anything Spanish. The 2015 is a decade old and showing real complexity.
story: White Rioja is one of wine's great underappreciated categories. While the world focuses on Rioja's Tempranillo reds, a small number of producers have been making age-worthy oak-aged whites from Viura (Macabeo) and indigenous varieties for generations. At the Gran Reserva level — minimum aging requirements are two years in oak and two in bottle — these wines develop a complexity that challenges white Burgundy. The 2015 at a decade of age is showing exactly what serious white Rioja becomes.
trivia: Maturana Blanca is one of Rioja's rarest indigenous white varieties — it was thought extinct until the 1990s when a few surviving plants were discovered in old vineyards and a replanting program began.
White Rioja Gran Reserva requires minimum two years in oak and two years in bottle before release — a total of at least four years of aging before the wine can be sold.

Viura — the most widely planted white variety in Rioja — is the same grape as Macabeo, the variety used in Cava and in white Côtes du Rhône blends. In Rioja it produces wines with more body and aging potential than in its other incarnations.

name: Fendant, Valais
tableside: Fendant is the Swiss name for Chasselas — a grape found almost nowhere else in the world at serious quality levels. Domaine de Beudon farms biodynamically on the steep terraces of the Valais, one of Europe's most dramatic vineyard landscapes. For guests who want something genuinely unusual and impeccably made.
story: Domaine de Beudon is one of Switzerland's most extraordinary wine estates — built on terraces so steep that the owners installed a cable car to access the vineyards and have lived there year-round since the 1970s. The Valais, running along the Rhône River east of Lake Geneva, is Switzerland's most important wine region and produces Fendant of remarkable quality from ancient terraces. Chasselas — the grape behind Fendant — is considered too neutral by most of the world's wine producers, but in the Valais's rocky, sunny terraces it produces wines of genuine mineral character.
trivia: Domaine de Beudon is accessible only by cable car — the owners, the Rouvinez family, live on the property year-round and farm the steep terraces without mechanization.
Chasselas is considered Switzerland's national grape variety — it is grown across the country under various names including Fendant (Valais), Dorin (Vaud), and Perlan (Geneva).

Switzerland produces very little wine relative to its size — roughly 100 million liters per year, compared to France's 4.5 billion. Most Swiss wine is consumed domestically, making bottles like this genuinely rare outside the country.

name: Riesling/Sylvaner, Valais
tableside: Riesling/Sylvaner is the Swiss name for Müller-Thurgau — a crossing created in Switzerland in 1882. At over a decade old, this Beudon is showing what the variety does with age on the Valais terraces: complex, mineral, and genuinely contemplative. An unusual and serious bottle.
story: Riesling × Sylvaner — Müller-Thurgau — was created by Hermann Müller from the Swiss canton of Thurgau at the Geisenheim research station in Germany in 1882. It was originally believed to be a crossing of Riesling and Silvaner (hence the Swiss name) but DNA analysis has since revealed it to be Riesling × Madeleine Royale. In most of the world it's planted for high-yield bulk wine, but on the steep Valais terraces of Beudon, farmed biodynamically with very low yields, it produces wine of real complexity and longevity.
trivia: Müller-Thurgau is the most widely planted variety in Germany by area, but it is associated almost entirely with cheap bulk wine in that context. In the right hands and on the right site it is capable of far more.
Domaine de Beudon has been certified biodynamic since the late 1970s — among the earliest biodynamic wine estates in Europe.

The Valais receives over 300 days of sunshine per year despite its Alpine location — a rain shadow effect created by the mountains keeps the valley warm and dry even as the surrounding peaks are covered in snow.

name: Petit Manseng, Markham
tableside: Petit Manseng from one of Virginia's more boutique producers. This variety — originally from southwest France — thrives in Virginia's climate and produces wines with a distinctive tropical and floral character. Approachable and aromatic.
story: Château O'Brien is a small family estate in Markham in the Blue Ridge foothills, farming a range of varieties that suit Virginia's climate. Petit Manseng — a high-acidity variety from the Jurançon region of southwest France — has proven exceptionally well-suited to Virginia: its thick skins resist the humidity that damages thinner-skinned varieties, and its natural acidity keeps it fresh despite the state's warm summers.
trivia: Petit Manseng is the variety behind Jurançon Sec and the great Jurançon sweet wines of southwest France — its thick skins allow it to be left on the vine for late harvest without rotting.

Virginia's humidity during the growing season is the primary challenge for fine wine production — varieties with thick skins like Petit Manseng, Tannat, and Cabernet Franc have proven more resistant to fungal disease than thin-skinned varieties like Pinot Noir.
Château O'Brien is named after the O'Brien family who farm the estate — one of Virginia's smaller artisan producers working with limited but focused production.

name: Grüner Veltliner/Riesling, Ox Eye Vineyards
tableside: A Virginia wine made from Grüner Veltliner and Riesling — the same two varieties that define Kamptal in Austria — grown at Ox Eye Vineyards in the Shenandoah Valley. A direct conversation between what we do here and what Austria does there. Worth championing to guests who love the Austrian section.
story: Ox Eye Vineyards in the Shenandoah Valley is one of Virginia's most serious experiments in Austrian viticulture — they've planted Grüner Veltliner and Riesling specifically because the valley's climate and elevation share certain characteristics with Kamptal. Dogwood & Thistle works with this fruit to produce a blend that is explicitly Austrian in inspiration but distinctly Virginian in character. For guests who've worked through the Austrian section, this is the local reflection of what they've been tasting.
trivia: The Shenandoah Valley is one of Virginia's most promising wine regions — the valley sits at higher elevation than the Piedmont, with cooler temperatures and better diurnal temperature swings that suit cool-climate varieties like Grüner and Riesling.

Grüner Veltliner has been successfully grown in Virginia since the early 2000s — it adapts reasonably well to the state's climate and produces wines with the characteristic white pepper note, though with more tropical fruit than the Austrian originals.

Dogwood & Thistle takes its name from two plants that define different landscapes — the dogwood is Virginia's state flower; the thistle represents the Scottish heritage of some of the Shenandoah Valley's early settlers.

name: Reserve Chardonnay, Amissville
tableside: Gray Ghost's Reserve Chardonnay — one of Virginia's most established producers, farming in Rappahannock County near the Blue Ridge. A classic Virginia Chardonnay: oak-influenced, round, and food-friendly. For guests who want something local and approachable.
story: Gray Ghost has been farming in Amissville in Rappahannock County since 1987 — one of Virginia's longer-established wine estates. Their Reserve Chardonnay is made in a classic style: barrel fermented and aged in French oak, producing a wine with the cream and vanilla character that guests associate with quality Chardonnay, but with a distinctly Virginian fruit expression that reflects the state's particular combination of clay soils and continental climate.
trivia: Gray Ghost is named after Colonel John Singleton Mosby, the Confederate guerrilla leader known as the 'Gray Ghost' who operated throughout Rappahannock County during the Civil War.

Rappahannock County is one of Virginia's most rural and scenic wine regions — the combination of mountain views, historic farms, and quality wineries has made it a popular weekend destination from Washington D.C.
Virginia's wine industry has grown from fewer than 10 wineries in 1980 to over 300 today — making it one of the fastest-growing wine regions in the United States.

name: Seyval Blanc, Amissville
tableside: Seyval Blanc is a French-American hybrid variety that produces crisp, food-friendly whites in Virginia's humid climate. Rarely seen outside the eastern United States. Fresh, honest, and genuinely local.
story: Seyval Blanc is a hybrid crossing of two Seibel varieties developed in France in the early 20th century — it was created specifically to resist the fungal diseases that devastate European vinifera grapes in humid climates like Virginia's. While the wine world has traditionally looked down on hybrid varieties, their disease resistance makes them genuinely valuable in the eastern United States, and at their best they produce wines of real freshness and food-friendliness.
trivia: Seyval Blanc is one of the most widely planted hybrid varieties in the eastern United States — its disease resistance makes it significantly easier to farm in Virginia's humid summers than European vinifera grapes.

The wine establishment's prejudice against hybrids has softened somewhat in recent years as producers in Virginia, New York, and the Midwest have demonstrated that certain hybrids can produce genuinely high-quality wines.
Gray Ghost has been one of Virginia's most consistent producers of Seyval Blanc — maintaining the variety when many Virginia wineries abandoned it in favor of the more fashionable European varieties.

name: Vidal Blanc, Amissville
tableside: Vidal Blanc is another French-American hybrid — aromatic, fresh, and genuinely suited to Virginia's climate. Best known as the grape behind Canadian ice wine, but here made dry and food-friendly. The most aromatic of the Gray Ghost whites.
story: Vidal Blanc was developed in France in the 1930s as a cold-hardy, disease-resistant hybrid suitable for northern climates. In Canada it became famous as the primary grape for ice wine. In Virginia, where it ripens fully without freezing, it produces wines with a distinctive floral and peachy character that is closer to an aromatic European variety than the neutral hybrid style most guests expect.
trivia: Vidal Blanc is the primary variety used for Canadian Icewine — the grapes are left on the vine until January or February, when they freeze naturally and are pressed while frozen, producing intensely sweet concentrated juice.

As a hybrid variety, Vidal Blanc is not permitted in many European wine appellations, which consider all hybrids inferior to pure vinifera grapes — a position that is increasingly challenged by quality results from eastern American producers.
The Blanc in Vidal Blanc does not indicate the wine style — it's simply part of the variety's name, distinguishing it from other Vidal crossings.

name: Chardonnay 'Hardscrabble,' Linden
tableside: Linden is Virginia's most serious white wine producer and the Hardscrabble is their flagship — a single-vineyard Chardonnay from the Blue Ridge foothills that consistently ranks as one of the finest white wines made on the East Coast. Local pride, absolutely justified.
story: Jim Law built Linden Vineyards over four decades into one of Virginia's most respected estates — his Hardscrabble Chardonnay has appeared on lists alongside established Burgundy producers and holds its own. The vineyard sits at 1,400 feet elevation in Fauquier County, where the higher altitude and clay-loam soils over granite produce Chardonnay with natural acidity and minerality that most Virginia wines lack. It's the bottle to open when a guest questions whether Virginia can make serious wine.
trivia: Linden Vineyards has been producing wine since 1987 and Jim Law is widely considered the most influential figure in Virginia's fine wine movement — his approach to viticulture has shaped how a generation of Virginia winemakers think about quality.

The name Hardscrabble refers to the difficulty of farming the thin, rocky soils on the vineyard's steep slopes — originally a term for subsistence farming on poor land, here reclaimed as a badge of quality.

Virginia Chardonnay from the Blue Ridge foothills has been compared favorably to premier cru white Burgundy in international blind tastings — a fact worth mentioning to guests who default to French whites.

name: Petit Manseng, Clifton
tableside: Paradise Springs is a Fairfax County winery — closer to Washington D.C. than most Virginia wine estates. Their Petit Manseng is fresh, aromatic, and approachable. For guests who want to support a genuinely local producer.
story: Paradise Springs is located in Clifton, Virginia — within Fairfax County, making it one of the closest quality wine producers to Washington D.C. Their Petit Manseng reflects what the variety does in Virginia's warm, humid climate: aromatic, round, and built for approachable drinking rather than cellaring.
trivia: Fairfax County, where Paradise Springs is located, is better known as a Washington D.C. suburb than a wine region — but its western portions have suitable elevation and soil for quality viticulture.
Petit Manseng was first planted in Virginia in the 1990s when producers were searching for varieties that could resist the state's humid disease pressure while producing quality wine.
Paradise Springs has been recognized with medals at the Governor's Cup — Virginia's most prestigious wine competition — multiple times since their founding.

name: Petit Manseng Concrete Aged, Clifton
tableside: The same Paradise Springs Petit Manseng but aged in a concrete egg — a vessel that adds texture and a subtle mineral quality without the oak influence of barrels. A direct comparison to their standard version is one of the more instructive tasting exercises on the Virginia section.
story: Concrete eggs have become increasingly popular among natural and low-intervention winemakers over the last two decades — the egg shape creates a natural convection current in the fermenting wine, keeping the lees in suspension and building complexity without the oxygen exposure of barrels. For Petit Manseng, the concrete adds a subtle mineral and textural quality that the stainless steel version lacks, while avoiding the vanilla and toast of oak.
trivia: Concrete fermentation vessels were standard in European winemaking for centuries before stainless steel became dominant in the 1960s and 70s. The recent revival reflects a broader movement toward traditional and minimal-intervention winemaking.

The egg shape of modern concrete vats is believed to create a natural convection current that keeps the lees in constant gentle motion — acting as a natural bâtonnage without the winemaker needing to stir the lees manually.

Concrete is porous enough to allow micro-oxygenation — similar to large neutral oak casks — but does not impart flavor the way oak does, making it ideal for producers who want texture without oakiness.

name: Chardonnay, Huntly
tableside: Rappahannock Cellars' still Chardonnay — the same estate that makes the Prestige sparkling. Their still white shows a lighter, more floral face of the property. A reliable, well-priced Virginia Chardonnay from one of the state's most consistent producers.
story: Rappahannock Cellars has built a reputation for both sparkling and still wines from their Huntly estate in the Blue Ridge foothills. Their still Chardonnay is made in a lighter, more floral style than the Prestige sparkling — unoaked or lightly oaked, designed for immediate accessibility and food compatibility rather than the extended lees aging that defines the sparkling program.
trivia: Huntly is a small community in Rappahannock County, Virginia — a rural area that has become one of the state's more concentrated wine regions with several quality estates in close proximity.
Rappahannock County takes its name from the Rappahannock River, which flows from the Blue Ridge Mountains through the Virginia Piedmont to the Chesapeake Bay.
Virginia was the site of the first European attempts at winemaking in North America — the Virginia Company's charter of 1606 specifically mentioned wine production as a goal for the new colony.

name: Chardonnay, Purcellville
tableside: Walsh Family Winery is in Purcellville in Loudoun County — one of Virginia's most active wine regions. Their Chardonnay is riper and rounder than the Blue Ridge expressions, reflecting Loudoun's warmer, lower-elevation terroir. Approachable and food-friendly.
story: Walsh Family Winery farms in the rolling hills of Loudoun County, where the proximity to Washington D.C. has driven rapid growth in both winery establishment and wine tourism. Their Chardonnay reflects the warmer, lower-elevation character of Loudoun compared to the higher Blue Ridge sites — riper fruit, more accessible texture, and a style that suits the casual weekend visitor as much as the serious wine drinker.
trivia: Loudoun County is now home to more than 40 wineries — more than any other county in Virginia — making it one of the densest wine regions per square mile in the eastern United States.
Walsh Family has built a reputation for Bordeaux varieties as well as Chardonnay — their Tannat and Merlot/Cab Franc blends appear in the red section of the list.

The proximity to Washington D.C. — roughly 40 miles — means Loudoun wine country is easily accessible as a day trip from the capital, which has significantly shaped the region's wine tourism identity.

name: SP68 White, Moscato di Alessandria/Albanello, Sicily
tableside: Arianna Occhipinti is one of natural wine's most celebrated producers — her SP68 is named after a Sicilian country road. The white is a fragrant, barely-there natural wine: light, floral, aromatic, and made with zero intervention. For guests curious about natural wine without wanting something challenging.
story: Arianna Occhipinti began making wine in her uncle's cellar at age 21 and has since become one of the most influential voices in the natural wine movement globally. The SP68 series — named after the provincial road that runs past her vineyards in the Vittoria area of southern Sicily — is her most accessible range: wines made with minimal intervention that express the pure character of their varieties and terroir. The white, made from Moscato di Alessandria and Albanello, is as close to grape juice that has been persuaded to become wine as anything on the list.
trivia: Arianna Occhipinti is the niece of Giusto Occhipinti, who co-founded the celebrated COS winery in Sicily — she grew up surrounded by serious natural winemaking.

SP68 is the road designation for a rural Sicilian provincial road — using a road number as a wine name was a deliberate rejection of the pretension Arianna associated with conventional wine marketing.

Moscato di Alessandria — also known as Zibibbo — is the variety behind Passito di Pantelleria, one of Sicily's most celebrated sweet wines. In this context it's harvested early and fermented dry, producing something completely different from its typical dessert wine incarnation.

name: Weiss Traminer, Carnuntum
tableside: Weiss Traminer — Gewürztraminer — made with extended skin contact, giving it an amber color and a tannic grip that the variety never has in conventional winemaking. Biodynamic farming, zero added sulfur. For guests who want to understand what orange wine is actually about.
story: Hanna Glatzer is a young biodynamic producer in Carnuntum — a small appellation east of Vienna better known for Zweigelt reds than aromatic whites. Her skin-contact Weiss Traminer is made by fermenting the Traminer juice in contact with the grape skins for an extended period, extracting tannins, color, and complexity that you never find in conventionally made Gewürztraminer. The result is an orange wine that retains the variety's distinctive aromatics — rose, lychee, spice — while adding structure and grip.
trivia: Orange wine — white wine made with extended skin contact — is one of the oldest winemaking techniques in the world. The method was standard in the ancient world and survived in traditional Georgian and Slovenian production before being rediscovered by natural wine producers in the 1990s.

Skin contact extracts tannins from the grape skins — tannins are normally associated only with red wine, but all grape varieties have them. In white wine they provide grip and structure that conventional winemaking deliberately avoids.

Carnuntum takes its name from the ancient Roman city of Carnuntum, which was a major military and trading center on the Danube — the same Roman presence that established viticulture throughout the region.

name: Auxerrois Blanc 'Amphore Amore,' Neusiedlersee
tableside: Amphore Amore — wine fermented and aged in clay amphora, the ancient vessel that preceded both barrel and stainless steel. Heidi Schröck is one of Austria's most thoughtful producers. This is an orange wine of genuine depth: oxidative, complex, and genuinely ancient in feel.
story: Heidi Schröck ferments this wine in terracotta amphora — clay vessels used for wine storage since ancient Mesopotamia, now experiencing a revival among natural wine producers who value their micro-oxygenation properties and cultural resonance. Auxerrois Blanc is a variety related to Pinot Blanc and Pinot Gris, rarely seen on its own. Combined with amphora aging and extended skin contact, Schröck creates something that feels genuinely ancient: complex, oxidative, and unlike anything else on the list.
trivia: Clay amphora were the primary vessel for wine storage and transport throughout the ancient Mediterranean world — they predate oak barrels by thousands of years.

The revival of amphora winemaking in the modern era was driven largely by Josko Gravner in Friuli, who began experimenting with Georgian-style clay vessels in the late 1990s and visited Georgia in 2000, returning with qvevri that transformed his winemaking.
Auxerrois Blanc is believed to be related to Pinot Blanc and is grown primarily in Alsace, Luxembourg, and Austria — it produces wines of delicate aromatics and good natural acidity.

name: Morillon, Styria
tableside: A decade-old biodynamic orange wine from Muster — Chardonnay fermented on the skins and aged in large oak casks without added sulfur. At ten years this is one of the most complex and evolved bottles on the natural wine section. For guests who want to understand what the extreme end of this category can achieve.
story: Sepp and Maria Muster farm biodynamically in Styria and make some of Austria's most intellectually serious natural wines — slow-fermented on the skins, aged without additions, and released only when the family believes the wine is ready. The 2014 Morillon at a decade old is showing exactly what serious orange wine becomes with age: the oxidative fruit has deepened into something complex and contemplative, the tannins from the skin contact have integrated, and what remains is genuinely profound.
trivia: Sepp Muster is one of the most influential figures in Austrian natural wine — his commitment to zero added sulfur and minimal intervention has inspired a generation of younger producers.
Styria — Steiermark in German — is Austria's southernmost wine region, bordering Slovenia, and produces wines with a different character from the more famous Kamptal and Wachau.

Zero-sulfur wines like the Muster range require careful storage — they are sensitive to temperature fluctuation and oxidation in a way that conventionally made wines are not. Worth mentioning to guests who plan to cellar rather than drink immediately.

name: Sauvignon Blanc/Morillon 'Erde,' Styria
tableside: Erde — 'earth' — is Muster's most austere expression: Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay fermented together on the skins, aged in large oak casks, zero sulfur. Nothing is softened. For guests who want to be challenged rather than comforted.
story: The Erde is Muster at their most uncompromising — a skin-contact blend of Sauvignon Blanc and Morillon (Chardonnay) that ferments and ages with zero intervention. The name 'earth' reflects the philosophy: this is wine that tastes of the soil it came from, not of winemaking technique. At five years the 2019 is still young — the tannins from the skin contact are present and the oxidative complexity is building. Not a wine for guests who want comfort.
trivia: The concept of terroir — the idea that wine should taste of the place it comes from — is the philosophical foundation of the natural wine movement. Wines like the Erde are designed to express nothing but their origin.

Sauvignon Blanc and Morillon (Chardonnay) are the two most important white varieties in Styria — blending them together is a statement about the region as a whole rather than about either variety individually.
The extended skin contact in Erde produces tannins that will continue to integrate for years — natural wine producers argue this makes their wines more age-worthy than conventionally made whites.

name: Sauvignon Blanc, Styria
tableside: Muster's skin-contact Sauvignon Blanc — the same variety as the Tement Zieregg on the white wine list, but made in an entirely different way. Where Tement's version is clean and precise, this is amber, tannic, and austere. Two completely different philosophies applied to the same grape variety from the same region.
story: Comparing Muster's skin-contact Sauvignon Blanc to Tement's conventionally made Zieregg is one of the most instructive exercises on the entire list — same variety, same region (Styria), completely different outcomes based entirely on winemaking philosophy. The Muster is made with extended skin contact and zero sulfur; the Tement is clean, precise, and sulfured. Neither is right or wrong — they're different arguments about what wine should be.
trivia: Tannins in white wine were considered a defect by most conventional winemakers for decades — only the natural wine revival of the 1990s and 2000s reframed them as a quality characteristic in certain contexts.

The same grape variety can produce radically different wines based on winemaking decisions — this is why the natural vs. conventional comparison is so instructive. The terroir is the same; what changes is the human intervention.
Muster exports primarily to Germany, Japan, and the United States — three markets where natural wine has found the most enthusiastic audiences.

name: Chardonnay/Sauvignon Blanc 'Sgaminegg,' Styria
tableside: Sgaminegg is Muster's flagship single-vineyard expression — a specific site that produces their most concentrated and complex wine. Seven years old, biodynamic, zero sulfur. The most serious natural wine on the list.
story: The Sgaminegg is a named site in Styria that Muster has farmed biodynamically for decades — their most important parcel and the source of their most concentrated wines. At seven years, the 2017 is showing the full development that skin-contact winemaking can achieve with time: the primary fruit has given way to something deeper and more mineral, the tannins have integrated, and the wine has the kind of complexity that conventional white wine almost never achieves.
trivia: Single-vineyard natural wines are rare — most natural wine producers blend across multiple sites to balance vintage variation and ensure consistency.
The Sgaminegg site is on clay and limestone soils — Muster argues that the clay provides richness and the limestone provides the mineral precision that runs through all their wines.
Seven years is still relatively young for a Muster Sgaminegg — the estate believes their wines peak between 10 and 20 years after harvest.

name: Pinot Blanc/Chardonnay 'Weiss No. 11,' Styria
tableside: Strohmeier is a biodynamic producer in Styria making some of the most elegant natural wines in Austria. Weiss No. 11 is their white blend — Pinot Blanc and Chardonnay with skin contact — showing the floral, delicate end of the orange wine spectrum. More accessible than the Muster range but equally serious.
story: Franz Strohmeier farms biodynamically in the West Styria wine region and has built a reputation for wines that sit at the intersection of natural winemaking and genuine elegance — less austere than some natural wine producers, more structured than the light-intervention crowd. The Weiss No. 11 is his flagship white blend: skin contact that adds texture and complexity without tipping into the heavy, tannic territory of the more extreme orange wines.
trivia: West Styria — Weststeiermark — is Austria's smallest wine region and is primarily known for Schilcher, a rosé made from the indigenous Blauer Wildbacher grape. Strohmeier's work with white varieties is a relatively recent development in the region's identity.

The numbering system Strohmeier uses for his wines — Weiss No. 11, Rot No. 9 — reflects his philosophical approach to winemaking as a series of ongoing experiments rather than fixed, branded products.
Biodynamic farming in Styria faces particular challenges from the region's rainy, humid climate — making the commitment to zero synthetic inputs significantly more demanding than in drier regions.

name: Vineyard Blend 'Cat-Silver No. 2,' Styria
tableside: Strohmeier's field blend — multiple varieties harvested and fermented together from the same vineyard, as was traditional before the modern focus on single varieties. Skin contact, biodynamic, zero additions. A wine that looks backward to go forward.
story: Field blending — harvesting multiple grape varieties together from the same vineyard and fermenting them as one — was the standard winemaking practice throughout Europe before the 20th century emphasis on varietal purity. Strohmeier's Cat-Silver is a deliberate revival of this tradition: grapes from his mixed biodynamic vineyard harvested together and fermented as a unit, producing a wine that reflects the whole vineyard rather than any single variety.
trivia: Field blending was the norm in European viticulture for centuries — the concept of a 'varietal wine' made from a single grape is largely a 20th century invention driven by marketing and appellation law.

In many traditional wine regions — particularly in parts of Portugal, Spain, and the Rhône — field blends remain standard practice, producing wines that are difficult to replicate because they depend entirely on the specific mix of varieties in a specific vineyard.
The name 'Cat-Silver' is an Austrian dialect term for fool's gold — pyrite — which appears in the soils of Strohmeier's vineyard and which he considers a mark of mineral quality in the wines.

name: Gelber Muskateller 'Lockvogel,' Burgenland
tableside: Lockvogel — 'decoy bird' — is Weinbau Wenzel's skin-contact Gelber Muskateller. Where Tement's dry Muskateller in the Other Whites section is clean and floral, this is amber, structured, and complex. The same grape variety, completely different winemaking philosophy.
story: Weinbau Wenzel is a small biodynamic estate in Rust on the shores of Lake Neusiedl — the same village as Heidi Schröck. Their Lockvogel takes the naturally aromatic Gelber Muskateller variety and adds extended skin contact, producing a wine that retains the variety's floral aromatics while adding the amber color, tannic structure, and oxidative complexity of orange winemaking. The name — 'decoy bird' — refers to the traditional hunting practice of using a caged bird to attract wild birds within range.
trivia: Rust is one of Austria's most historic wine towns — it received a royal charter in 1681 allowing it to put the letter R on its barrels, a mark of quality that was recognized throughout Central Europe.

Gelber Muskateller (Yellow Muscat) produces one of wine's most intensely aromatic profiles — the combination of lychee, rose, and ginger survives even the extended skin contact of orange winemaking, which tends to suppress delicate aromatics in less aromatic varieties.

Lake Neusiedl — the shallow lake on whose shores Rust sits — is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its unique cultural and ecological significance. The lake creates a specific microclimate that supports both quality viticulture and one of Europe's most important wetland bird habitats.

name: Pinot Gris 'Voodoo Child,' Burgenland
tableside: Voodoo Child is Wenzel's skin-contact Pinot Gris — a wine that leans into everything that makes orange wine distinctive. Amber in color, structured with tannin, complex from oxidative aging. Named after the Jimi Hendrix song. For guests who want to commit to the natural wine experience.
story: Pinot Gris has a natural affinity for skin contact — its pink-to-gray skins add color, tannin, and the dried fruit and floral complexity that defines the best orange wines. Wenzel's Voodoo Child is made with long skin maceration and aged in large neutral casks without added sulfur, producing a wine of real depth and complexity. The Jimi Hendrix reference is a nod to the radical, rule-breaking spirit of the natural wine movement.
trivia: Pinot Gris actually has pinkish-gray skins — unlike most white varieties — which is why skin contact produces wines with a distinctive copper-orange color rather than the pale gold of other orange wines.
The color of orange wine comes from anthocyanins in the grape skins — the same pigments that give red wine its color, present in smaller quantities in some white varieties.
