Weinbau Wenzel is one of a cluster of biodynamic producers around Rust who have built an international reputation for serious natural wine — a remarkable concentration of quality in a very small geographic area.

name: Grüner Veltliner RSV Alte Reben, Langenlois — Magnum
tableside: Alte Reben means old vines — this is Bründlmayer's top Grüner Veltliner expression, from their oldest Kamptal blocks, in a magnum, and now a decade old. A 2014 Kamptal Grüner in this format is genuinely rare and shows what the grape can become with time and the right conditions. For guests who know Austrian wine at a serious level, or guests who want to discover it at its absolute peak.
story: The RSV (Reserve) Alte Reben is Bründlmayer's flagship Grüner Veltliner — made from their oldest vines in the Kamptal, vinified with extended lees contact and aged in large neutral oak. In magnum, the 2014 has developed the rich, layered complexity that only great Grüner achieves with age: the bright white pepper and citrus of youth have given way to something deeper, more mineral, and more textured. One of the most impressive white wines on the list.
trivia: Grüner Veltliner is one of the world's great age-worthy white wines — serious examples from top Wachau and Kamptal producers can develop for 20–30 years. The 2014 vintage was excellent in both regions.
Magnums of Austrian white wine are rarely seen outside Austria — the format is associated with Champagne and Burgundy in most markets, making this a genuine rarity worth flagging.
Bründlmayer's Alte Reben has appeared in multiple international comparative tastings alongside top white Burgundy, where it has consistently outperformed wines at several times its price.

name: Riesling, Ried Heiligenstein, Kamptal DAC — Magnum
tableside: A 2015 Hirsch Heiligenstein Riesling in magnum — nearly a decade old and in its absolute prime. The Heiligenstein is one of Austria's most celebrated single vineyards for Riesling: volcanic gneiss soils, south-facing slopes, and a microclimate that produces wines of extraordinary concentration and longevity. In magnum, that aging potential is amplified further.
story: The Heiligenstein is a volcanic gneiss outcrop in the Kamptal that produces some of Austria's most distinctive Riesling — mineral, crystalline, and intensely concentrated. Hirsch is the benchmark producer for this site, farming organically and making wine with minimal intervention. The 2015 was a warm, excellent vintage; in magnum, this wine has had nine years to develop the complexity that makes aged Kamptal Riesling one of the world's great white wine experiences.
trivia: The Heiligenstein vineyard takes its name from the warm, sun-trapping microclimate created by its south-facing volcanic rock face — 'Heiligenstein' means 'holy stone' in German, a reference to the almost miraculous warmth the rock absorbs and radiates.

Hirsch farms biodynamically (Co/b certification) on the Heiligenstein — an approach that the estate argues produces wines with more precise terroir expression than conventional farming, particularly in a site as geologically distinctive as this one.

The 2015 Austrian vintage was warm and generous — producing Rieslings with more immediate richness than cool years, but with enough underlying acidity to age magnificently. Nearly a decade on, the best 2015s are peaking.

name: Riesling, Ried Heiligenstein, Kamptal DAC — Magnum
tableside: The 2021 Hirsch Heiligenstein in magnum — the more recent vintage alongside the 2015. Still relatively young and tightly wound, but the quality is unmistakable. For guests who want to invest in something they can cellar, or for a table celebrating with a wine that will still be extraordinary in ten years.
story: Having both the 2015 and 2021 Hirsch Heiligenstein magnums on the list is an unusual opportunity — two very different vintages of the same wine in the same format. The 2015 is generous, mature, and complex; the 2021 is more reserved and tightly wound, still in its opening phase. The 2021 Austrian vintage was cool and elegant, producing wines of exceptional acid retention and long aging potential.
trivia: Having two vintages of the same magnum — 2015 and 2021 — is one of the more educational pairing opportunities on the list. It's essentially a mini-vertical in the most celebration-worthy format available.
The 2021 Austrian vintage was cooler than 2020 or 2019, producing wines with better natural acidity and more linear structure — they'll need more time than the 2015 to open fully.

Magnums of the Heiligenstein are produced in very small quantities — Hirsch's production from this vineyard is already limited, and the format allocation is tiny. They are among the most sought-after large-format Austrian wines available.

name: Sauvignon Blanc 'Pruh,' Ciringa, Slovenia — 3 Liter
tableside: A 3-liter of Tement's flagship single-vineyard Sauvignon Blanc from their Slovenian estate — now six years old and drinking magnificently. The Pruh is one of the great Sauvignon Blancs of the world: rich, mineral, and built for aging in a way that most Sauvignon Blanc never achieves. In a 3-liter, it's a celebration bottle that will anchor a dinner.
story: Tement's Ciringa estate sits across the border in Slovenia, on the same ridge as their Austrian Zieregg vineyard — essentially the same terroir divided by a political boundary. The Pruh is their top Slovenian expression: old vines, volcanic soils, and winemaking precision that produces Sauvignon Blanc of extraordinary concentration and complexity. In a 3-liter, the wine's aging potential is maximized further.
trivia: The Slovenia-Austria border runs through some of Styria's best Sauvignon Blanc vineyards — the same ridge, the same soils, the same microclimate. The political division is real; the terroir division is not.

3-liter bottles (also called double magnums or Jeroboams in some regions) age wine even more slowly than magnums — the ratio of wine to oxygen is further reduced, producing exceptionally long-lived bottles.
At $580, this is the most expensive white wine on the list. It reflects the combination of format size, vineyard quality, and aging potential — not just the volume of wine.

name: Sauvignon Blanc 'Pruh,' Ciringa, Slovenia — 3 Liter
tableside: The 2019 Pruh in 3-liter — slightly younger and more reserved than the 2018, with a more linear, tightly wound structure. Both are extraordinary. The 2018 is ready now; the 2019 has more future ahead of it. For a table that wants to invest in something to take home, or for a long dinner that deserves a bottle of this caliber.
story: Having both the 2018 and 2019 Pruh in 3-liter format is a remarkable opportunity — two consecutive vintages of the same wine in the most impressive format available. The 2019 Styrian vintage was cooler than 2018, producing wines with more precise acidity and longer aging potential. The two bottles make for one of the most interesting vintage comparison conversations on the entire list.
trivia: The 2019 Austrian growing season was characterized by cool nights that preserved natural acidity — particularly valuable in a variety like Sauvignon Blanc, which can become flabby in warm years.
Having two consecutive vintages of the Pruh in 3-liter format is almost certainly unique — it requires a cellar program of exceptional depth and foresight to maintain both.

Tement's H1 (the standard 750ml Pruh in the Sauvignon Blanc section of this list) and these 3-liter formats come from the same vineyard — having all three on the list allows comparison of both vintage and format.

name: Zweigelt Reserve
tableside: Entry-level Zweigelt from Burgenland — juicy red fruit, easy tannins, good acidity. The kind of wine that works with almost everything. A safe recommendation for guests who want to try Austrian red without committing to something heavier.
story: Erich Sattler farms in southern Burgenland, one of Austria's most important red wine regions. The Reserve designation means extra aging and fruit selection, giving the wine more concentration than the standard bottling. Zweigelt is Austria's most widely planted red variety — a crossing of Blaufränkisch and St. Laurent developed in the early 20th century.
trivia: Zweigelt was bred in 1922 by Dr. Fritz Zweigelt at the Klosterneuburg Agricultural Institute — a cross of Blaufränkisch and Sankt Laurent that became Austria's most planted red variety, and one of the few grapes named after its creator.
Burgenland's warm Pannonian climate makes it Austria's warmest and driest wine region, ideal for red varieties.
Austria was considered a white-wine country by most drinkers until the 1990s, when Burgenland producers began proving the region could make serious reds.

name: Blaufränkisch, Ried Kulm
tableside: Heidi Schröck is one of Burgenland's most respected producers, better known for her whites and natural wines, but her Blaufränkisch is equally serious. The Ried Kulm site gives this wine more structure and mineral depth than a straightforward entry-level Burgenland red. For guests curious about Austrian red who want something with genuine complexity.
story: Schröck is based in Rust — the famous village on the western shore of Lake Neusiedl, known for both its red wines and its legendary Ausbruch dessert wines. She was among the first generation of producers who elevated Burgenland's reputation internationally. The Ried Kulm is a single-vineyard expression from a site with particularly mineral-rich soils.
trivia: Rust is famous for its storks — hundreds nest on chimney tops every summer, giving the village an almost mythological character that belies its serious wine credentials.
Lake Neusiedl is one of Europe's largest shallow lakes, never deeper than about 1.8 meters, and its microclimate moderates temperatures in ways that affect every vineyard on its shores.
Heidi Schröck was one of the first female producers in Austria to achieve international recognition — her natural wines drew attention to Burgenland before the region's reds became fashionable.

name: Merlot/Blaufränkisch/Zweigelt 'Hill 1,' Leithaberg DAC
tableside: The flagship red from Hillinger — a three-grape blend that balances Blaufränkisch elegance with Merlot roundness and Zweigelt freshness. Full-bodied, oaked, long finish. This is Hillinger making a statement about what Burgenland can do.
story: Leo Hillinger built one of the most visible wine brands in Austria from a small family estate in Jois on the northern shore of Lake Neusiedl. His dramatic hillside winery is as well-known in Austria as his wines. Hill 1 is the top of his red wine pyramid: a blend designed to compete with serious international benchmarks.
trivia: Hillinger's winery was designed to be integrated into the hillside — from the air it's barely visible, the underground cellar naturally temperature-controlled by the surrounding earth.
The Leithaberg DAC appellation was approved in 2009 and came into force in 2010, focused specifically on Blaufränkisch and Pinot Blanc from the limestone and schist soils of the Leitha Mountains.
Leo Hillinger was instrumental in modernizing the image of Austrian wine internationally, investing heavily in design and marketing when most producers focused solely on the wine.

name: Blaufränkisch Reserve, Eisenberg DAC
tableside: Eisenberg is the southernmost Blaufränkisch appellation in Burgenland, and the wines tend to be darker and more mineral than those from the lake regions to the north. This Jalits Reserve is structured and serious — a cellar wine in the best sense. For guests who know Barolo, this is the conversation to have.
story: The Eisenberg DAC appellation was created to protect the reputation of Blaufränkisch from this compact hill at the southern end of Burgenland. The iron-rich basalt soils produce wines with a distinct metallic, almost volcanic mineral character quite different from the more fruit-forward lake-region examples. Jalits is a small family producer making wines that punch well above their price.
trivia: Eisenberg translates directly as 'iron mountain' — the soils are genuinely iron-rich basalt and volcanic rock, and you can often taste it as a distinct metallic or blood-orange note.

The Eisenberg DAC was established in the late 2000s, one of Austria's more recent appellations, created to give legal identity to wines that a small group of producers were already making with conviction.
Blaufränkisch from Eisenberg is aged longer than most Austrian reds — the structure requires time to integrate, and the best bottles improve for 15-20 years.

name: Blaufränkisch 'Clemens,' Eisenberg DAC
tableside: One of the benchmark Blaufränkisch producers in Austria. The 'Clemens' is the flagship — named for a family member, from old vines, built for the long haul. If a table wants something serious and isn't afraid of structure, this is the bottle.
story: The Krutzler family has been making Blaufränkisch in Eisenberg for three generations and is widely considered the reference producer for what this appellation can achieve. The Clemens comes from the estate's oldest vines on the steepest slopes — a site that produces concentrated, mineral-driven wine that needs years to open. At this price it's one of the best value-for-quality propositions for serious Blaufränkisch on the list.
trivia: In blind tastings, Krutzler Blaufränkisch has been compared favorably to serious Barolo and Northern Rhône Syrah — a structural depth most casual drinkers don't expect from Austria.
Blaufränkisch is the same grape as Lemberger (Germany and Washington State) and Kékfrankos (Hungary) — all different names for a variety producing some of Central Europe's most age-worthy reds.
The Krutzler estate sits near an ancient Roman trade route through southern Burgenland — archaeological finds of Roman-era winemaking equipment have been made nearby.

name: Blaufränkisch, Ried Goldberg, Leithaberg DAC
tableside: The Grand Cru equivalent of Prieler's Blaufränkisch lineup — single vineyard, old vines, limestone soils. This is Blaufränkisch at its most aristocratic. For the guest who thinks Austrian red is just a curiosity, this bottle changes the conversation completely.
story: Prieler is based in Schützen am Gebirge on the western edge of Lake Neusiedl, where soils transition from lake alluvium to the limestone and schist of the Leitha Mountains. The Goldberg is their most prestigious single vineyard — steep slopes, naturally low yields, limestone bedrock. Silvia Prieler, who runs the estate, is one of Austria's most important winemakers.
trivia: Silvia Prieler took over from her father in the late 1990s and is widely credited with pushing the estate from regional to international recognition.
The Ried Goldberg has been planted with Blaufränkisch for at least 80 years — old enough that the vine roots reach deep into limestone bedrock.
Prieler is one of the few Austrian estates explicitly documenting single-vineyard terroir differences through annual comparison bottlings — essentially the Burgundy cru approach.

name: Blaufränkisch, Ried Pratschweingarten, Leithaberg DAC
tableside: The younger-sibling bottling to the Goldberg — same producer, same philosophy, slightly more clay in the soil and a bit more accessibility in its youth. A great entry point into serious Prieler Blaufränkisch without the patience requirement.
story: The Pratschweingarten is a lower-altitude vineyard than the Goldberg, with more clay in the soil and slightly warmer exposition. The wine opens up faster and offers more immediate red fruit — still mineral and structured, but approachable within a few years. The 2018 vintage was warm and dry, producing ripe, concentrated wines across the region.
trivia: Pratschweingarten and Goldberg sit less than half a kilometer apart, yet the wines are noticeably different — a textbook example of how small soil variations translate into the glass.
The 2018 vintage in Burgenland was one of the driest in decades, with some producers reporting yields down by a third.
The name 'Pratschweingarten' is an old Burgenland dialect term referring to a specific soil type — the kind of hyper-local vineyard naming that echoes Burgundy's climat system.

name: Blaufränkisch, Ried Pratschweingarten, Leithaberg DAC
tableside: The 2019 Pratschweingarten — a notch cooler than the 2018, with more red fruit and aromatic lift. More Burgundian in style. For guests who want structure without weight, this vintage is the better choice.
story: The 2019 vintage was cooler and later than 2018, producing wines with better acid retention and more aromatic precision. Having both the 2018 and 2019 on the list gives servers the opportunity to discuss vintage character with interested guests — same vineyard, different weather.
trivia: The 2019 Pratschweingarten is a perfect staff exercise in vintage character: broader and riper in 2018, more precise and lifted in 2019.
Leithaberg DAC requires minimum aging before release — 18 months standard, 30 months for Reserve designations.
The word 'Blaufränkisch' translates roughly as 'Blue Frankish' — the 'Frankish' part is thought to reference historical French grape varieties as markers of quality.

name: Blaufränkisch, Ried Pratschweingarten, Leithaberg DAC
tableside: Three vintages of the same vineyard on one list is unusual — use it. The 2020 sits between the richness of 2018 and the precision of 2019: a balanced vintage that's approachable now and will age gracefully. A good choice for guests who want to enjoy it tonight.
story: Having three consecutive vintages of the Pratschweingarten allows a real conversation about how terroir expresses itself across different growing seasons — essentially a mini-vertical from a single producer at a single price. The 2020 was a mixed vintage across Austria but quite good in the Leitha Mountains, where cooler nights preserved acidity.
trivia: Three vintages of one vineyard from one producer is effectively a micro-vertical — a tasting format usually requiring advance planning at high-end wine events.
The Leitha Mountains form the natural boundary between Burgenland and Lower Austria. The eastern slopes face the warming Pannonian influence; the western slopes are cooler and more mineral.
Prieler has planted cover crops between vine rows for decades — a practice that reduces erosion, adds organic matter, and encourages biodiversity.

name: Blaufränkisch, Göttlesbrunn DAC
tableside: Certified organic Blaufränkisch from Carnuntum — lighter-bodied than the Burgenland examples, with more freshness and crunch. Glatzer is one of the benchmark producers for the appellation. For tables wanting to explore Austrian red without committing to something more structured.
story: Walter Glatzer runs his family estate in Göttlesbrunn, one of the villages defining the Carnuntum wine region in Lower Austria. Carnuntum is a Roman name — a major garrison town on the Danube frontier — and its gravel, loess, and clay soils give the wines a different, lighter character from Burgenland Blaufränkisch.
trivia: Carnuntum was once one of the most important Roman cities in the western empire — home to up to 50,000 people, with amphitheaters and palaces whose remains still define the landscape.
The Carnuntum DAC appellation was established in 2019, recognizing the distinct terroir between Vienna and the Slovak border.
Certified biodynamic viticulture (Co/b) requires moon-based planting calendars, horn manure preparations, and documented organic farming practices verified by an independent body.

name: Zweigelt 'Dornenvogel,' Göttlesbrunn DAC
tableside: The 2021 Dornenvogel — marginally more concentrated than the 2020, with a cooler-vintage brightness. Same light, food-friendly style. For tables who enjoy the 2020 and want to try the newer vintage.
story: The 2021 vintage in Carnuntum was cooler than 2020, retaining better natural acidity. Having both on the list gives an unusual opportunity to compare vintages within an affordable bottling — and to show guests how much weather changes a wine without changing the winemaker's approach.
trivia: Carnuntum is only about 30 kilometers east of Vienna — one of Europe's few major wine regions within commuting distance of a capital city.
Glatzer's biodynamic certification requires detailed record-keeping including documentation of lunar cycles, weather events, and every input applied to the land.
Both the 2020 and 2021 Dornenvogels being on the list simultaneously is a good opportunity to discuss vintage differences without requiring a large financial commitment from the guest.

name: Zweigelt/Merlot/Cab Sauv 'Anna-Christina,' Göttlesbrunn 1ÖTW
tableside: A serious Austrian red blend from one of Carnuntum's best producers. Zweigelt gives the cherry fruit, Merlot adds body and roundness, Cab Sauvignon provides the structure and aging potential. Oaked and full-bodied — a crowd-pleasing style for guests who like California red but want something more interesting.
story: The Netzl family farms some of Göttlesbrunn's best sites, including several classified as 1ÖTW — the country's top vineyard classification. Anna-Christina is named for a family member and is the estate's flagship red: an international blend that uses Carnuntum fruit to compete with Napa and Bordeaux on familiar terms.
trivia: The 1ÖTW classification — roughly equivalent to Burgundy's Premier and Grand Cru — is administered by the Österreichische Traditionsweingüter, an association of top Austrian estates that has been classifying vineyard sites since the early 1990s.
Netzl's vineyards in Göttlesbrunn are planted on ancient gravel terraces — remnants of the prehistoric Danube delta — that drain quickly and force vines to root deeply.
Cabernet Sauvignon rarely headlines in Austria, but Carnuntum's warm continental climate is actually well-suited to it.

name: Pinot Noir, Langenlois
tableside: Bründlmayer making Pinot Noir — the same producer whose Grüner Veltliner is the backbone of the white wine list. Lighter and more perfumed than Oregon or California, closer to a village Burgundy in style. For guests who love Pinot and want to try an Austrian take on it.
story: Willi Bründlmayer is most famous for white wines, but he's been quietly making excellent Pinot Noir for decades. The Langenlois Pinot tends toward the lighter, more aromatic end of the spectrum: think Volnay rather than Chambolle, with a distinct Austrian mineral character running through it.
trivia: Bründlmayer's winery is one of the oldest continuously operating estates in the Kamptal — records of winemaking on the property go back to the 18th century.
The Kamptal's cooler nights and significant diurnal temperature variation give Pinot Noir the acidity and freshness the grape needs to thrive.
The same Grüner and Riesling plots adjacent to the Pinot Noir blocks give the winery a unique ability to study how identical terroir expresses itself across completely different grape varieties.

name: Pinot Noir, Langenlois
tableside: The 2020 Bründlmayer Pinot — a warmer vintage than 2019, more red fruit weight but still the characteristic Kamptal precision. Same price as the 2019; a great comparison conversation for guests interested in vintage.
story: The 2020 vintage was warm and dry, producing wines with higher alcohol and riper fruit. The Bründlmayer Pinot benefits from this — the fruit is more plush and immediately appealing while retaining the mineral backbone that defines all wines from this estate.
trivia: Having both the 2019 and 2020 on the list simultaneously is a rare opportunity to discuss vintage differences in Austrian Pinot at a single price point.
The Kamptal Pinot Noir harvest runs about two weeks later than the whites — allowing skins to develop more color and tannin without overripening.
Austrian Pinot Noir exports have grown significantly in the past decade, driven partly by Burgundy-obsessed collectors who discover it at comparative tastings.

name: Pinot Noir 'Holzspur,' Tattendorf
tableside: 'Holzspur' means 'wood track' — a reference to the significant oak influence. This is Austrian Pinot Noir at its most ambitious: rich, structured, and built for the cellar. For guests who love serious Pinot and are open to something between Burgundy and Napa.
story: Johanneshof Reinisch is based in Tattendorf in the Thermenregion — a wine region south of Vienna historically famous for its Pinot Noir. The Thermenregion's clay-limestone soils and warm, dry climate produce bigger, more structured Pinot than the cooler regions to the north. Holzspur is fermented and aged in a combination of large and small French oak.
trivia: The Thermenregion takes its name from the famous thermal springs at Baden bei Wien, a spa town and favorite retreat of the Viennese aristocracy in the 19th century.
'Holzspur' (wood track) references old logging trails through the Thermenregion's forests — a poetic name gesturing at both the oak aging and the region's pre-wine history.
Reinisch has been farming biodynamically for years — their vineyard practices emphasize minimal chemical intervention and soil health.

name: 'Dial Tone,' Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: A newer-label, affordable Santa Barbara Pinot from a serious team. Sta. Rita Hills is one of California's coolest Pinot regions — the Pacific fog rolls in every afternoon and slows ripening to something closer to Burgundy's pace. Fresh, light, and well-priced.
story: Babs is a small-production label focused on cool-climate Santa Barbara fruit. Sta. Rita Hills was only officially recognized as its own AVA in 2001, but it's already producing some of California's most compelling Pinot Noir. The cool temperatures and Pacific influence give the wines a brightness and finesse unusual for California.
trivia: The Santa Rita Hills AVA runs east-west, perpendicular to the coast — an unusual orientation that funnels cold Pacific air directly from the ocean into the valley.
The region's fame was boosted significantly by the film Sideways (2004), which caused a surge of interest in both Pinot Noir and Santa Barbara wine country.
Babs takes its name from a family nickname — many of California's most interesting small producers have personal, story-driven origins.

producer: Dave Phinney / Our Lady of Guadalupe
name: Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: Dave Phinney made his name with The Prisoner — one of California's most commercially successful cult wines. This Sta. Rita Hills Pinot is a different animal: focused, site-specific, and built for dinner rather than the lifestyle brand. For guests who know Phinney's name and want to see his serious side.
story: Phinney's Our Lady of Guadalupe project draws from established Sta. Rita Hills vineyards that have defined the appellation's reputation. The wine is rich by Sta. Rita Hills standards but still maintains the region's characteristic acidity.
trivia: Dave Phinney founded The Prisoner Wine Company in 2000 and sold it to Constellation Brands in 2010. Constellation then sold The Prisoner brand in 2016 for a reported $285 million — one of the most valuable wine brand sales in American history.

The Our Lady of Guadalupe project is inspired partly by the iconography of Mexican culture in California winemaking — a reference to the many workers who've been central to the California wine industry.
Sta. Rita Hills' climate is so consistently cool that producers sometimes harvest in late October or November — weeks after most of Napa is done.

name: Sunny View Vineyard, Russian River Valley
tableside: A 2016 Russian River Pinot — now approaching a decade of age. Russian River is Sonoma's fog belt: cooler than most of Napa, closer to the ocean. The fruit has receded, the earth and spice have come forward. Good choice for guests who enjoy aged Pinot.
story: The Calling sources from established Russian River Valley vineyards, where afternoon Pacific fog creates one of California's coolest wine climates. The 2016 vintage was widely considered one of the best of the decade — moderate temperatures, good hang time, and wines built for aging.
trivia: Russian River Valley Pinot Noir commands some of the highest prices per ton of any California appellation — reflecting low yields, limited land, and intense demand.
The Petaluma Gap, which channels Pacific air into Russian River Valley, was officially designated its own AVA in 2017.
The 2016 vintage is consistently rated among the best of the decade. A 2016 Russian River at the table now is showing genuine secondary complexity.

name: Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: If you're going to pour one California Pinot that explains why the state has become a reference point, make it Domaine de la Côte. Sashi Moorman and Rajat Parr built these vineyards with a Burgundian philosophy — low yields, old vines, minimal intervention. For Burgundy lovers who are open-minded about California.
story: Sashi Moorman and Rajat Parr founded Domaine de la Côte to prove that Santa Barbara could produce wines with the precision and site-specificity of the Côte d'Or. They planted vineyards in limestone-heavy Lompoc soils, kept yields brutally low, and made wine with minimal intervention. The result has been compared favorably to Chambolle-Musigny.
trivia: Rajat Parr is one of the most decorated sommeliers in America, having run wine programs at San Francisco's top restaurants before co-founding Domaine de la Côte.
At 12.5% alcohol, this is unusually low for California Pinot — a deliberate choice to preserve freshness and increase aging potential.
The estate uses organic farming with cover crops and no synthetic inputs. Exceptional limestone drainage means naturally stressed vines producing small, concentrated berries.

name: Bloom's Field, Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: Bloom's Field is Domaine de la Côte's flagship single-vineyard expression — the one that established the estate's reputation. The 2021 is ethereal: barely any color, enormous aromatic complexity, and a finish that won't quit. The Burgundy comparison earns its keep here.
story: Bloom's Field sits on a north-facing slope in Lompoc that receives less direct sun than most California sites. Combined with the Pacific fog, this keeps temperatures low enough to preserve the delicacy in the fruit. The wine is famous for its translucent color and extraordinary aromatic complexity.
trivia: Bloom's Field was featured in early press coverage of Domaine de la Côte and is widely credited with changing how critics think about California Pinot's potential.
North-facing slopes in California — unlike in Burgundy, where they're sometimes avoided — help maintain freshness that would otherwise be lost in the strong sun.
Multiple vintages of Bloom's Field are on the list simultaneously — a rare opportunity to taste the same vineyard across different years.

name: Bloom's Field, Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: The 2022 Bloom's Field — essentially the same wine from a slightly warmer vintage, adding a touch more concentration without losing the delicacy. For guests who've had the 2021 and want a comparison.
story: The 2022 vintage in Santa Barbara was slightly warmer and drier than 2021, producing wines with a fractionally richer texture while retaining the site's characteristic mineral precision. The two vintages make for an excellent side-by-side discussion.
trivia: Domaine de la Côte's multiple Bloom's Field vintages on one list is unusual — most restaurants stock only one vintage of any wine, making this an implicit invitation to compare.
The name 'Bloom's Field' refers to the wildflower meadows that originally covered the site before vines were planted — flowers still bloom between vine rows each spring.
California wine labels must include the vintage year if 95% or more of grapes come from that harvest — stricter than the EU's 85% minimum.

name: Bloom's Field, Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: The youngest Bloom's Field on the list — vibrant and immediate. The 2023 is the most accessible of the three vintages right now, and the best value. For guests discovering Domaine de la Côte for the first time.
story: The 2023 vintage in Santa Barbara was characterized by consistent, moderate temperatures and no extreme heat events — producing wines with natural freshness and good aging potential. The 2023 is expressive right now, making it the natural starting point for Domaine de la Côte newcomers.
trivia: The 2023 harvest ran ahead of schedule — an early spring pushed budbreak and harvest arrived before typical autumn fog windows, giving winemakers clean, healthy fruit with excellent acidity.
The price difference between the 2021 and 2023 reflects both bottle age (older vintages appreciate) and vintage production size.
Three consecutive Bloom's Field vintages may make this the most extensively represented single vineyard on the entire list.

name: Memorious, Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: The 'other' Domaine de la Côte single vineyard. Where Bloom's Field is floral and chalk-driven, Memorious is earthier, darker, and more savory. Named for a Borges story — appropriate, since it takes time to reveal itself. For guests wanting more than one Domaine de la Côte to compare.
story: 'Memorious' is taken from Jorge Luis Borges's short story about a man cursed with perfect, infinite memory. The wine's name refers to the idea that great terroir remembers and expresses its past, vintage after vintage. Memorious comes from a different plot than Bloom's Field — slightly different soil and exposition, which accounts for its earthier, darker character.
trivia: Borges's 'Funes el memorioso' was published in 1942 and is considered one of the great short fictions of the 20th century — a meditation on memory, identity, and the limits of perception.
Domaine de la Côte's named bottlings are all inspired by literature or philosophy — a distinctive approach in an industry that trends toward simpler naming.
Having both the 2021 and 2023 Memorious available gives a genuine opportunity to demonstrate how the same site evolves across vintages.

name: Memorious, Lompoc, Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: The 2023 Memorious — all the savory, earthy complexity of the 2021, with more fresh fruit to make it drinkable now. Great comparison to Bloom's Field if guests want to explore the estate's different sites.
story: Having both the 2021 and 2023 Memorious available gives a genuine opportunity to demonstrate vintage evolution within a single site — the 2021 shows more tertiary development, the 2023 leads with primary fruit. Neither is better; they're at different stages.
trivia: Domaine de la Côte ages wines in large-format French oak (demi-muids and foudres) plus smaller barriques, integrating oak without dominating the terroir expression.
The 2021 and 2023 Memorious offer a snapshot of what two different growing seasons produce from the same plot — a rare teaching opportunity on a single wine list.
Lompoc, California — the city nearest to the estate's vineyards — was historically known as the 'Flower Seed Capital of the World' before wine became its most famous industry.

name: Wester Ranch, Russian River Valley
tableside: DuMOL is a Russian River specialist whose wines have been reference points for California Pinot for decades. Wester Ranch is one of their top single-vineyard expressions — richer and more oak-influenced than lighter California styles, but with genuine complexity underneath. For guests who like full-bodied reds but want to explore Pinot.
story: DuMOL was one of the early proponents of serious, age-worthy Russian River Valley Pinot Noir. Wester Ranch produces fruit with exceptional concentration — low yields, cool temperatures, and established vines translate into deeply colored, richly textured wine that can be cellared for a decade or more.
trivia: Russian River Valley's Goldridge sandy loam soils are considered ideal for Pinot Noir — the rapid drainage forces vine roots deep, reducing yields and concentrating flavor.
The 2020 vintage in California was complicated by wildfire smoke in some regions, but Russian River Valley largely escaped the worst effects.
DuMOL ferments in small open-top fermenters, using a combination of whole cluster and destemmed fruit depending on the vintage — a practice that adds complexity and spice.

name: Wester Reach, Russian River Valley
tableside: The 2021 DuMOL Wester — a slightly cooler vintage than 2020, with better natural acidity and more immediate approachability. For guests who want DuMOL without waiting years.
story: The 2021 vintage in Russian River Valley was cooler and more classic than 2020, producing wines with better acid retention. The Wester Reach in 2021 shows DuMOL with a moderate growing season: still richly textured, still deeply colored, but with a freshness that makes it more versatile at the table.
trivia: Having two consecutive DuMOL vintages allows comparison of both vintage character and the subtle difference between 'Wester Ranch' and 'Wester Reach' designations.

Russian River Valley's designation as a distinct AVA in 1983 was partly the result of advocacy by winemakers who recognized that the valley's fog belt produced fundamentally different wines from the rest of Sonoma.
DuMOL's name comes from the founders' first names — du for Duncan, mol for Molly.

name: 'Valliant,' Central Coast
tableside: Certified organic and well-priced for the quality. Eden Rift is a historical San Benito County vineyard — one of California's oldest planted Pinot sites, with vines that date back generations. Great glass pour for guests wanting something interesting without a bottle commitment.
story: Eden Rift farms what is believed to be the oldest continuously cultivated Pinot Noir vineyard in California — the Tallent Vineyard in San Benito County, planted in the 1920s. Old roots, organic farming, and an often-overlooked cool climate produce wines that are genuinely distinctive.
trivia: San Benito County is one of California's smallest and least-known wine appellations, despite being home to some of the state's oldest vines.
Eden Rift's old-vine Pinot has been cited in academic studies on vine age and wine quality — long root systems appear to buffer wines against vintage variation in ways younger plantings can't.
The 'Valliant' name references the vineyard's French historical ownership — a nod to the tradition of naming vineyards after family names or local landmarks.

name: 'Côteaux Nobles,' Fort Ross-Seaview
tableside: Luc Morlet is a Frenchman who trained in Champagne and Bordeaux before coming to California. He makes wine the way an obsessive Frenchman makes wine — extreme vineyard management, tiny yields, near-perfect execution. Fort Ross-Seaview Pinot at its most ambitious. Worth the conversation.
story: Luc Morlet was born and trained in France — his family owns a Champagne house — before moving to California. The Côteaux Nobles comes from Fort Ross-Seaview, an AVA perched on the coastal ridgeline above the Pacific at elevations of 1,200–2,200 feet. The combination of altitude, fog, and thin rocky soils produces Pinot of extraordinary concentration and mineral intensity.
trivia: Fort Ross-Seaview was designated as a separate AVA in 2011, carved out of the broader Sonoma Coast appellation to distinguish higher-elevation coastal sites.
Morlet's Champagne family background informs everything about how he makes wine — his obsession with acidity and his belief that great wine is made in the vineyard first.
At $330, this is one of the most expensive Pinot Noirs on the list. The Côteaux Nobles has been reviewed at 97+ points by multiple major publications.

name: Pomarium Estate, West Sonoma Coast
tableside: Peay is a family estate on the extreme western Sonoma Coast — essentially cliff-hanging above the Pacific. Constant wind, thick fog, very cool temperatures. The Pinot that comes out is some of California's most elegant. For guests who want Burgundy-style Pinot from California.
story: The Peay family has been farming the extreme western Sonoma Coast since the late 1990s. The Pomarium vineyard sits at around 1,000 feet elevation, directly exposed to the Pacific. Yields are tiny, the growing season is long and cool, and the wines consistently punch above their price.
trivia: Peay's location is so windy and cold that the growing season is among the longest in California — often stretching into late November, allowing complex flavors to develop without sugar accumulation.
The name 'Pomarium' comes from the Latin word for orchard — a reference to the apple orchards that originally occupied the land before vines were planted.
Winemaker Vanessa Wong has won multiple awards and is cited by critics as one of California's finest winemakers working in Pinot Noir.

name: 'Bodega,' Freestone-Occidental
tableside: The most ambitious wine from Carlo and Dante Mondavi — grandsons of Robert Mondavi, building their own legacy with extreme West Sonoma Coast Pinot. The Bodega vineyard is right at the fog line above the Pacific. Dense, mineral, and unmistakably serious.
story: Carlo and Dante Mondavi founded RAEN with the explicit goal of making California Pinot that could be discussed in the same breath as top Burgundy. They farm the western Sonoma Coast with extreme care — low yields, organic practices, minimal intervention — and their results have drawn comparisons to Chambolle and Fort Ross.
trivia: Carlo and Dante Mondavi are the grandsons of Robert Mondavi, who arguably did more to elevate California wine's international reputation than anyone else.

RAEN stands for Research in Agriculture and Enology Naturally — but the spelling also nods to rain, the maritime moisture that defines their coastal vineyards. The name captures both the scientific rigor and the elemental conditions that shape every bottle.
The Freestone-Occidental area was designated its own AVA in 2011, recognizing the distinctive cooling influence of the Bodega Bay coastal access point.

name: 'Royal St. Robert,' Sonoma Coast
tableside: The more accessible RAEN — Royal St. Robert is the one guests can open tonight and enjoy fully. Same philosophy, broader sourcing, more immediate pleasure. Great introduction to the estate before committing to the Bodega.
story: Royal St. Robert is RAEN's appellation-level Sonoma Coast expression — a blend from multiple coastal vineyards rather than a single site. It mirrors what Burgundy producers call their 'village' wine: less site-specific than the Bodega, but more immediately accessible and still squarely in the house style.
trivia: RAEN uses the same farming philosophy across all their wines — the only difference between Royal St. Robert and the Bodega is vineyard source, not winemaking ambition.
The Sonoma Coast AVA covers an enormous geographic area, which is why sub-appellations like Freestone-Occidental were created to provide more geographic precision.
The name 'Royal St. Robert' references the town of Occidental, California, which sits in a redwood forest near RAEN's vineyards.

name: 'Southing,' Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: Sea Smoke is one of the iconic names in California Pinot. Their estate vineyard in Sta. Rita Hills sits at the bend in the valley where the Pacific fog is most intense — they named the estate after the vapor that rises from the ocean every morning. Rich, full-bodied, and crowd-pleasing in the best sense.
story: Sea Smoke planted their estate vineyard in 1999 with the explicit intention of making California's finest Pinot Noir. They own the land outright and farm with extreme care. The Southing is one of two flagship expressions — made from southern-facing blocks that receive more sun and produce richer, more concentrated fruit.
trivia: Sea Smoke's estate is entirely surrounded by other top Sta. Rita Hills producers — Melville, Brewer-Clifton, and Domaine de la Côte are all nearby.
'Sea Smoke' refers to the morning fog that rolls off the Pacific and looks like smoke from the hillside.
Sea Smoke holds wines back for extended aging before release — a practice more common in Burgundy and Barolo than in California.

name: 'Ten,' Sta. Rita Hills
tableside: Sea Smoke 'Ten' — ten clones blended into one wine. More structured and age-worthy than the Southing; a bit less immediately approachable. For guests who want to lay something down, or who like their Pinot as big as possible.
story: The Ten is named for the ten Pinot Noir clones that go into it. Using multiple clonal selections — each ripening slightly differently — is a traditional Burgundian approach to adding complexity. The Ten is typically the more powerful and age-worthy of Sea Smoke's two flagship wines.
trivia: Pinot Noir has more approved clones than almost any other grape variety — over 1,000 have been identified worldwide.
Sea Smoke sells almost entirely direct-to-consumer through their mailing list, making any restaurant allocation genuinely coveted.
The 2021 vintage was particularly good for Sta. Rita Hills — a long, cool season with no heat events produced wines with both concentration and regional freshness.

name: Torie's Vineyard, Los Carneros
tableside: A Carneros Pinot from The Vice — the same label making one of the more interesting Cabs on the list. Carneros straddles Napa and Sonoma and catches Pacific fog off the bay, making it one of California's cooler appellations. Good value, easy to drink.
story: Los Carneros sits at the cool southern end of both Napa and Sonoma valleys, where San Pablo Bay creates a persistent wind and fog that moderates temperatures significantly. It's historically Pinot Noir and Chardonnay territory. The Vice's Torie's Vineyard is named for a family connection.
trivia: 'Carneros' means 'rams' in Spanish — a reference to the sheep ranching that occupied the land before wine arrived.
Carneros was the first AVA in California that straddled two counties, recognizing that the bay's cooling influence ignores county boundaries.
The Vice makes both Pinot Noir and Cabernet Sauvignon, reflecting a range of sourcing across California's climatic spectrum.

name: Bob's Ranch, Sonoma Coast
tableside: Certified sustainable, 2018 vintage — now well-aged and showing the tertiary complexity that comes with time. Bob's Ranch is one of Walt's signature single-vineyard expressions. A good choice for guests who want something that's had time to evolve.
story: Walt Family Winery sources from established Sonoma Coast vineyard sites. Bob's Ranch is named for the longtime ranch manager who has tended the site for decades. The 2018 is now six years old, and secondary characters — dried herbs, earth, subtle game — are beginning to emerge nicely.
trivia: The 2018 Sonoma Coast vintage was warm and has aged gracefully — initially compared to the legendary 2013, and time has largely confirmed that comparison.
'Sustainable' (CS) means the vineyard meets requirements around water use, energy, ecosystem protection, and community — not as strict as organic, but covering a broader range of practices.
Kathryn Hall, Walt's founder, also runs HALL Wines in Napa Valley — an unusual dual-label operation across two serious California wine regions.

name: 1er Cru, Volnay
tableside: Premier Cru Volnay — one of Burgundy's most beloved villages for elegant Pinot. Volnay wines are famous for their perfume and silkiness. The 2017 is now nine years old and drinking beautifully.
story: Volnay is a Côte de Beaune village whose Premier Cru vineyards are among Burgundy's most sought-after for fragrance and structure. The 2017 vintage was excellent in the Côte d'Or — medium concentration, great aging potential, drinking well now.
trivia: Volnay produces only red wine — unlike nearby Meursault, which produces almost exclusively white. The two villages share a commune boundary, and some Meursault Premier Crus planted with red grapes are actually labeled Volnay.
There are 26 Premier Cru vineyards in Volnay alone.
Burgundy's 2017 vintage was initially overshadowed by the famous 2015 and 2016, but has proven itself over time as a consistent, elegant year with surprisingly good aging potential.

name: Vosne-Romanée
tableside: Vosne-Romanée contains Romanée-Conti, La Tâche, and Richebourg — the most famous and expensive Pinot Noir vineyards on earth. Grivot's village-level wine gives a genuine sense of what makes Vosne special, at a fraction of the Premier and Grand Cru prices.
story: Étienne Grivot is one of the most respected producers in Burgundy — his family has been farming in Vosne-Romanée for generations. The 2022 vintage was warm and produced wines with more immediate appeal than cool-climate years, but with enough structure to age gracefully.
trivia: Romanée-Conti is just 1.8 hectares — the entire vineyard produces fewer than 6,000 bottles per year, and a single bottle at auction regularly sells for $10,000–30,000.
Vosne-Romanée has more Grand Cru vineyards per square kilometer than almost any other Burgundy village.
The 2022 Burgundy vintage is widely considered one of the best of the 21st century — warm and ripe, but with surprisingly good acidity retention.

name: 'Les Charmois,' Nuits-Saint-Georges
tableside: Nuits-Saint-Georges from Grivot — a touch more structured and earthy than the Vosne. The village is known for wines with more tannin and rustic character than Chambolle or Volnay. Good for guests who want a bit more grip alongside their food.
story: Nuits-Saint-Georges is the southernmost major village of the Côte de Nuits and the largest producer of red Burgundy by volume. Its soils are deeper and more varied, producing wines that tend toward earthiness and structure. Les Charmois is a specific vineyard that Grivot farms with the same care as their top sites.
trivia: Nuits-Saint-Georges has no Grand Cru vineyards — a historical anomaly, since many of its Premier Crus are considered superior to Grand Crus in other villages. Thought to be a result of 19th-century bureaucratic politics rather than terroir judgment.
Grivot farms vineyards in four Côte d'Or villages — Vosne-Romanée, Nuits-Saint-Georges, Vougeot, and Chambolle-Musigny — giving them one of the broadest portfolios of any single Burgundy estate.
The 2022 vintage was the subject of enormous pre-release enthusiasm — some critics have compared it favorably to the great 2015.

name: Grand Cru, Charmes-Chambertin
tableside: Grand Cru Burgundy — the real thing. Charmes-Chambertin is the largest Grand Cru in Gevrey-Chambertin and produces wines of great weight, depth, and complexity. The 2019 vintage was warm and concentrated. For the guest who wants to understand what all the Burgundy fuss is actually about.
story: Charmes-Chambertin is one of nine Grand Crus in Gevrey-Chambertin, the northernmost major village of the Côte de Nuits. Odoul-Coquard is a small domaine farming their Grand Cru parcel with intense care and minimal intervention.
trivia: Chambertin was reportedly Napoleon Bonaparte's favorite wine — he allegedly demanded it be available at every meal and on every campaign.
Charmes-Chambertin at 31 hectares is the largest Grand Cru in Gevrey by area, but the wines still represent a tiny fraction of total Burgundy production.
The 2019 Burgundy vintage is universally well-received — warm enough for full ripeness, with better acidity than initial fears about the heat suggested.

name: Gevrey-Chambertin
tableside: Louis Jadot is one of Burgundy's major négociant houses. Gevrey-Chambertin village wine from a reliable house: earthy, structured, classic. Good choice for guests who want genuine Burgundy without Grand Cru pricing.
story: Louis Jadot was founded in 1859 and now owns over 200 hectares of vineyards across the Côte d'Or. The Gevrey-Chambertin village wine blends fruit from multiple plots throughout the commune to capture the earthiness and structure the village is known for.
trivia: Jadot's winery in Beaune is one of the largest in Burgundy, producing millions of bottles across all appellations from Chablis to Mâconnais.

The négociant tradition involves buying grapes or finished wine from small growers and blending them under a single label — Jadot's strength comes partly from long-term relationships with exceptional growers.
Gevrey-Chambertin village wines can be cellared for 10–15 years in good vintages. The 2020 should drink well for at least a decade.

name: Gevrey-Chambertin
tableside: Nicolas Potel's house — he started Roche de Bellene after a legal dispute over his previous label and built it into one of Burgundy's most respected négociants. Village Gevrey from a producer who clearly knows what great Burgundy should taste like.
story: Nicolas Potel has one of the more dramatic stories in Burgundy: built his original label into a critically acclaimed success, was forced out by investors, started Roche de Bellene from scratch. His second act has been if anything better than his first — consistent, terroir-driven, and fairly priced.
trivia: Nicolas Potel's family owned Domaine de la Pousse d'Or in Volnay — he grew up surrounded by serious Burgundy and has an intuitive understanding of what the region's terroir can produce.
'Roche de Bellene' refers to a cliff face near Beaune that has been a landmark in the Côte d'Or for centuries.
The 2020 Burgundy vintage was warm but colder than 2019. Some worried about overripeness, but the best producers managed the heat well.

name: 'Vincengetorix,' Sancerre Rouge
tableside: Sancerre Rouge is one of France's best-kept secrets. Same region as the famous white, but Pinot Noir on limestone and flint rather than Sauvignon Blanc. Light, bright, and completely different from California or Oregon Pinot. For the guest who thinks they know Pinot but has never tried Loire Valley red.
story: Vincent Gaudry makes both the famous Sancerre Blanc and this far-less-known red from Loire Valley holdings. Sancerre Rouge is made entirely from Pinot Noir planted on the same flint and limestone soils as the Sauvignon Blanc — the result is a wine of extraordinary minerality and lightness unlike Pinot from any other region. The name 'Vincengetorix' playfully combines the winemaker's name and Vercingetorix, the Gallic warrior chief from the region's ancient history.
trivia: Sancerre's famous minerality comes from Kimmeridgian limestone and silex (flint) soils — the same geological formation underlying Chablis, 100 kilometers southeast.
Sancerre Rouge represents only about 20% of the appellation's total production — overwhelmingly known for white wine, the red is perpetually undervalued relative to its quality.
Vercingetorix, the Gallic chieftain who united the tribes of Gaul against Julius Caesar in 52 BC, was born in the region that is now central France.

name: Luxuria, Willamette Valley
tableside: Antiquum is a biodynamic farm in the Willamette Valley that raises sheep, grows cover crops, and makes wine in a very specific, intentional way. The Luxuria is their flagship Pinot: delicate, aromatic, and very Oregon. Good for guests who want to explore Pacific Northwest Pinot.
story: Jeff and Jill Meader run Antiquum Farm as a genuine working farm — sheep graze the vineyard in winter, cover crops improve soil health year-round. The Luxuria Pinot is made with whole clusters, native yeasts, and no added sulfur until bottling — a purist approach that translates into the wine's purity and freshness.
trivia: Oregon's Willamette Valley lies at almost the same latitude as Burgundy, a fact that early settlers like David Lett of Eyrie Vineyards cited when they first planted Pinot Noir there in the 1960s.
Biodynamic farming requires homeopathic vineyard preparations — fermented herbal and mineral teas applied at specific times according to the lunar calendar.
Antiquum's sheep serve a practical purpose: they graze the vineyard floor in winter, managing weeds without chemicals, and their manure fertilizes naturally.

name: Les Dijonnais, Willamette Valley
tableside: Brick House is one of the founding estates of serious Oregon Pinot — Doug Tunnel has been farming biodynamically in the Chehalem Mountains since the late 1980s. Les Dijonnais uses the Dijon clones that transformed Oregon Pinot quality in the 1990s. Classic and precise.
story: Doug Tunnel started Brick House in 1990 and was one of the first Oregon producers to commit fully to biodynamic farming. 'Les Dijonnais' refers to the Dijon clones (113, 114, 115, 667, 777) imported from Burgundy's Dijon university in the 1980s, which have since become the dominant clonal selections for serious Oregon Pinot.
trivia: The Dijon clones arrived in Oregon partly through the work of viticulture researchers who convinced French scientists that American Pinot producers deserved access to the same plant material available in Burgundy.
Brick House was Oregon's first certified biodynamic vineyard. Doug Tunnel's commitment helped establish biodynamic viticulture's credibility in the Pacific Northwest.
Oregon's Tillamook County, where many serious Pinot producers farm, is also famous for the Tillamook Creamery — making dairy products since 1909.

name: Roserock, Eola-Amity Hills
tableside: A Burgundian house making Oregon Pinot — Drouhin planted Roserock in the Eola-Amity Hills in 1988 because Robert Drouhin believed the conditions were comparable to his best Côte d'Or sites. The Eola-Amity Hills are Oregon's coolest sub-appellation, producing lighter, more aromatic wines. Good value for the provenance.
story: Robert Drouhin's investment in Oregon was seen as either bold or eccentric. Véronique Drouhin, who makes wine at both the Oregon and Burgundy estates, has turned Domaine Drouhin Oregon into a reference point for what French terroir philosophy produces in Pacific Northwest conditions.
trivia: Robert Drouhin first visited Oregon in 1961 but didn't invest until 1987 — after the famous 1979 Gault Millau tasting in Paris where the 1975 Eyrie Vineyards Pinot finished in the top ten among world Pinots, stunning the wine world. Drouhin organized a rematch in 1980, where Eyrie finished a narrow second to his own 1959 Chambolle-Musigny.
The Eola-Amity Hills benefits from the Van Duzer Corridor, a gap in the Coast Range that channels cool Pacific winds directly into the area each afternoon.
Drouhin has maintained both the Oregon and Burgundy projects simultaneously for over 30 years — a genuine vote of confidence in both locations.

name: La Source, Willamette Valley
tableside: La Source is Evening Land's top expression — whole-cluster, native yeast, old French oak, from the estate's most concentrated sites. Big for Oregon, but with genuine complexity. A good recommendation for guests who want serious Pacific Northwest Pinot.
story: Evening Land was founded with backing from Burgundian investors who wanted to translate their understanding of terroir to Oregon conditions. La Source is the estate flagship — the name references an underground spring running through the property, thought to give the wines their mineral character.
trivia: Evening Land's investment in Oregon was driven partly by the work of writer Matt Kramer, who argued in 'Making Sense of Burgundy' that the Willamette Valley had more in common with the Côte d'Or than any other region on earth.
Whole-cluster fermentation — adding uncrushed grape bunches, stems and all — adds spice, aromatic complexity, and structure, but requires very ripe stems to avoid greenness.
La Source has been rated among the top 100 wines in the world by Wine Spectator multiple times — unusual recognition for an Oregon Pinot outside the established marquee producers.

name: 'Anklebreaker Block,' Corrine Vineyard
tableside: 'Anklebreaker' refers to the steep, rocky path through the vineyard that winemaker Patricia Green navigates daily. Single-vineyard Oregon Pinot from one of the Willamette Valley's most respected small producers. Light, precise, and very food-friendly.
story: Patricia Green Cellars sources from some of the Willamette Valley's finest vineyards. The Anklebreaker Block is named for the literal challenge of walking through the rocky, steep section of the Corrine Vineyard where the fruit comes from.
trivia: Patricia Green sources from multiple designated vineyards throughout the Willamette Valley, making it possible to compare the same winemaking approach across different sites.
The 2021 Oregon vintage was complicated by a late June heat dome event. Producers in cooler sub-appellations fared better, and Green sources from some of the coolest sites available.
Oregon requires that wines labeled with a specific appellation contain at least 95% of their fruit from that appellation — stricter than California's 85% minimum.

name: Willamette Valley
tableside: Tony Soter made his name making wine for Etude in Napa before moving his focus to Oregon Pinot. This is his estate Willamette Valley expression — consistent, well-priced, food-friendly. A reliable recommendation for guests exploring Oregon for the first time.
story: Tony Soter is one of the most respected winemaking consultants in California — his work for Etude, Spottswoode, and others established his reputation before he shifted attention to Willamette Valley Pinot. His Oregon estate reflects a belief that Pacific Northwest conditions allow for more elegant, less extracted wines than California's warmer climate typically produces.
trivia: Tony Soter has worked as a consulting winemaker for some of the most prestigious estates in California, including Dalla Valle and Araujo. His move to Oregon surprised many observers.

The Willamette Valley is named for the Willamette River, which flows northward from the Cascades to the Columbia River. The north-south orientation shelters the valley from Pacific storms by the Coast Range.

Oregon Pinot has undergone a remarkable quality revolution since the 1990s — driven by better clonal selection, improved understanding of specific vineyard sites, and a new generation of winemakers who trained in Burgundy.

name: Shea Vineyard
tableside: Shea Vineyard is one of Oregon's most famous single-vineyard sites — dozens of producers buy fruit from it, and the resulting wines are reliably excellent. Walt's take is richer and more California-influenced than some, but with the characteristic Shea earthiness that makes the site famous.
story: Shea Vineyard in the Chehalem Mountains has been supplying fruit to many of Oregon's top producers for over two decades. The site's combination of volcanic Jory soils, good drainage, and ideal exposition for Pinot Noir has made it a reference point for Willamette Valley terroir.
trivia: Shea Vineyard's owners plant the vineyard but sell the fruit to multiple producers — a model common in Burgundy where individual growers sell to négoçiants.

The Chehalem Mountains sub-appellation has three soil types: Jory (volcanic basalt), Laurelwood (wind-deposited loess), and Willakenzie (marine sedimentary). Each produces Pinot with a noticeably different character.
The contrast between Walt's California Pinot (Bob's Ranch, Sonoma Coast) and Oregon Pinot (Shea Vineyard) allows for a useful California–Oregon comparison with a single producer's perspective.

name: 'Patricia,' Alto Adige
tableside: Alto Adige Pinot Noir is one of Italy's best-kept wine secrets. High altitude, Austrian cultural influence, and cool temperatures produce a very different Pinot than California or Burgundy. Light, precise, and surprisingly good at this price.
story: Girlan is a cooperative based in the South Tyrol — the German-speaking wine region of northern Italy that was part of Austria until 1919. The cultural duality shows in the wine: Germanic precision and Alpine freshness combined with Italian attention to the table.
trivia: Alto Adige (also Südtirol or South Tyrol) is the northernmost wine region in Italy and one of the highest-altitude. Road signs are in both Italian and German; locals often speak both plus a local dialect called Ladin.
The region was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire until 1918, which explains the Germanic grape varieties grown alongside Italian ones.
Alto Adige has the highest density of DOP-awarded restaurants per capita of any Italian region.

name: Vigna San Carlo, Pinot Nero
tableside: Paolo Saracco is famous for Moscato d'Asti, but this single-vineyard Pinot Nero shows his versatility. One of the few serious Pinot Nero expressions from a region better known for Nebbiolo. Aged and interesting.
story: Paolo Saracco farms in the Moscato d'Asti zone in southern Piedmont, where his sweet and semi-sparkling Moscato wines are benchmark expressions of the variety. The Vigna San Carlo Pinot Nero comes from a single vineyard he's farmed for decades — an unusual expression of a grape that rarely appears in this part of Italy.
trivia: Pinot Nero (Italian for Pinot Noir) has a long history in Piedmont, though historically overshadowed by the region's dominant Nebbiolo variety.
Paolo Saracco's Moscato d'Asti is a benchmark of the category — low-alcohol, gently effervescent, and aromatic in a way that shows what Italy can do with sweet wine as a craft.
The Langhe hills of Piedmont sit at similar latitudes to Burgundy — a parallel noted by wine scholars studying where Pinot Noir thrives internationally.

name: 'Sequentis,' Paso Robles
tableside: DAOU's Merlot flagship. The same family that turned Paso Robles Cabernet into a national conversation. Sequentis is full-bodied, oaked, and built for a steak. Good entry-level Merlot for guests who want something rich without going straight to the Cab list.
story: Georges and Daniel Daou built their Paso Robles estate on a high-altitude hillside where day-to-night temperature swings are some of the most dramatic in California wine country. The Sequentis Merlot benefits from these conditions — warm days build concentration, cold nights preserve acidity. The result is a Merlot with more structure than the variety typically shows in warmer California regions.
trivia: DAOU became famous primarily for their Soul of a Lion Cabernet, which became a cult wine almost overnight. The Merlot is their way of showing the estate can excel with other varieties.
Paso Robles has two distinct sub-regions separated by US Highway 101: the warmer eastern side (where DAOU's vineyards are) and the cooler western side. The two sides produce markedly different wines.

Merlot's reputation never fully recovered from the movie Sideways (2004), in which the lead character famously declares he won't drink it. Wine sales of the variety dropped nationally after the film's release.

name: Napa Valley
tableside: Jarvis makes some of the most serious wines in Napa from an extraordinary underground cave winery in the Vaca Mountains. This Merlot is big and structured — not a drink-me-now wine. For guests who appreciate Napa Cabernet's seriousness but want something different from the standard.
story: William Jarvis carved a winery directly into the volcanic basalt of the Vaca Mountains in the early 1990s — blasting 30,000 square feet of underground tunnels that provide ideal natural storage conditions: constant temperature, high humidity, complete darkness. The Merlot comes from estate vineyards at high elevation where yields are naturally restricted.
trivia: Jarvis Winery's cave was one of the first major winery cave projects in California. Since then, cave wineries have become a status symbol in Napa, but Jarvis remains one of the few built primarily for practical wine storage rather than tourism.
The Vaca Mountains on Napa's eastern boundary are geologically distinct from the valley floor — volcanic basalt rather than alluvial sediments, producing smaller crops with more concentrated flavors.
William Jarvis has been making wine in Napa since the 1980s and largely avoided the marketing machinery of the industry — Jarvis wines are known through reputation and word of mouth.

name: Walla Walla Valley
tableside: Leonetti is arguably Washington State's most iconic producer — Gary Figgins was one of the pioneering figures of Walla Walla wine in the 1970s. This Merlot is dense, rich, and shows why Washington State Merlot is a completely different animal from California: more concentrated, more tannin, longer aging potential.
story: Gary Figgins founded Leonetti Cellar in 1978, making him one of the true pioneers of Washington State wine. The Walla Walla Valley's hot days and cold nights — even more extreme than Napa's diurnal swings — produce wines with exceptional concentration and structure. The Merlot has been one of Leonetti's flagship expressions from the beginning.
trivia: Leonetti Cellar has been on a mailing list allocation system since the early 1980s, with a wait list that stretched to years at its peak. Getting it requires either being on the list or finding restaurant allocations.
Walla Walla is unusual among American wine regions in that it straddles a state line — about 7% of the appellation falls in Oregon rather than Washington.

Gary Figgins's son Chris now runs Leonetti, continuing the family tradition. It is one of very few original Washington State producers to have remained entirely family-owned through the industry's consolidation.

name: Rutherford Estate, Napa Valley
tableside: St. Supery's estate Merlot from Rutherford. Certified sustainable, well-aged (2017), and drinking in a beautiful window right now. Good value for Rutherford Napa — and a useful reply to any guest who thinks Merlot can't be serious.
story: St. Supery is a Rutherford estate with significant French ownership that has always maintained a Bordeaux-influenced approach. The Dolce Ranch, where this Merlot's fruit comes from, is one of the classic Rutherford addresses. Rutherford's famous silky tannin character — sometimes called 'Rutherford Dust' — shows up in the wine's long, seamless finish.
trivia: 'Rutherford Dust' is one of wine's most famous marketing terms — coined in the 1990s to describe the chalky, mineral quality of tannins in wines from this specific stretch of the Napa Valley floor.

St. Supery was acquired by Chanel (the fashion house) in 2015 — the French luxury brand's first American winery acquisition. Chanel's wine portfolio also includes Château Rauzan-Ségla in Margaux, Château Canon and Château Berliquet in St-Émilion.
The 2017 Napa vintage was complicated by October wildfires. St. Supery's Rutherford vineyards avoided the worst smoke effects, producing clean, concentrated wine.

name: 'Jersey Girl,' Paso Robles
tableside: A bold, full-throttle Paso Robles Syrah from Jada — same estate that makes Hell's Kitchen on the list. Jersey Girl is their varietal Syrah: inky, rich, and built for guests who like big reds. Named with self-deprecating humor (the owner is from New Jersey). An easy sell.
story: Jada Vineyard sits in the oak-studded hills of western Paso Robles, where the Pacific influence moderates temperatures just enough to preserve freshness in otherwise warm-climate grapes. The Jersey Girl Syrah is the estate's statement on what Paso Syrah can be: dense, dark, with enough structure to age but enough fruit to drink young.
trivia: Paso Robles' western hills benefit from the Templeton Gap — a break in the Santa Lucia mountain range that allows cool Pacific air to flow in each afternoon. The region is also famous for dramatic diurnal swings of up to 50°F between daytime highs and overnight lows, preserving natural acidity in the grapes.
Syrah in warm climates like Paso tends toward black fruit, tobacco, and dark chocolate. Cool-climate Syrah — Northern Rhône or West Sonoma Coast — is more purple-fruited and peppery.
At 15% alcohol, this is one of the higher-ABV wines on the list — worth noting for guests who are sensitive to alcohol, though the richness of the wine tends to absorb it well.

name: 'Velvet Glove,' McLaren Vale
tableside: 16.5% alcohol, nearly opaque in the glass, and scored above 99 points in multiple vintages. This is Australian Shiraz at maximum intensity. Not for the faint-hearted — but if a table wants the most powerful red on the list, this is the answer.
story: Sparky and Sarah Marquis founded Mollydooker in McLaren Vale after decades making wine for other estates. Velvet Glove is their prestige Shiraz, sourced from old-vine McLaren Vale fruit. The name comes from the phrase 'iron fist in a velvet glove' — the wine has enormous power wrapped in unusual smoothness for its size.
trivia: At 16.5% alcohol, Velvet Glove is one of the highest-ABV wines on the list. Australian wine law allows any alcohol level that's accurately stated on the label — there's no upper limit.
'Mollydooker' is Australian slang for left-handed person. The label art and packaging reflect this theme throughout.

Sparky Marquis developed a proprietary winemaking technique he calls the 'Marquis Emanation Process' — an extreme form of tank-to-tank racking with oxygen exposure that he claims adds texture and softens the appearance of high tannins.

name: 'Black Chicken,' Napa Valley
tableside: Black Chicken is one of the most famous Zinfandel labels in California — a reference to an old Prohibition-era story involving coded deliveries. Old vines, full body, and the characteristic Napa richness. For guests who think Zinfandel is just a sweet blush wine, this is the education.
story: During Prohibition, black-market wine was allegedly delivered by truck with a black chicken on the door as a coded signal. Robert Biale now farms old-vine Zinfandel in various parts of the valley, including plots dating to the 19th century. The Black Chicken comes from a specific old-vine block with roots reaching into volcanic Napa soils.
trivia: Robert Biale Vineyards sources from some of Napa's oldest-producing Zinfandel vines — some over 100 years old, with roots deep into the valley floor. Old vines produce naturally small crops of intensely concentrated berries.
The 'Black Chicken' Prohibition story may be apocryphal, but it has been part of the Biale family lore for so long it functions as cultural fact.

Zinfandel is genetically identical to the Croatian grape Tribidrag (also called Crljenak Kaštelanski), identified through DNA testing in the early 2000s. Its Italian relative, Primitivo, is also the same variety.

name: Malbec 'Serenade,' Mendoza
tableside: Good Argentine Malbec at a fair price. Serenade is Alta Vista's premium expression — more concentration and structure than their entry-level wine. Malbec is Argentina's signature variety: deeply colored, spicy, and satisfying in a way that works with a wide range of food.
story: Alta Vista sources from vineyards at multiple elevations in Mendoza, blending for complexity. Malbec was originally a minor Bordeaux variety before being transformed in Argentina — brought over by French immigrants in the 19th century, it found conditions in Mendoza's high-altitude vineyards far more suited to its expression than its European home.
trivia: Malbec in Mendoza is grown at elevations up to 5,000 feet — the altitude reduces temperatures and UV radiation, allowing slower ripening that preserves natural acidity and produces more aromatic, complex wines than lower-altitude expressions.
Malbec is essentially the national grape of Argentina — the wine industry has been so successful promoting it internationally that the variety now sells more Argentine wine than all others combined.

In Bordeaux, Malbec (also called Côt or Auxerrois) was largely abandoned after phylloxera and hard winters devastated the variety in the 19th century — it survived in Argentina while disappearing from its homeland.

name: Bonarda, Mendoza
tableside: Bonarda is Argentina's second most planted variety after Malbec — but almost no one outside Argentina has heard of it. Light, fresh, and very food-friendly. For guests who want something genuinely unexpected from South America.
story: Bonarda was planted extensively in Argentina by Italian immigrants who found it easier to work with than other varieties. It produces light, fresh, slightly lively wines in the local tradition — something like the Argentine equivalent of Beaujolais. Colonia Las Liebres specializes in Bonarda as a tribute to the variety's underappreciated character.
trivia: Bonarda is Argentina's second most planted red variety after Malbec — but while Malbec commands export attention, Bonarda remains largely domestic.
The exact identity of Argentine Bonarda is debated — DNA testing suggests it may be the same variety as Charbono (California) and Douce Noir (Savoie, France).
At $45, this is one of the most affordable wines on the list. Its obscurity is part of the appeal — guests who try it rarely expect to enjoy it as much as they do.

name: Mourvèdre, Santa Barbara County
tableside: Pure Mourvèdre from Santa Barbara — unusual, savory, and a bit wild. Mourvèdre is the grape behind the great wines of Bandol in Provence, where it produces deeply colored, herb-and-game-scented reds. California Mourvèdre is lighter but captures the variety's savory character. For adventurous guests.
story: Clementine Carter is a small Santa Barbara producer making varietal wines from grapes that most California producers blend into other things. Mourvèdre is traditionally a blending grape — the M in GSM — but as a varietal wine it reveals a character that disappears in blends: garrigue, olive, and a slightly meaty, almost wild quality.
trivia: Mourvèdre (also known as Monastrell in Spain and Mataro in California and Australia) is one of the oldest grape varieties in the Western Mediterranean, potentially originating in Valencia before spreading throughout the region.
Bandol, the Provence appellation that mandates Mourvèdre as the dominant variety, produces wines considered among the longest-lived reds in France — some need 20 years to open fully.

'Garrigue' is the French term for the wild scrubland covering the hills of Provence — lavender, rosemary, thyme, wild fennel. It's both a real landscape and a tasting note for wines that grow in proximity to it.

name: Tempranillo, Napa Valley
tableside: Napa Valley Tempranillo — unusual enough that guests will ask about it. Jarvis grows Tempranillo in their high-altitude Vaca Mountain vineyards, and it produces something quite different from Rioja: richer, more concentrated, but with the grape's characteristic aromatic lift.
story: Tempranillo is Spain's most important red variety, but it's rarely grown in Napa. Jarvis's Vaca Mountain vineyards provide the elevation and cool nights that give the grape the acidity it needs to balance the California sun. The result is a California interpretation of a Spanish classic: familiar in structure, different in fruit character.
trivia: Tempranillo's name comes from the Spanish 'temprano' (early) — a reference to the grape's tendency to ripen earlier than other varieties in its native Spanish regions.

California had significant plantings of Tempranillo in the early 20th century when Spanish immigrants brought it over. Most were pulled during Prohibition or replaced with more commercially valuable varieties.

Jarvis's Vaca Mountain location is one of the few California sites cool enough to ripen Tempranillo without losing its characteristic acidity — at lower elevations, the heat would produce overripe, flabby wine.

name: Cabernet Franc, Napa Valley
tableside: Varietal Napa Cab Franc — a grape that's usually blended with Cabernet Sauvignon but shows extraordinary complexity on its own. Peju's version is fully ripe, which removes the aggressive green character Cab Franc can show in cooler climates. Elegant, savory, and genuinely unusual on any Napa list.
story: Cab Franc has been having a global moment — its natural elegance and freshness at lower alcohol levels have attracted a new generation of winemakers. Peju Province, a family estate in Rutherford, has made varietal Cab Franc for decades and understands how to ripen it fully without losing its characteristic tobacco and pencil-shaving aromatics.
