The Talento is aged for several years before release, giving the firm tannins time to integrate.
trivia: Aglianico's name is thought to derive from 'Hellenico' — Hellenic, or Greek — a reference to its introduction to Italy by Greek colonists who established cities throughout southern Italy from the 8th century BC onward.

The volcanic soils of Campania, where Aglianico thrives, are the same soils that buried Pompeii and Herculaneum in 79 AD — the mineral richness that comes from volcanic activity is thought to contribute directly to the grape's distinctive character.

Aglianico del Vulture, from the volcanic slopes of Mount Vulture in neighboring Basilicata, is considered by many wine scholars to be the most age-worthy red wine in southern Italy — some vintages need 20+ years to fully open.

name: Nebbiolo, Monvigliero Reserva, Barolo DOCG
tableside: Barolo from Castello di Verduno — one of the oldest estates in the appellation, making wine in the traditional style: long maceration, large Slavonian oak casks, no compromise on the variety's structure. The Monvigliero is one of Barolo's most prized single vineyards. For guests who know Barolo, this is the serious conversation.
story: Castello di Verduno has been making wine in the Barolo commune since the early 19th century — it was actually the preferred supplier to the royal House of Savoy. The Monvigliero vineyard in Verduno produces Barolo with a more delicate, floral character than the more powerful expressions from La Morra or Serralunga d'Alba, and Castello di Verduno is considered the benchmark producer for this site.
trivia: Nebbiolo is one of the world's most challenging grapes to grow: it buds early (making it frost-sensitive), ripens late (requiring a long, warm autumn), and produces wines of such high acidity and tannin that they need years — sometimes decades — to become approachable.

The classic tasting note for Barolo is 'tar and roses' — the tar coming from the grape's high tannin and the rose from its distinctive floral aromatics. It's one of the most instantly recognizable flavor profiles in fine wine.

The Monvigliero vineyard in Verduno is one of Barolo's most prized single-vineyard sites, known for producing wines of exceptional elegance and floral aromatics compared to the more powerful expressions from other communes.

name: Nebbiolo, Barbaresco DOCG
tableside: Barbaresco is Barolo's neighbor and rival — same Nebbiolo grape, slightly different soils and microclimate, and a reputation for wines that are more approachable younger. Ceretto is one of Piedmont's most respected estates. A great introduction to Nebbiolo for guests who find Barolo too demanding.
story: Ceretto is a family estate that has been at the forefront of modern Piedmontese wine for decades, working with some of the finest single vineyards in both Barolo and Barbaresco. Their Barbaresco tends toward the elegant, approachable end of the Nebbiolo spectrum — more immediately drinkable than their single-vineyard Barolo expressions, but with all the complexity the grape is capable of.
trivia: Barbaresco and Barolo are separated by the Tanaro River — both are made from 100% Nebbiolo, but Barbaresco's soils tend to produce wines that open earlier and show more aromatic complexity in their youth.

Ceretto is famous not just for wine but for art — the family has commissioned remarkable architectural installations in their vineyards, including the famous 'Acino' (grape berry) glass chapel on a hilltop above Castiglione Falletto.
The 2020 Barbaresco vintage was widely celebrated — a warm, dry year that produced wines with excellent concentration and earlier approachability than more classic years like 2016 or 2019.

name: Barbera 'Pomorosso,' Nizza
tableside: Pomorosso is one of Italy's most ambitious Barbera expressions — aged in new French oak for 18 months, concentrated from old vines, and built like a serious red wine rather than an everyday Italian table staple. For guests who think they know Barbera from cheap Barbera d'Asti, this is the education.
story: Coppo has been making wine in Canelli in the Asti province since 1892, and Pomorosso — named for the pomodorose tomatoes that used to grow on the property — is their showcase for what Barbera can achieve when treated with serious intent. Old vines, low yields, and new French oak transform a variety usually associated with everyday drinking into something genuinely ambitious.
trivia: Barbera is Italy's third most planted red variety after Sangiovese and Montepulciano — it's ubiquitous as an everyday table wine throughout Piedmont. But old-vine Barbera from serious producers like Coppo has almost nothing in common with the industrial version.
Nizza became its own DOCG appellation in 2014, carved out of the broader Barbera d'Asti zone to recognize the superior quality of wines from this specific area around the town of Nizza Monferrato.

Barbera's naturally high acidity is one of its defining characteristics — even in warm vintages, it retains the freshness that makes it work with food. Combined with very low natural tannin, it's one of the most food-friendly of all Italian red varieties.

name: Nebbiolo, Barolo Lazzarito DOCG
tableside: A 2015 single-vineyard Barolo — now eleven years old and entering its drinking window. Lazzarito is one of the great vineyards of Serralunga d'Alba, known for producing Barolo of exceptional power and longevity. For guests who want to understand why Barolo is called the 'king of Italian wines.'
story: Lazzarito is one of the Barolo MGA (Menzione Geografica Aggiuntiva — the Italian equivalent of Premier Cru) sites in the Serralunga d'Alba commune, where the compact Helvetian soils produce wines of extraordinary structure and longevity. The 2015 vintage was warm and produced wines with more immediate fruit than the austere classic vintages, but the Lazzarito's structure is still very much present.
trivia: Barolo's MGA system — established in 2010 — officially recognized 181 distinct vineyard sites within the appellation, finally giving legal identity to the single-vineyard distinctions that producers had been making informally for decades.

Serralunga d'Alba's Helvetian soils are the oldest and most compact in the Barolo zone — they produce wines of extreme structure and longevity that need more time than wines from softer-soiled communes like La Morra.

The 2015 Barolo vintage was initially compared to the legendary 2010 — warmer and more immediately approachable than 2010, but with excellent underlying structure. The best bottles are just now entering their drinking window.

name: Primitivo di Manduria 'Papale'
tableside: Primitivo di Manduria is Puglia's signature wine — rich, dark, and full-bodied from the sun-baked heel of Italy's boot. Primitivo is genetically identical to Zinfandel, which gives California fans an interesting comparison point. 'Papale' means 'papal' — a reference to the wine's historical connection to the Vatican's table.
story: Varvaglione is one of the historic estates of the Primitivo di Manduria DOC in Puglia, where the variety has been cultivated since the 17th century. Papale is their flagship expression — made from old bush-trained vines and aged in French and American oak to balance the variety's natural richness with structure.
trivia: Primitivo and Zinfandel are genetically identical — both are the same variety as the Croatian grape Tribidrag. Primitivo likely arrived in Puglia from Dalmatia in the 18th century, decades before Zinfandel appeared in California.

Bush-trained vines (alberello in Italian) are the traditional training system in Puglia — the low, unsupported vines shade their own fruit from the intense southern Italian sun, reducing stress and producing more complex flavor.

The name 'Papale' reflects the historical importance of Puglia's wines to the Catholic Church — the region was a major supplier of strong, concentrated wine for blending and for sacramental use throughout the medieval period.

name: Rosso del Conte, Nero d'Avola/Perricone
tableside: The flagship red of Regaleali — one of Sicily's most historic estates. Rosso del Conte is a blend of Nero d'Avola and the ancient Perricone variety, and has been made since 1970. The 2017 is now nine years old and showing real complexity. For guests who want to explore serious Sicilian red.
story: Tenuta Regaleali (also known as Tasca d'Almerita) has been the benchmark for quality Sicilian wine since the post-war era. Rosso del Conte was the wine that first demonstrated that Sicily could produce wines of genuine complexity and aging potential — before it, most Sicilian red went north for blending rather than being bottled under its own identity. The Perricone component adds freshness and herbal character to the dark, plummy Nero d'Avola.
trivia: Nero d'Avola is Sicily's signature red variety — its name means 'black grape of Avola,' a town in the southeastern corner of the island. It produces deeply colored, rich wines that have attracted international attention since the 1990s.

Perricone (also called Pignatello) is one of Sicily's oldest native varieties, mentioned in historical records going back centuries. It was nearly extinct in the late 20th century before estates like Regaleali began preserving it.

Sicily is one of the world's most geologically diverse wine regions — the island has been shaped by volcanic activity, African tectonic movement, and millennia of Mediterranean climate, producing soils unlike anywhere else in Italy.

name: 'Pian delle Vigne,' Sangiovese, Montalcino
tableside: A 2012 Brunello-style Sangiovese from Antinori's Montalcino estate — now fourteen years old and in a beautiful drinking window. Brunello di Montalcino requires 100% Sangiovese and extended aging, producing wines of extraordinary longevity. This is a rare opportunity to drink a properly aged example.
story: Antinori's Pian delle Vigne estate in Montalcino was acquired in the 1990s and has become one of the appellation's respected producers. The wine is made under Brunello di Montalcino DOCG rules — 100% Sangiovese, minimum 5 years aging before release including at least 2 in barrel. At twelve years of age, the 2012 has had time to develop the complexity and softness that make aged Brunello one of Italy's great wine experiences.
trivia: Brunello di Montalcino is one of Italy's longest-lived wines — top vintages can age for 30–50 years. The extended aging requirement (5 years for standard, 6 for Riserva) makes it one of the most expensive wines to produce in Italy.
The Sangiovese clone grown in Montalcino is called Brunello — genetically the same variety as the Chianti Classico Sangiovese, but adapted over centuries to Montalcino's specific conditions.

Antinori is one of the oldest wine families in the world — records of the family's involvement in the Florence wine trade go back to 1385, making it one of the longest continuously operating wine businesses in history.

name: Tignanello, Sangiovese/Cab Franc/Cab Sauv
tableside: Tignanello invented the category of Super Tuscan wines in 1971 when Antinori first blended Sangiovese with Cabernet and aged it in small French oak barrels — at the time a radical departure from Chianti Classico rules. It remains the most famous Super Tuscan and one of Italy's most important wines. For any guest who appreciates Italian wine at a serious level.
story: Tignanello's creation in 1971 is one of the defining moments in modern Italian wine history. Giacomo Tachis, Antinori's legendary winemaker, blended Sangiovese with Cabernet Sauvignon and Cabernet Franc, aged it in barriques rather than large Slavonian oak casks, and released it as a vino da tavola (table wine) because it didn't conform to the Chianti Classico DOCG rules. The wine was immediately recognized as extraordinary, and it created the entire Super Tuscan category.
trivia: Tignanello was technically a 'table wine' (the lowest category in Italian wine law) when it was first released — because it contained non-traditional varieties and used French oak aging. The absurdity of Italy's finest wines being classified as table wine eventually led to the IGT category reform in 1992.
The vineyard 'Tignanello' from which the wine takes its name has been farmed by the Antinori family since the 14th century — it's one of the most historically documented vineyard sites in Tuscany.
The 2021 vintage in Tuscany was considered excellent — a balanced year with moderate temperatures and good acid retention that produced wines with both concentration and elegance.

name: 'Il Blu,' Cab/Sangiovese/Merlot
tableside: Il Blu is one of Tuscany's finest Super Tuscans — a Cabernet-dominant blend from the Swiss Widmer family who have been farming in Chianti Classico since the 1980s. More structured and age-worthy than many Super Tuscans. For guests who want a serious Tuscan red that isn't Tignanello.
story: Brancaia was founded in 1981 when the Widmer family from Switzerland purchased a run-down estate in the Chianti Classico zone. Il Blu (named for the family's favorite color) is made from their best parcels across both their Chianti Classico and Maremma estates, blended to create a wine of greater complexity than either site alone could produce.
trivia: Brancaia's two estates — one in the Chianti Classico hills and one in the Maremma coastal zone — produce fruit with quite different characters: the Chianti fruit is more structured and herbal, the Maremma fruit richer and more Mediterranean. Il Blu blends both to create complexity.
The Maremma, where part of Brancaia's fruit comes from, was a malaria-ridden swamp until Mussolini had it drained in the 1930s. It's now one of Tuscany's most dynamic wine regions.

The Super Tuscan category — which doesn't officially exist in Italian wine law but is universally understood — encompasses wines that use non-traditional varieties or methods and are labeled as IGT Toscana rather than under a specific DOCG appellation.

name: Brunello di Montalcino, Sangiovese
tableside: Brunello di Montalcino from a smaller producer — 100% Sangiovese, extended aging, and the mineral, herb-driven character that defines wines from this hilltop town. The 2018 vintage was excellent in Montalcino. A good benchmark Brunello for guests exploring the appellation.
story: Mate is a smaller estate in Montalcino making Brunello in the traditional style — large Slavonian oak casks, extended aging, minimal intervention. The 2018 vintage was warm and produced wines with excellent concentration and the characteristic mineral depth that Montalcino's galestro and alberese soils contribute.
trivia: Montalcino sits on a hilltop at about 1,700 feet above sea level — the elevation moderates the summer heat and allows Sangiovese to ripen slowly, developing complexity without losing acidity.
Brunello di Montalcino was one of the first wines to receive DOCG status (the highest classification in Italian wine) in 1980 — a recognition of its quality and historical significance.
The 2018 vintage in Montalcino was warm and dry — some producers considered it comparable to the legendary 2015 and 2016 vintages, with generous fruit and excellent structure for long aging.

name: 'Philip,' Cab Sauv, Chianti Classico
tableside: Mazzei's 'Philip' is an unusual Chianti Classico — pure Cabernet Sauvignon rather than the traditional Sangiovese, from one of the appellation's oldest estates. The 2015 is now eleven years old and drinking beautifully. For guests who love Cab and want to see what Tuscany does with it.
story: The Mazzei family has been farming in Chianti Classico since 1435 — one of the oldest wine dynasties in Italy. 'Philip' is named for Filippo Mazzei, an 18th-century figure who became a close friend of Thomas Jefferson and helped introduce Italian viticulture to Virginia (a connection that adds a nice local resonance for this Washington DC-area restaurant). The wine is a statement on what Cabernet Sauvignon can achieve in Tuscany's specific soil and climate.
trivia: Filippo Mazzei — for whom this wine is named — moved to Virginia in 1773 at Thomas Jefferson's invitation and established one of the first Italian-style vineyards in America at Colle, adjacent to Jefferson's Monticello. The Virginia connection between the Mazzei family and American wine history is genuine and direct.

The Mazzei family's estate, Fonterutoli, has been continuously owned by the same family since 1435 — through wars, plagues, political upheavals, and the complete restructuring of Italian wine law. It's one of the longest continuous wine estate ownerships in the world.

Cabernet Sauvignon was planted widely in Tuscany during the Super Tuscan boom of the 1980s and 1990s. While most Super Tuscan blends use it as a supporting variety, Philip's 100% Cabernet expression shows how Tuscan conditions give the grape a distinctly Italian character — more herbal and mineral than California.

name: 'Indaco,' Malbec/Cab Sauv/Merlot
tableside: Bolgheri is the coastal Tuscan zone that produced Sassicaia — the wine that started the Super Tuscan revolution. Tenuta Sette Cieli is a smaller estate in the same area, making an unusual Malbec-led blend. The coastal microclimate and the unusual variety combination make this one of the most distinctive wines on the Italian section.
story: Tenuta Sette Cieli ('Estate of Seven Skies') sits in the hills above Bolgheri, where the microclimate is influenced by both the Tyrrhenian Sea and the Apennine Mountains. The Indaco blend leads with Malbec — unusual for Bolgheri, where Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot dominate — and produces a wine with a distinctive violet and plum character.
trivia: Bolgheri became famous when Sassicaia (made by the Incisa della Rocchetta family) was revealed to be one of Italy's finest wines in a 1978 Decanter tasting — it had been produced for decades as a private wine before being commercially released.
Malbec in Bolgheri produces wines quite different from Argentine Malbec — the Italian coastal climate gives the wine more freshness and herbal complexity than the high-altitude Mendoza expression.
The name 'Sette Cieli' (Seven Skies) refers to the seven colors visible at sunset from the estate's hilltop position — a poetic name for a winery that takes its landscape seriously.

name: Sagrantino, Montefalco
tableside: Sagrantino di Montefalco is one of the world's most tannic red wines — it makes Barolo look approachable. Grown only in a tiny area of Umbria, the variety produces wines of extraordinary structure that need years to open. This is for the guest who says they want the most intense red on the list.
story: Sagrantino is grown almost exclusively in the hills around Montefalco in Umbria, where it has been cultivated since at least the 17th century. The grape has the highest polyphenol content of any red variety in the world — meaning massive tannins that require years of aging to soften. Rocca di Fabri is one of the appellation's respected producers, making Sagrantino in the traditional way with extended maceration and oak aging.
trivia: Sagrantino has the highest tannin levels of any commercially produced red variety in the world — significantly higher than Barolo's Nebbiolo or Bordeaux's Cabernet Sauvignon. Young Sagrantino can be almost undrinkable; with age it becomes magnificent.
Montefalco Sagrantino DOCG requires a minimum of 37 months aging before release — 12 months in oak, the rest in bottle. This is one of Italy's longest mandatory aging requirements.

Sagrantino was historically a sweet wine (Sagrantino Passito) used for religious purposes — 'Sagrantino' is thought to derive from 'sacrament.' The dry version we know today became dominant only in the late 20th century.

name: Corvina/Corvinone/Rondinella, Valpolicella Ripasso Marogne DOC
tableside: Ripasso means the wine is 're-passed' over the dried grape skins used to make Amarone — picking up extra color, tannin, and concentration without the full power of Amarone itself. Zeni's Ripasso is one of the best values on the list: more complexity than a standard Valpolicella, less weight than Amarone.
story: The Ripasso technique — macerating standard Valpolicella on the leftover pomace from Amarone fermentation — was developed as a way to create a 'baby Amarone' style at a lower price point. Zeni is one of the most respected producers in Valpolicella, farming in the Classico zone around the historic villages of Bardolino and San Pietro in Cariano.
trivia: The Ripasso technique was first documented at Masi winery in the 1960s and 1970s — what had previously been an informal practice became a recognized winemaking method. Masi trademarked the name 'Campofiorin' for their Ripasso, which is why other producers use the more generic 'Ripasso' designation.

Corvina is the dominant variety in all Valpolicella wines — Ripasso, Amarone, and Recioto. The grape's high natural acidity and relatively thin skin make it ideal for both the Ripasso and Amarone production methods.
Marogne is a specific geographic designation within Valpolicella Ripasso DOC, referring to a particular area within the Classico zone known for producing wines with additional mineral character.

name: Corvina/Molinara/Rondinella, Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG Classico
tableside: Amarone is made from partially dried grapes — the clusters are laid on bamboo racks for 3–4 months, concentrating the sugars and flavors before pressing and fermenting to dryness. The result is one of Italy's most powerful and distinctive red wines. At $99, this is one of the best price-to-quality plays on the entire list.
story: Zeni's Amarone at this price is genuinely remarkable — Amarone from serious producers typically starts at $80–100 and climbs rapidly to several hundred dollars. The fact that Zeni can produce a DOCG Classico Amarone at this price reflects their scale, efficiency, and long-standing relationships with growers throughout the Valpolicella Classico zone. The 2022 vintage was warm and produced wines with excellent concentration.
trivia: Amarone is fermented completely dry — the name comes from 'amaro' (bitter) to distinguish it from Recioto della Valpolicella, the sweet version made from the same dried grapes. When early producers first made a fully dry version, the name 'Amarone' (big bitter) was applied informally and eventually became official.

The appassimento (grape drying) process for Amarone reduces the grape weight by 30–40% through water evaporation, concentrating sugars, acids, and flavor compounds dramatically. This is why the wine is so rich and powerful.

At 15.5% alcohol, Amarone is one of the highest-ABV naturally fermented dry red wines produced anywhere in the world — the result of fermenting the concentrated sugar in dried grapes to complete dryness.

name: Frappato/Nero d'Avola 'SP68,' Sicily
tableside: Arianna Occhipinti's SP68 red is one of natural wine's most famous bottles — light, fresh, and made from two ancient Sicilian varieties that most of the world had never heard of before she started making this wine. Frappato gives the floral lift, Nero d'Avola gives the dark fruit spine. It's effortless and it's delicious. The same label as the white SP68 on the list.
story: Arianna Occhipinti returned to her family's land in Vittoria, Sicily in 2004 at age 22, having trained in enology in Milan. She started making wine with a clarity of purpose that was unusual for the time: organic farming, indigenous varieties, minimal intervention, and total honesty about what the land produced. SP68 — named for the provincial road that runs through her vineyards — became one of the emblematic bottles of the natural wine movement, and one of the most widely copied labels in the world.
trivia: Frappato is one of Sicily's most ancient indigenous varieties — light, aromatic, and often described as the Sicilian Pinot Noir for its delicacy and floral character. It was nearly forgotten before producers like Occhipinti championed it.

The SP68 white and red are made from different grapes but the same philosophy — both are certified organic, made with ambient yeast, and aged without new oak. The matching labels make them a natural pairing conversation for guests.

Arianna Occhipinti's uncle is Giusto Occhipinti of COS, one of Sicily's most celebrated natural wine producers. She trained partly under him before starting her own project — a genuinely remarkable family lineage in Sicilian wine.

name: Pinot Noir, Carnuntum
tableside: Hanna Glatzer's Pinot Noir is the natural wine counterpart to the Bründlmayer Kamptal Pinots in the main list — same country, same grape, completely different philosophy. Certified biodynamic, made with ambient yeasts, unfiltered. Lighter and more transparent than most Austrian Pinot. For guests who want natural wine and love Pinot.
story: Hanna Glatzer farms in Carnuntum — the same Roman-era wine region as Walter Glatzer (same family, different generation). Her project is explicitly natural: biodynamic certification, native yeasts, no added sulfur until bottling, and minimal cellar intervention. The Pinot Noir reflects this approach — nothing is forced or smoothed out, and the result is a wine of genuine delicacy and terroir expression.
trivia: Hanna Glatzer represents a generational shift in Austrian wine — a younger producer taking the family's biodynamic farming philosophy and applying it with even stricter natural winemaking principles in the cellar.

Carnuntum's proximity to Vienna makes it one of the world's few major wine regions within easy cycling distance of a major capital city — the local wine scene benefits from an educated, curious urban consumer base.

The same Hanna Glatzer Weiss Traminer appears in the Natural Whites section of the white wine list — having both the white and red from the same producer is an unusual opportunity to compare how the same natural winemaking approach expresses itself across two completely different varieties.

name: Zweigelt, Styria
tableside: A 2013 natural Zweigelt from Muster — now over ten years old. The Musters make several of the most celebrated natural whites on the list; this is their red, and the same philosophy applies: certified biodynamic, minimal intervention, aged without new oak. The age on this bottle has produced something genuinely complex and tertiary.
story: Maria and Sepp Muster farm in Schlossberg in the Südsteiermark, producing some of the most sought-after natural wines in Austria. Their approach is consistent across all their wines: biodynamic viticulture, spontaneous fermentation, extended aging in large old oak vessels, and bottling without fining or filtration. The 2013 Zweigelt has had a decade to develop complexity that young natural reds rarely show.
trivia: The Muster estate in Schlossberg is one of the most intensely followed natural wine addresses in Austria — their whites (Morillon, Sauvignon Blanc, 'Erde', 'Sgaminegg') appear in the natural whites section of the wine list, making this one of the most extensively represented natural wine producers across the full list.

At over ten years of age, this is one of the oldest natural red wines on the list. Natural wines — made without significant sulfur additions — can age differently from conventional wines, developing complex tertiary character that surprises guests who assume natural means fragile.

Zweigelt in Styria grows in quite different conditions from Burgenland or Carnuntum — the cooler, hillier Styrian climate gives the grape more freshness and herbal character than the warmer lake-region expressions.

name: Zweigelt 'Rot No. 9,' Styria
tableside: Franz Strohmeier is one of the most uncompromising natural wine producers in Austria — a genuine original who has been farming biodynamically since before it was fashionable. Rot No. 9 is his flagship red: Zweigelt made with total conviction. Lighter and more aromatic than conventional Austrian red, with a vibrancy that makes it unlike anything else on the list.
story: Franz Strohmeier farms steep slate vineyards in the West Styrian hills near Deutschlandsberg, making wines that consistently divide opinion and consistently reward the open-minded. His approach is extreme even by natural wine standards: no added sulfur at any stage, native yeasts, extended maceration for the reds, and aging in old large-format vessels. The Rot No. 9 designation refers to his internal barrel-numbering system — each wine is numbered rather than named by conventional variety or vineyard.
trivia: Franz Strohmeier's wines appear on both the natural whites and natural reds sections of this list — the 'Weiss No. 11' and 'Cat-Silver No. 2' are in whites, the 'Rot No. 9' is here. His numbering system rather than conventional naming is a statement of philosophy: the wine is more important than the label.

West Styria (Weststeiermark) is best known for Schilcher — a bracingly acidic rosé made from the indigenous Blauer Wildbacher grape. Strohmeier's work with Zweigelt in the same region is unconventional by local standards.
The slate soils of Strohmeier's vineyards give his wines a mineral, almost electric quality — the same geological formation that produces some of the great Rieslings of the Mosel and the Wachau.

name: Merlot/Cab Sauv/Petit Verdot, Listrac-Médoc
tableside: Entry-level classified Bordeaux from Listrac-Médoc — one of the six communal appellations of the Haut-Médoc. The 2016 vintage is now ten years old and drinking beautifully. A genuine Bordeaux at a fair price for guests who want the real thing without a Premier Cru bill.
story: Listrac-Médoc sits in the middle of the Haut-Médoc, west of the more famous communes of Pauillac and St-Julien. The soils here are heavier and the wines tend toward a more rustic, earthy style than the polished grands châteaux. Château Ducluzeau produces honest, food-friendly Bordeaux from a blend that leans on Merlot for roundness with Cabernet Sauvignon providing structure.
trivia: Listrac-Médoc and Moulis-en-Médoc are the only two of the six Haut-Médoc communal appellations with no Cru Classé estates — a historical quirk of the 1855 classification that excluded these villages despite their quality potential.

The 2016 Bordeaux vintage is widely considered one of the greatest of the 21st century — a near-perfect growing season produced wines of exceptional concentration, balance, and aging potential across all appellations.
Merlot-dominant Bordeaux blends tend to be more approachable younger than Cabernet-dominant ones — the grape's softer tannins and plush fruit make it a gentler introduction to the Bordeaux style.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot, Pauillac
tableside: A Fifth Growth Pauillac from the legendary 2016 vintage — now ten years old and entering its drinking window. Pauillac is the commune that contains three of Bordeaux's five First Growths: Lafite, Latour, and Mouton. Grand-Puy Ducasse gives you genuine Pauillac character at a fraction of those prices.
story: Château Grand-Puy Ducasse is one of the Médoc's Cinquièmes Crus — Fifth Growth in the famous 1855 classification that ranked Bordeaux's best estates. While the First Growths get the headlines, Fifth Growth Pauillac at a good vintage offers extraordinary value for genuine classed-growth Bordeaux. The estate sits in the heart of Pauillac, sharing the appellation with Lafite-Rothschild and Latour.
trivia: The 1855 Classification of Bordeaux — commissioned by Napoleon III for the Paris World Exhibition — divided the top Médoc châteaux into five growths. Only one change has been made in nearly 170 years: Mouton Rothschild was elevated from Second to First Growth in 1973.

Pauillac's soils are dominated by deep gravel beds that drain quickly and force vine roots deep — the same conditions that make it ideal for Cabernet Sauvignon, which needs well-drained soils and a long growing season.

The 2016 Bordeaux vintage is considered by many critics to be the greatest since 2009 and 2010 — exceptional growing conditions across the entire Gironde produced wines of remarkable concentration and elegance.

name: Merlot/Malbec/Cab Franc, Blaye
tableside: The most affordable Bordeaux on the list — right-bank Blaye, Merlot-dominant, and designed for drinking now rather than cellaring. Good for tables that want genuine French red at a friendly price. A solid by-the-glass recommendation if guests ask for something French.
story: Blaye (formally Blaye Côtes de Bordeaux) sits on the right bank of the Gironde estuary, directly across from the Médoc. It's one of Bordeaux's less glamorous appellations — no classified estates, no famous châteaux — but it produces honest, food-friendly Merlot-based wine at prices that make Bordeaux accessible to everyday dining.
trivia: Blaye's strategic position on the Gironde made it an important fortress town — the Citadelle de Blaye, built by Vauban in the 17th century, is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The region's wine culture predates the fortress by centuries.

The right bank of Bordeaux (Blaye, Bourg, Fronsac, Pomerol, St-Emilion) is dominated by Merlot and Cabernet Franc, while the left bank (Médoc, Graves) is dominated by Cabernet Sauvignon — a fundamental division that explains why right-bank wines tend to be softer and more approachable young.

The 2022 Bordeaux vintage was controversial — the extreme heat of summer 2022 produced very ripe wines that some critics praised for concentration and others criticized for imbalance. At the entry level, 2022 Bordeaux tends to be plush and immediately approachable.

name: Merlot/Cab Franc/Cab Sauv/Malbec, St-Émilion
tableside: St-Émilion Grand Cru from a smaller estate — right-bank Bordeaux at its most food-friendly. The 2019 vintage was excellent. Merlot-dominant with Cab Franc adding the herbal, pencil-lead lift that distinguishes right-bank Bordeaux from the more austere left bank.
story: St-Émilion is the most democratic of Bordeaux's major appellations — its classification system is reviewed every decade (unlike the 1855 Médoc classification, which has barely changed in 170 years), and the Grand Cru designation covers hundreds of estates ranging from tiny family producers to internationally famous names. Haut-Rocher is a smaller estate making wines that reflect the appellation's Merlot-and-limestone character.
trivia: St-Émilion's classification is unique in Bordeaux for being periodically revised — estates can be promoted or demoted. The most recent revision in 2022 was legally challenged by demoted châteaux, creating one of Bordeaux's most dramatic classification controversies in decades.
The town of St-Émilion itself is a UNESCO World Heritage Site — its medieval streets, underground church, and catacombs are among the most visited wine tourism destinations in France.

The 2019 Bordeaux vintage was universally praised — warm and dry with good acid retention, producing wines of excellent concentration and structure that are drinking well now and will continue to improve.

name: Merlot/Cab Franc/Petit Verdot 'Le Lion,' Pomerol
tableside: Pomerol is the smallest and most intimate of Bordeaux's great appellations — no classification system, no grand châteaux in the architectural sense, just small family estates on a plateau of clay and iron soils producing some of the world's most distinctive Merlot. Le Lion is the estate's second wine: accessible, silky, and genuinely lovely.
story: Château La Fleur de Boüard has holdings in both Lalande-de-Pomerol and the fringes of Pomerol proper. 'Le Lion' is the second wine — from younger vines and second-selection lots — offering access to the estate's Pomerol philosophy at a price that makes the appellation approachable. The 2022 vintage produced wines that are rich and immediately appealing.
trivia: Pomerol has no official classification — unlike the Médoc (1855) and St-Émilion (reviewed periodically), Pomerol has never submitted to a ranking system. This means Pétrus, the world's most expensive Bordeaux, has no official classification at all.

The iron-rich clay soils of Pomerol — especially the famous 'crasse de fer' (iron crust) layer beneath Pétrus — are thought to be central to the appellation's distinctive wine character. The iron retains heat and moisture, allowing Merlot to ripen fully even in cooler years.
Pomerol's most famous estate, Pétrus, produces only about 30,000 bottles per year from just 11.4 hectares — one of the smallest production volumes among the world's most sought-after wines.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot, Pessac-Léognan
tableside: The 2016 La Louvière — two years older than the 2018 and in a more developed stage. The 2016 Bordeaux vintage is legendary; this wine has had ten years to integrate and shows genuine secondary complexity. For guests who want to compare vintages from the same estate, or who specifically want aged Bordeaux.
story: Having both the 2016 and 2018 La Louvière on the list is an unusual opportunity — two highly regarded vintages from the same estate at different points in their development. The 2016 is further along, showing more complexity and integration; the 2018 is still primary and structured. Two different expressions of the same terroir.
trivia: The 2016 Bordeaux vintage is considered by most critics to be the best since 2010 — exceptional ripeness combined with unusually good acidity produced wines that are both concentrated and elegant.

Having two vintages of the same Bordeaux estate on a restaurant list is rare enough to be worth flagging to guests — it's an implicit invitation to compare what vintage does to a wine without changing the producer or the terroir.
Graves (the broader appellation that encompasses Pessac-Léognan) takes its name from the gravelly soils that define the region — 'graves' simply means 'gravel' in French.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot, St-Estèphe
tableside: A 2005 St-Estèphe — twenty-one years old and one of the most historically significant vintages of the 21st century. St-Estèphe is Bordeaux's most structured commune: more tannin, more mineral, longer aging requirement than its neighbors. This is a genuinely rare opportunity to drink properly aged Bordeaux from a great year.
story: Château Meyney is one of St-Estèphe's most consistently reliable estates — not a classified growth, but consistently performing at Cru Bourgeois Exceptionnel level. The 2005 is now nineteen years old and in a beautiful mature drinking window: the primary fruit has receded, secondary characters of cedar, tobacco, and earth have emerged, and the tannins have softened into something approaching velvet.
trivia: The 2005 Bordeaux vintage is one of the most celebrated of modern times — a hot, dry summer produced wines of exceptional concentration and structure that critics predicted would age for decades. Twenty-one years on, the best bottles are proving them right.

St-Estèphe is the northernmost of the four major Médoc communal appellations. Its heavier clay soils produce wines with more tannin and less immediate approachability than Pauillac or St-Julien, but with exceptional aging potential.
Château Meyney has been owned by the Cordier family for decades and maintains one of the most consistent track records in St-Estèphe — a reliable producer that over-delivers relative to its price.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot/Petit Verdot, St-Estèphe
tableside: The 2022 Meyney — the most recent vintage, still very primary and structured. A significant contrast with the 2005 on the list: same producer, same terroir, seventeen years apart. For guests interested in how Bordeaux ages, this pairing tells the whole story.
story: Having the 2005 and 2022 Meyney on the list simultaneously is one of the most educational pairings on the entire red wine list — the same château, the same soils, but one wine at nineteen years of age and one at two. The contrast between primary cassis fruit and evolved cedar-and-tobacco complexity is Bordeaux aging in a single side-by-side comparison.
trivia: The 2022 Bordeaux growing season was one of the hottest and driest on record in the Gironde — some critics worried about overripeness, but structured appellations like St-Estèphe, with their heavy clay soils that retain moisture, fared particularly well.

Having the 2005 and 2022 Meyney available simultaneously is a teaching opportunity that most wine lists don't offer — it's essentially a seventeen-year vertical from a single reliable producer at the same price tier.

Petit Verdot, added to the 2022 blend, contributes color intensity and spice — its use has become more common in Bordeaux as warming temperatures allow it to ripen more consistently than in past decades.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot 'La Chapelle,' St-Estèphe
tableside: La Chapelle is Meyney's second wine — lighter and more approachable than the grand vin, from younger vines and second-selection lots. The 2018 is eight years old and drinking well now. A good entry point into Meyney's St-Estèphe style without the aging requirement of the grand vin.
story: Second wines in Bordeaux serve the same function as second labels in California — they offer access to an estate's terroir and philosophy at a lower price and earlier drinking window. La Chapelle draws its name from the historic chapel on the Meyney property, one of the oldest continuously operated winery buildings in St-Estèphe.
trivia: Three vintages of Château Meyney on the list — La Chapelle 2018, grand vin 2022, and grand vin 2005 — make it the most extensively represented Bordeaux estate in the cellar. It's an unusual opportunity to understand a single producer across three very different expressions.

Second wines in Bordeaux originated as a way for estates to maintain quality standards for the grand vin by declassifying lots that didn't meet the bar. Over time, second wines have developed their own followings and market.
The chapel on the Meyney estate dates to the 17th century — the estate's full name, Prieuré de Saint-Estèphe, references the religious community that originally farmed the land.

name: Merlot, Blaye-Côtes de Bordeaux
tableside: The lightest, most immediately drinkable Bordeaux on the list — pure Merlot from Blaye, no heavy oak, no demanding tannins. For tables that want something French and red but approachable enough for lighter food or guests who typically prefer white wine.
story: Château Mondesir is a small Blaye estate producing fresh, fruit-driven Merlot in a style that prioritizes immediacy over complexity. At this price and with this style, it fills an important gap on the list — a genuine French red for guests who find Bordeaux too heavy or too expensive.
trivia: Blaye's right-bank location across the Gironde from the Médoc means it benefits from similar Atlantic maritime influence, but its heavier soils and lesser reputation keep prices accessible.
Pure varietal Merlot is unusual in Bordeaux, where blending is the norm — but for easy-drinking styles, single-variety Merlot offers cleaner, more immediate fruit character than complex blends.

The Côtes de Bordeaux designation was created in 2009 to group several right-bank appellations (Blaye, Castillon, Cadillac, Francs) under a common umbrella identity, making the wines easier to market internationally.

name: Malbec 'Bardon,' Blaye-Côtes de Bordeaux
tableside: Bordeaux Malbec — unusual enough that guests will ask about it. Malbec was historically grown throughout Bordeaux but nearly disappeared after phylloxera. Mondesir-Gazin is one of a handful of estates reviving it as a varietal wine. A great talking point and genuinely distinctive.
story: Malbec's Bordeaux origins are often forgotten now that Argentina has claimed it as a national variety. Château Mondesir-Gazin deliberately cultivates old Malbec vines on the right bank and produces 'Bardon' as a tribute to the variety's French heritage. The wine has more freshness and herbal complexity than Argentine Malbec — closer to what the variety would have tasted like in its original home.
trivia: Malbec (also called Côt or Auxerrois in France) was once widely planted throughout Bordeaux and the Loire Valley before phylloxera and harsh winters dramatically reduced plantings in the late 19th century. It survived primarily in Cahors, southwest of Bordeaux, where it's still called Côt.
The contrast between this Bordeaux Malbec and the Alta Vista Argentine Malbec on the list illustrates how dramatically terroir can transform a single variety — same grape, entirely different wine.
Blaye's right-bank soils are better suited to Malbec than the Médoc's gravel — the variety prefers clay and limestone, which Blaye has in abundance.

name: Syrah/Grenache 'Pharaon,' Maury
tableside: Dave Phinney again — the same winemaker behind the Our Lady of Guadalupe Sta. Rita Hills Pinot on the list — but here making ambitious southern French red from Maury in the Roussillon. 'Department 66' refers to the département number of Pyrénées-Orientales. The 2015 is now eleven years old and at peak.
story: Dave Phinney has built multiple successful wine projects across California and France. Department 66 reflects his fascination with the Roussillon — the wild, sun-baked southern French wine region that borders Spain and produces wines of extraordinary concentration from old Grenache and Syrah vines. Maury is one of the appellation's most prized addresses, historically famous for its fortified wines but increasingly respected for dry reds.
trivia: Maury is famous primarily for its Vin Doux Naturel (VDN) — a fortified wine style made from Grenache that's been produced in the area since the 14th century. The modern trend toward dry reds from the same vineyards is relatively recent.

The 'Department 66' name refers to the administrative department number of Pyrénées-Orientales, the French department that encompasses the Roussillon. It's a reference that only makes sense to those who know French administrative geography — deliberately obscure in a way that rewards the curious.

Maury's schist soils and extreme southern climate produce old-vine Grenache and Syrah of remarkable concentration — the same geological conditions that make neighboring Priorat famous on the Spanish side of the Pyrenees.

name: Syrah/Grenache/Cinsault 'Chemin de Moscou,' Pays d'Oc
tableside: A Languedoc blend from a biodynamically-farmed estate near Carcassonne. 'Chemin de Moscou' — the road to Moscow — is named for a local trail that runs through the estate. Syrah, Grenache, and Cinsault in a blend that's more elegant and food-friendly than most southern French reds. Good for guests who want something European and savory.
story: Domaine Gayda is an international project — founded by British and South African partners who fell in love with the Languedoc's potential — that has become one of the region's benchmark estates for quality and sustainability. Their vineyards are farmed biodynamically, and the Chemin de Moscou blend reflects their belief that Languedoc can produce wines of genuine finesse rather than just power.
trivia: The Languedoc is France's largest wine region by volume — it produces more wine than all of Australia and has historically been associated with bulk production. The quality revolution of the past three decades, driven by producers like Gayda, has transformed its international reputation.

'Cinsault' (also spelled Cinsaut) is one of the Languedoc's oldest and most traditional varieties — it contributes freshness and aromatic lift to blends, qualities it also passes on to Pinotage, the South African variety that is a crossing of Cinsault and Pinot Noir.

The Pays d'Oc IGP covers the entire Languedoc-Roussillon region and is the most commercially successful French wine designation outside the classic appellations — its flexibility allows producers to label wines by grape variety, making them easier to sell internationally.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre/Cinsaut, Gigondas
tableside: Gigondas is the Southern Rhône's most underrated appellation — neighboring Châteauneuf-du-Pape gets all the attention, but Gigondas produces wines of comparable quality at significantly lower prices. Saint Cosme is one of the benchmark producers. A great recommendation for guests who love Rhône but don't want to pay Châteauneuf prices.
story: Château de Saint Cosme has been farming in Gigondas since the 16th century — one of the oldest continuously operating wine estates in the Southern Rhône. Louis Barruol, the current owner, has raised the estate's profile significantly while maintaining its roots in the traditional Grenache-dominated blends of the appellation.
trivia: Gigondas was elevated to its own appellation in 1971, having previously been sold as Côtes du Rhône. Its limestone soils and high-altitude vineyards (up to 500 meters) give the wines a freshness and mineral character unusual for the warm Southern Rhône.

The Southern Rhône blend (Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre, often called GSM) is one of wine's most versatile and food-friendly combinations — Grenache provides warmth and red fruit, Syrah adds color and spice, and Mourvèdre contributes structure and savory depth.

Louis Barruol of Saint Cosme is also a highly respected négociant, producing single-vineyard wines from throughout the Northern and Southern Rhône under his own label — one of the most knowledgeable voices in the region.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre, Côtes du Rhône
tableside: The most affordable Rhône on the list — Côtes du Rhône is the broad appellation that covers the entire Rhône Valley, and at this price it's the best entry point to the classic GSM blend. Fresh, herby, and genuinely food-friendly. For tables that want something French and approachable without committing to a serious bottle.
story: Château Malijay is a large estate in the southern Rhône whose Côtes du Rhône represents the everyday face of the appellation — honest, food-friendly, and consistent. The classic GSM blend at this price level is designed for the table rather than the cellar: drink it young, drink it with food, and enjoy it without analysis.
trivia: Côtes du Rhône is France's second-largest appellation by volume after Bordeaux — it covers an enormous geographic area and produces wines ranging from basic table wine to serious, complex expressions. The quality range is vast.

Grenache's natural high alcohol (it ripens easily to 14-15% in the Southern Rhône sun) makes it one of the warmest-feeling varieties in the world — its plush red fruit and round tannins make it one of the most approachable red varieties for new wine drinkers.

The 2022 Southern Rhône vintage was warm and produced wines with excellent fruit concentration — at the entry level, this translates into plush, immediately drinkable wines that over-deliver for their price.

name: Grenache/Syrah, Rhône Valley
tableside: Chêne Bleu is one of the most ambitious estates in the Southern Rhône — a medieval priory at nearly 1,000 meters elevation in the Vaucluse, producing wines that critics have compared to the Northern Rhône's best. This 2013 is now thirteen years old and showing remarkable complexity. An unusual and special bottle.
story: The Chêne Bleu estate is built around a 12th-century priory deep in the mountains of the Vaucluse — at 900-1,000 meters elevation, it's one of the highest-altitude vineyards in the Southern Rhône. The extreme conditions — cold nights, thin soils, dramatic diurnal variation — produce wines with a finesse and mineral complexity quite unlike most southern French red. The winery was restored by Xavier and Nicole Rolet in the early 2000s.
trivia: Chêne Bleu's elevation at nearly 1,000 meters makes it one of the highest-altitude Southern Rhône estates — for context, most Châteauneuf-du-Pape vineyards sit at 50-100 meters. The temperature difference means Chêne Bleu harvests 3-4 weeks later than the valley floor.

The estate's name — 'Blue Oak' — references the ancient oak trees that cover the surrounding forest. The oaks were used for centuries for timber and charcoal production before the estate was converted back to viticulture.

The 2013 Rhône vintage was mixed — a cool, wet summer that challenged many producers but rewarded those with the best-situated vineyards. Chêne Bleu's elevation and north-facing aspects actually benefited from the cooler conditions.

name: Grenache/Syrah, Rhône Valley
tableside: The 2015 Chêne Bleu — a warmer vintage than the 2013, with more concentration and riper fruit while retaining the estate's characteristic mountain minerality. Having both years on the list is an unusual opportunity to compare two very different growing seasons from this exceptional estate.
story: The 2015 Southern Rhône vintage was one of the finest of the decade — warm, ripe, and concentrated, producing wines of extraordinary power and aging potential. For Chêne Bleu specifically, the warm year produced fruit with more density than the cooler 2013, while the estate's altitude kept the wines from becoming overripe or losing their mineral freshness.
trivia: Having both the 2013 and 2015 Chêne Bleu on the list offers a rare opportunity to compare a cool, delicate vintage with a warm, concentrated one from the same estate — essentially a vertical tasting that tells the story of Rhône vintage variation.
The 2015 Rhône vintage is widely considered among the greatest in recent memory — wines from this year have exceptional aging potential and are still evolving in the cellar.
Chêne Bleu produces only a small amount of wine annually given the estate's size — most of it is allocated directly to restaurants and private clients, making any restaurant listing relatively rare.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre, Châteauneuf-du-Pape
tableside: Châteauneuf-du-Pape — the Southern Rhône's most famous appellation and one of France's great wine names. At 15% alcohol, this is a genuinely powerful wine. For guests who want to understand what southern French red means at its most ambitious.
story: Châteauneuf-du-Pape takes its name from the 'new castle of the Pope' — the Avignon papacy moved here in the 14th century and the vineyards supplied the papal table. Today the appellation is famous for wines of extraordinary richness and complexity, built primarily around Grenache with support from Syrah, Mourvèdre, and up to 13 other permitted varieties. Bertrand-Gonnet is a small family estate making traditionally styled CNDP.
trivia: Châteauneuf-du-Pape allows up to 18 different grape varieties in its wines — more than any other major French appellation. In practice, most wines are dominated by Grenache, but the diversity of permitted varieties reflects the appellation's long and complex history.

The large smooth stones (galets roulés) that cover many CNDP vineyard floors are one of the most distinctive viticultural sights in France — they store heat during the day and radiate it at night, helping Grenache ripen fully even in cooler years.
CNDP was one of France's first Appellations d'Origine Contrôlée (AOC) — designated in 1936, making it a founding example of the appellation system that now covers the entire French wine industry.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre 'Barville Secret,' Côtes du Rhône
tableside: Barville Secret is Domaine Brotte's premium Côtes du Rhône — a lighter, more elegant expression of the GSM blend than you'd expect from the Southern Rhône. More floral, more red-fruited, more food-friendly. For guests who want Rhône style without the weight of a full Châteauneuf.
story: Domaine Brotte is one of the Southern Rhône's most respected négociant-domaine operations, with vineyards throughout the Châteauneuf-du-Pape and Côtes du Rhône appellations. The Barville Secret is their premium Côtes du Rhône — sourced from specific plots that produce lighter-bodied, more aromatic wine than the full-bodied flagship expressions.
trivia: Brotte's Père Anselme line is one of the most widely distributed Châteauneuf-du-Pape brands in the world — the family has been in the Rhône wine business for generations and understands the market for both everyday and prestige expressions.

The trend toward lighter, more elegant Côtes du Rhône — sometimes called the 'Burgundy style' of the Southern Rhône — reflects a broader shift in consumer preferences away from extracted, high-alcohol wines toward fresher, more food-friendly expressions.
The 2023 vintage in the Southern Rhône was fresh and aromatic — a relief after the extreme heat of 2022. Wines from 2023 tend to be lighter and more elegant than the previous year.

name: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
tableside: Traditional CNDP from a family estate — full-bodied, spiced, and built for the table. The 2021 vintage in the Southern Rhône was very good. For guests who want classic Châteauneuf-du-Pape without paying for a prestige label.
story: Domaine de Ferrand is a family estate in Châteauneuf-du-Pape that has been making wine for generations in the traditional style — Grenache-dominant, full-bodied, and aged in large neutral oak foudres rather than new barriques. The result is wine that expresses the appellation's character without the overlay of new oak.
trivia: The use of large neutral oak foudres (rather than small new barriques) for aging is the traditional approach in Châteauneuf-du-Pape — it allows the wine to develop slowly without adding oak flavor, letting the terroir and variety speak clearly.
The 2021 Southern Rhône vintage was excellent — a return to more classic conditions after the extreme heat of 2020, with good acid retention and more elegant, food-friendly wines.

Châteauneuf-du-Pape's minimum alcohol requirement of 12.5% is the highest of any French AOC — reflecting the region's consistently warm, sun-baked conditions that naturally push Grenache to high sugar levels.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Vaccarèse 'Mistral,' Côtes du Rhône
tableside: Ferrand's approachable Côtes du Rhône — same estate as the CNDP, lighter fruit from broader appellation sourcing. The Vaccarèse adds an unusual herbal, almost wild note. Good value and a good talking point: Vaccarèse is one of the rarest of Châteauneuf's 18 permitted varieties.
story: The Mistral is named for the powerful north wind that scours the Rhône Valley — the same wind that dries the grapes, concentrates flavors, and prevents rot. Having both the Ferrand CNDP ($132) and the Mistral ($46) on the list gives guests a direct comparison of what appellation designation adds in price and what it takes away in approachability.
trivia: Vaccarèse (also called Brun Argenté) is one of the rarest of Châteauneuf's 18 permitted varieties — planted on only a handful of hectares across the entire appellation. Its inclusion in the Mistral is a genuine rarity worth pointing out to curious guests.

The Mistral wind — which can gust to 90 km/h through the Rhône Valley — is one of the defining climatic features of Provençal and Rhône wine culture. It dries the air, reduces disease pressure, and keeps the vines healthy, contributing to the region's consistent ripeness.

At $46, the Mistral is one of the most affordable French reds on the list. Its obscurity (Vaccarèse!) and genuine quality make it a great staff recommendation for guests who want something interesting at a low price.

name: Châteauneuf-du-Pape
tableside: Elisabeth Chabellan is a small, highly regarded CNDP producer — one of the appellation's best-value estates. The 2022 was a warm, concentrated vintage. Full-bodied, spicy, and unmistakably Southern Rhône. At $89 it's one of the better-priced Châteauneufs on the market.
story: Elisabeth Chabellan produces small quantities of Châteauneuf-du-Pape from estate vineyards that include some old-vine Grenache. The estate is known for traditional winemaking — minimal intervention, large oak aging, and a focus on letting the appellation's character speak. The 2022 vintage was warm and produced wines of excellent concentration.
trivia: Small, family-run CNDP estates like Chabellan often represent better value than the famous names (Château Rayas, Château Beaucastel) because they lack the marketing budgets and international distribution that drives prices up.

At $89, this is one of the most affordable Châteauneuf-du-Pape bottles available from a serious producer — reflecting the estate's size and limited international visibility rather than any compromise in quality.

The 2022 vintage in Châteauneuf-du-Pape was one of the warmest on record. Early harvest dates preserved freshness in some estates; those who waited too long made overripe wines. Chabellan's timing was good.

name: Grenache/Syrah/Mourvèdre, Rhône Valley
tableside: Ogier is one of the Rhône's oldest négociant houses — they have been making wine in the valley since 1859. This Rhône Valley blend is their showcase of what the entire appellation can produce: the warmth and spice of Grenache, the structure of Syrah, the savory depth of Mourvèdre. Good for guests who want a reliable introduction to the Rhône style.
story: Maison Ogier has been a fixture of the Rhône wine trade for over 160 years, with deep roots in Ampuis — the village at the heart of the Northern Rhône's Côte-Rôtie appellation. While their most famous wines are from the Northern Rhône, their Southern Rhône blend reflects the same commitment to quality and the same generational knowledge of the valley.
trivia: Ogier's history in Ampuis gives them access to some of the most prestigious Northern Rhône sites — their Côte-Rôtie expressions are among the benchmarks for that appellation. The Southern Rhône blend is their accessible face.

The Rhône Valley is one of the world's most geographically diverse wine regions — the Northern Rhône (cool, granite-based, Syrah-dominated) and Southern Rhône (warm, galets-covered, Grenache-dominated) produce wines so different that they could be from different countries.
The 2018 Southern Rhône vintage was warm and produced wines of excellent concentration — they have had time to integrate and are now showing the complexity that comes with a few years of bottle age.

name: Napa Valley — Magnum
tableside: A 2001 Dominus in magnum — twenty-five years old and one of the most remarkable bottles on the list. Dominus is the Napa project of Christian Moueix, who also makes Pétrus in Bordeaux. The 2001 is a great vintage and this is now in its full mature expression: primary fruit long gone, complex secondary characters of cedar, tobacco, and earth fully developed. For a table marking a very special occasion.
story: Christian Moueix of Pétrus fame created Dominus in the Yountville appellation of Napa Valley in the 1980s, bringing Bordeaux philosophy to California terroir. The estate is farmed sustainably and the wines are made with the same precision and restraint that defines Moueix's Bordeaux approach. The 2001 vintage was one of Napa's finest of the decade — and in magnum, over twenty years of slow development has produced something genuinely exceptional.
trivia: Christian Moueix runs both Dominus in Napa and the Pétrus estate in Pomerol — one of the very few winemakers simultaneously responsible for two of the world's most iconic wines.

The 2001 Napa vintage came just after September 11, 2001 — a year in which many in the wine industry feared that the restaurant and luxury goods markets would collapse. The wines that were made that year have aged with remarkable grace.

A 2001 Napa Cabernet in magnum is now twenty-five years old — approaching the age at which even the most structured Cabernet blends begin to show true tertiary complexity. This is a genuinely historic bottle.

name: 'Cask 23,' Napa Valley — Magnum
tableside: Cask 23 is the wine that won the 1976 Paris Tasting — the red Cabernet that beat the First Growths alongside Montelena's Chardonnay. This 2008 is eighteen years old, in magnum, and at full development. The most historically significant bottle on the list. For a table that knows its wine history.
story: Warren Winiarski's Stag's Leap Wine Cellars 1973 Cabernet Sauvignon won the red wine category at the 1976 Paris Tasting — the moment that put California wine on the world map. Cask 23 is the estate's prestige expression, made from the best lots of their Stags Leap District estate. The 2008 vintage was excellent in Napa, and in magnum, sixteen years of slow aging has produced a wine of extraordinary complexity.
trivia: Stag's Leap Wine Cellars' 1973 Cabernet winning the 1976 Paris Tasting is one of the most consequential moments in wine history — it proved definitively that California could produce wines that competed with the greatest in the world. Warren Winiarski later sold the winery to Ste. Michelle Wine Estates and Antinori for $185 million in 2007.

Cask 23 takes its name from the specific cask number — 23 — that Warren Winiarski identified as producing the finest wine in the cellar in the early years. The name has been retained as the prestige label ever since.
At $855, this is the most expensive wine on the reds list. It reflects the historical significance of the estate, the magnum format, and eighteen years of cellar aging.

name: Grenache/Carignan/Syrah/Cab, Priorat DOCa — Magnum
tableside: René Barbier's flagship Priorat in magnum — the wine that helped reinvent a forgotten wine region in the late 1980s. The 2014 is now twelve years old and drinking magnificently. In magnum, the slow aging has amplified the wine's complexity. One of Spain's great bottles in its most impressive format.
story: Clos Mogador is the flagship wine of René Barbier's estate — the producer who, along with four others in 1989, essentially relaunched the Priorat wine region from scratch. Made from old-vine Grenache and Carignan planted on the llicorella slate-and-quartz soils that define Priorat, with Syrah and Cabernet adding complexity to the blend. In magnum, the 2014 has had a decade to develop the mineral depth and secondary complexity that make Priorat's best wines comparable to the great wines of Burgundy and Bordeaux.
trivia: René Barbier's role in relaunching Priorat in 1989 is one of wine history's great entrepreneurial stories — he convinced four friends to invest in a forgotten mountain region with ancient vines and impossible-to-farm slopes, and produced wines that shocked the wine world with their quality.

The 2014 Priorat vintage was considered excellent — cool temperatures that year preserved freshness in a region that can sometimes overheat, producing wines of unusual elegance alongside the expected power.

Priorat and Rioja are the only two wine regions in Spain with DOCa (Denominación de Origen Calificada) status — the highest classification in Spanish wine law, equivalent to Bordeaux's Grand Cru in terms of regulatory prestige.

name: Tempranillo/Graciano/Mazuelo, Gran Reserva, Rioja
tableside: A 2010 Cune Gran Reserva — sixteen years old and at its absolute peak. The same producer as the 2019 Gran Reserva in the Spanish Reds section, but a full nine years older and the difference is profound: where the 2019 still shows primary fruit, the 2010 has fully evolved into the classic aged Rioja profile — dried fruit, tobacco, leather, and vanilla from decades in American oak. A genuinely great bottle.
story: The 2010 Rioja vintage is considered one of the greatest in recent memory — cool and consistent, producing wines of exceptional structure and longevity. Cune Gran Reserva from 2010, at fourteen years, is now showing what traditional aged Rioja becomes at peak: the primary Tempranillo fruit has given way to a complex interplay of dried fruit, tobacco, and the distinctive vanilla and dill character from decades of American oak aging. In the large format bottle, this development has happened more slowly, producing even greater complexity.
trivia: The 2010 Rioja vintage is widely considered the finest since 1994 — a cool growing season produced wines of exceptional balance, concentration, and longevity. Gran Reservas from this year are now at their peak.

Having both the 2010 (here) and the 2019 (in the Spanish Reds section) Cune Gran Reserva on the list creates one of the most instructive vintage comparison opportunities on the entire list — nine years apart, same producer, entirely different stages of development.

Gran Reserva Rioja must age for a minimum of five years before release — this 2010 was likely released around 2016 and has then continued to develop in the cellar. The total development time at the table is sixteen years.

name: Abbot Claim Vineyard, Willamette Valley — 5 Liter
tableside: A 5-liter Jeroboam of Ken Wright's Abbot Claim Pinot Noir — the largest format bottle on the list and a genuinely spectacular table piece. Ken Wright is one of Oregon's most meticulous Pinot Noir producers, known for making single-vineyard wines that demonstrate place over winemaker. In this format, it's the ultimate celebration bottle for a large table.
story: Ken Wright Cellars is among Oregon's most celebrated Pinot Noir producers — Ken Wright was one of the pioneers who demonstrated that individual vineyard sites in the Willamette Valley produce distinctly different wines, and his single-vineyard lineup remains one of the most compelling in Oregon. Abbot Claim is a specific vineyard in the Chehalem Mountains whose combination of volcanic Jory soils and ideal exposition produces Pinot of exceptional elegance. In a 5-liter format, it's one of the most impressive celebration bottles in the Pacific Northwest.
trivia: A 5-liter bottle (also called a Jeroboam in the still wine world, though the term is used differently for Champagne) ages even more slowly than a magnum or double magnum — the ratio of wine to oxygen is at its lowest, producing the most gradual and complex development possible.
Ken Wright makes single-vineyard Pinot from over a dozen different Oregon sites — a research-focused approach that has produced some of the most detailed terroir mapping in American wine.

The 2018 Oregon vintage was warm and produced wines of excellent concentration. Six years on, the Abbot Claim is showing beautiful development — the primary cherry fruit has softened and the site's characteristic mineral depth is emerging.

name: 'Southing,' Sta. Rita Hills — Magnum
tableside: Sea Smoke Southing in magnum — the most recent vintage of one of California's iconic Pinot Noirs, in a format built for a special table. The 2023 is the same wine as the 750ml Southing in the Pinot Noir section, but the magnum will develop more slowly and more gracefully. For a table that wants to mark an occasion with something genuinely impressive.
story: Sea Smoke's Southing is one of California's most sought-after Pinot Noirs — made from estate vineyards at the bend in the Sta. Rita Hills valley where the Pacific fog is most intense, it consistently delivers the richness and concentration that define the estate's style. In magnum, the 2023 will develop more slowly than the standard bottle, rewarding cellaring or making a spectacular impression at a large dinner.
trivia: Sea Smoke holds wines back before release and sells primarily through a mailing list — getting a magnum of current-vintage Southing into a restaurant is genuinely unusual.

Magnums of California Pinot Noir are rarely produced — most producers focus on 750ml for practical and commercial reasons. Sea Smoke's commitment to magnum production reflects the estate's confidence in their wine's aging potential.
The 2023 Santa Barbara vintage was characterized by consistent, moderate temperatures — producing wines with natural freshness and excellent structure that will age gracefully in any format.

name: Blanc de Blancs, 2014
tableside: A vintage Blanc de Blancs from Delamotte, the sister house of Salon. The 2014 is twelve years old and showing beautifully: lemon curd, white peach, toasted brioche, and a persistent chalky finish. The kind of texture and depth that non-vintage Champagne simply cannot match.
story: Delamotte and Salon share the same cellar, the same winemaking team, and the same grand cru Chardonnay grapes from Le Mesnil-sur-Oger. In years when Salon is not produced, the finest lots go into the Delamotte vintage. The 2014 is one of those bottles — a wine made to last.
trivia: Founded in 1760 by François Delamotte, the house is one of the five oldest in Champagne — predating most of the famous names by decades.
The 2014 Champagne vintage was initially overlooked in favor of the flashier 2012 and 2015, but has proven itself as a wine of precision and longevity.
Le Mesnil-sur-Oger grand cru is widely considered the greatest terroir for Chardonnay in Champagne — the chalk here is deeper and purer than almost anywhere else in the appellation.

name: Brut, NV
tableside: Delamotte's Brut NV is primarily Chardonnay with small additions of Pinot Noir and Meunier for body. Citrus zest, green apple, toasted almond, and a clean chalky finish. Genuinely elegant at the price, drawing on the same Le Mesnil-sur-Oger grand cru philosophy as the vintage bottles.
story: Delamotte sits in the shadow of its more famous sibling Salon, but that shadow is misleading — the house makes exceptional Champagne in its own right, drawing on grand cru vineyards in the Côte des Blancs. The Brut NV is their approachable face, but the winemaking philosophy is identical throughout.
trivia: Founded in 1760 by François Delamotte, the house is one of the five oldest in Champagne — the Brut NV draws on the same Le Mesnil-sur-Oger grand cru philosophy as the vintage bottles.
Delamotte and Salon share the same cellar and winemaking team — the Brut NV is made with the same rigorous attention that goes into one of Champagne's rarest prestige cuvées.
Primarily Chardonnay-dominant, the Brut NV is an unusually transparent expression of Le Mesnil terroir at an accessible price point.

name: Blanc de Blancs, Le Mesnil, 2015
tableside: One of the rarest bottles in Champagne. Salon produces a single wine from a single vineyard in a single vintage, only when conditions are perfect. White flowers, lemon oil, wet chalk, toasted hazelnut, and a finish that doesn't quit. Nothing else on this list tastes like it.
story: Salon was founded in 1911 with a radical philosophy: one wine, one vineyard, one vintage, one grape variety. No blending across years, no Pinot Noir or Meunier, no compromise. In the hundred-plus years since, fewer than 50 vintages have been produced. The 2015 joins a list of bottles that define what Champagne can be at its absolute apex.
trivia: Salon has only produced approximately 45 vintages since its founding in 1911 — in years that don't meet the standard, no wine is released at all.
The Le Mesnil-sur-Oger grand cru chalk soils are among the deepest in Champagne — vines here access water from dozens of meters below the surface, maintaining freshness in warm years.
Salon and Delamotte share a cellar and ownership — when Salon is not produced, the finest Le Mesnil lots go into the Delamotte vintage Blanc de Blancs instead.

name: Pinot Blanc, Willamette Valley, 2024
tableside: Elk Cove's Pinot Blanc sits between Pinot Gris and Chardonnay in character. Pear, white peach, almond blossom, and a squeeze of lemon on the finish. Light, dry, and very food-friendly. One of the more unusual whites on the list and worth the conversation.
story: Pinot Blanc is a natural mutation of Pinot Gris, which is itself a mutation of Pinot Noir — making it genetically very close to Oregon's most celebrated red variety. Elk Cove has farmed this variety for decades, producing a style that emphasizes freshness and delicacy over richness.
trivia: Pinot Blanc is genetically almost identical to Pinot Gris — the difference in color and style comes from a single gene mutation affecting pigmentation in the grape skin.
Oregon's cool climate is particularly well-suited to Pinot Blanc, preserving the variety's natural acidity in a way that warmer regions cannot.
Elk Cove is one of the few Oregon producers to maintain a significant Pinot Blanc program — most wineries focus almost exclusively on Pinot Noir and Chardonnay.

name: Pinot Gris, Yamhill-Carlton, 2024
tableside: Oregon Pinot Gris at its best is nothing like Italian Pinot Grigio. Elk Cove's Yamhill-Carlton expression has stone fruit, honeydew, and a ginger spice note with real texture and weight. Broader and more satisfying than most whites at this price.
story: Yamhill-Carlton is one of the Willamette Valley's most respected sub-appellations, known for its ancient marine sedimentary soils that produce wines of distinctive weight and mineral character. Elk Cove has farmed here for decades, and their Pinot Gris shows what the variety can do when grown in the right place.
trivia: Yamhill-Carlton received its own AVA designation in 2005, recognizing the distinctive Willakenzie soil series — an ancient marine sedimentary deposit that produces wines of unusual richness.
Oregon Pinot Gris has its own identity distinct from both Alsatian and Italian versions — fuller than Grigio, drier than many Alsatian examples.
Elk Cove was among the first Oregon producers to take Pinot Gris seriously as a variety capable of fine wine production rather than easy everyday drinking.

name: 'Five Mountain,' Chehalem Mountains, 2023
tableside: Elk Cove's Five Mountain vineyard in the Chehalem Mountains produces Pinot with red cherry, dried rose, and forest floor complexity. Silky tannins, bright acidity, and a long finish. Classic Oregon Pinot from one of the state's founding estates.
story: The Five Mountain vineyard is one of Elk Cove's flagship sites, planted at elevation in the Chehalem Mountains where cooler temperatures and complex soils produce Pinot Noir of genuine distinction. The estate has farmed this land since the 1970s, giving them a depth of site knowledge that shows in the wine.
trivia: The Chehalem Mountains AVA encompasses three distinct soil types — volcanic Jory, ancient marine Willakenzie, and Laurelwood loess — each producing Pinot Noir with different character from the same appellation.
Elk Cove was founded in 1974, making it one of the pioneering estates of the Oregon wine industry — the current generation of winemakers works with vine material their parents planted.

Single-vineyard Pinot Noir from established Oregon estates like Elk Cove typically outperforms wines at twice the price from younger California producers simply by virtue of vine age and site knowledge.

name: 'Clay Court,' Chehalem Mountains, 2024
tableside: Clay Court sits on Willakenzie marine sedimentary soils that give this Pinot more structure and earthiness than the volcanic soil sites. Dark cherry, iron mineral, dried herb, and a tannic backbone that needs food. More brooding than the Five Mountain, built for the long haul.
story: Clay Court sits on Willakenzie soil — ancient marine sedimentary deposits laid down when this part of Oregon was an inland sea. These soils drain poorly compared to volcanic Jory, forcing vines to work harder and producing wines of unusual intensity and mineral character.
trivia: Willakenzie soil is named for the Willamette Valley and the McKenzie River — a portmanteau that reflects the complex geological history of the region.
Clay soils moderate vine stress during hot spells by retaining moisture — in warm vintages like 2024, this can be the difference between an elegant wine and an overripe one.
Elk Cove produces several single-vineyard Pinot Noirs simultaneously, allowing a genuine comparison of how soil type shapes flavor from the same producer and vintage.

name: 'Blue Dog,' Dry Creek Valley, Sonoma, 2021
region: Dry Creek Valley, Sonoma County, California
tableside: Sonoma Cabernet from the Dry Creek Valley, where the style runs leaner and more structured than Napa valley floor wines. Blackcurrant, dark plum, cedar, and dried herb with firm tannin that wants food. Adobe Road's Blue Dog is a serious bottle at a price that makes it easy to recommend.
