story: Dry Creek Valley has long been known for Zinfandel, but its Cabernet Sauvignon program is increasingly attracting attention. The valley's warm days and cool nights — moderated by fog from the Petaluma Gap — produce Cabernet with more freshness and structure than many Napa counterparts.
trivia: Dry Creek Valley was established as an AVA in 1983, one of the first wine regions in California to receive the designation — a recognition of its distinct identity as a Zinfandel and Cabernet producer.
Adobe Road was founded by Kevin Buckler, a professional race car driver who brings the same precision-focused approach to winemaking.
Sonoma Cabernet typically shows more herbal, savory character than Napa — the result of cooler temperatures and the influence of the Pacific.

name: 'The Bench,' Napa Valley, 2023
tableside: Pahlmeyer's most accessible Cabernet, sourced from across Napa rather than the estate's most prestigious blocks. Black cherry, cassis, vanilla oak, and soft tannin. More immediate and generous than the Jayson, and considerably easier to commit to.
story: Pahlmeyer built its reputation on powerful, structured Napa Cabernet, but The Bench fills a different role — an introduction to the house style at a price that makes it genuinely accessible. The 2023 vintage was warm and generous in Napa, producing wines of immediate appeal.
trivia: Pahlmeyer was founded by Jayson Pahlmeyer in 1986 and became one of the first 'cult' Napa Cabernets — wines produced in small quantities that developed a waiting list before they were ever released.

The Bench designation refers to the elevated benchland terraces that run along the hillsides of Napa Valley — not as prestigious as hillside sites but producing wines of more consistency than valley floor vineyards.
Napa Valley's 2023 vintage was marked by good winter rains ending a prolonged drought, producing wines of generous fruit and good structural balance.

name: 'Optimus,' Syrah/Cab Sauv/Petit Verdot, Paso Robles, 2022
tableside: L'Aventure is a French producer who found in Paso Robles the freedom to blend Syrah, Cabernet, and Petit Verdot simultaneously. Optimus delivers dark plum, black olive, cracked pepper, and toasted oak. Paso Robles concentration with serious winemaking behind it.
story: Stephan Asseo left Bordeaux in the late 1990s, frustrated by the AOC restrictions on what he could plant and blend. He found in Paso Robles the freedom to work with Syrah, Cabernet, Grenache, and Petit Verdot simultaneously — producing wines that don't fit any European category but stand on their own terms.
trivia: Stephan Asseo left France specifically because Bordeaux regulations prohibited him from blending Rhône varieties like Syrah with Bordeaux varieties like Cabernet — something he considered essential to his vision.

Paso Robles sits on a similar latitude to the southern Rhône but has a continental climate rather than Mediterranean — the diurnal temperature swings can exceed 50°F, preserving freshness in very ripe grapes.
L'Aventure — French for 'the adventure' — references Asseo's decision to leave the certainty of French winemaking tradition for the unknown of California.

name: Glou d'État, Valdiguié/Mourvèdre/Grenache, Sonoma, 2023
tableside: Jolie-Laide makes wines from obscure French varieties almost nobody else grows in California. Glou d'État is built on Valdiguié with Mourvèdre and Grenache: strawberry, cranberry, violet, and white pepper at 12.5% alcohol. Light, chillable, and genuinely fun.
story: Jolie-Laide makes wines from obscure French varieties grown in California — the name means 'unconventionally attractive' in French, which describes both the wines and the philosophy. Scott Schultz sources from old California vineyards planted with varieties most producers have never heard of.
trivia: Valdiguié was once widely planted in California under the mistaken name 'Napa Gamay' — DNA testing in the 1990s revealed it was actually a southern French variety unrelated to Beaujolais Gamay.
At 12.5% alcohol, Glou d'État is one of the lowest-alcohol reds on the list — a deliberate choice that makes it exceptionally versatile with food.
Jolie-Laide takes its name from the French concept of beauty found in unconventional places — the winery's entire philosophy is built around varieties and styles that mainstream California ignores.

name: '8 Years in the Desert,' Zinfandel/Petite Sirah/Syrah, California, 2023
tableside: Dave Phinney's Orin Swift label built on the success of The Prisoner. 8 Years in the Desert is built around old-vine Zinfandel with Petite Sirah and Syrah: blackberry jam, dark chocolate, vanilla, and spice. Rich, bold, and designed for immediate pleasure.
story: Dave Phinney founded Orin Swift after the success of The Prisoner, building a portfolio of wines designed for immediate enjoyment. 8 Years in the Desert references the time Phinney spent developing his winemaking philosophy before the wines started attracting attention — the desert metaphor for a period of struggle before recognition.
trivia: After selling The Prisoner Wine Company, Dave Phinney founded Orin Swift — named after his father Orion and mother Swift — focusing on bold blends like this one.

Old-vine Zinfandel from California's Lodi and Amador County regions can be over 100 years old — these gnarly, head-trained vines produce concentrated, intensely flavored fruit unlike any younger vineyard.
Petite Sirah adds the deep color and tannic backbone that keeps a wine like this from feeling flabby despite its generous fruit — without it, high-alcohol Zinfandel can fall apart in the glass.

name: Petaluma Gap, Sonoma County, 2022
tableside: The Petaluma Gap AVA is defined by wind — cold Pacific gusts that funnel through a break in the Coast Ranges and cool the vineyards dramatically. Adobe Road's Pinot shows it: red cherry, raspberry, dried rose, and a savory herb note with the kind of natural acidity that makes the wine exceptional with food.
story: The Petaluma Gap was designated its own AVA in 2017 specifically because of the wind — afternoon gusts regularly exceed 30mph, dramatically cooling the vineyards and extending the growing season. The result is Pinot Noir with a distinctly cool-climate character that sets it apart from warmer Sonoma expressions.
trivia: The Petaluma Gap AVA is the only California appellation named specifically for a wind phenomenon — afternoon gusts regularly exceed 30mph, dramatically cooling vineyards that would otherwise be too warm for Pinot Noir.
Adobe Road was founded by Kevin Buckler, a professional endurance racing driver who applies the same data-driven precision to winemaking as to motorsport.
Cool-climate Pinot Noir from the Petaluma Gap typically shows more red fruit and savory character than warmer Sonoma expressions — the wind is the difference.

name: Brunello di Montalcino, Sangiovese, DOCG, 2020
tableside: Argiano is one of Montalcino's oldest estates, making Brunello in a style that is approachable earlier than some producers but no less serious. Dark cherry, dried fig, leather, and iron mineral with firm tannin and a finish that goes on. The 2020 is a warm, generous vintage that shows beautifully now.
story: Argiano has been making wine in Montalcino since the 16th century, predating the modern Brunello appellation by several hundred years. The estate was acquired by Brazilian industrialist Andrés Sotomayor in 1992 and subsequently modernized — new cellars, new vineyard management, and a more international approach to winemaking while maintaining the traditional variety.
trivia: Brunello di Montalcino requires a minimum of five years aging before release — two years in oak and at least four months in bottle. The 2020 vintage will have aged for this minimum before reaching the list.
Argiano's vineyards sit at elevations between 250 and 350 meters on the southwest slopes of Montalcino — a position that provides excellent sun exposure while maintaining fresh afternoon breezes.

The 2020 Brunello vintage was widely praised for producing wines of excellent ripeness with good natural acidity — the combination of warm days and cool nights preserved freshness in what could have been an overripe year.

name: 'Basadone' Pelaverga, Verduno, 2023
tableside: Pelaverga is grown almost exclusively in the tiny commune of Verduno. Castello di Verduno is the benchmark producer, and this is unlike anything else on the list: rose petal, white cherry, white pepper, and cranberry at 13%. Silky, perfumed, and impossible to place without context.
story: Verduno Pelaverga is protected by its own DOC designation — a recognition of the variety's unique character and its near-exclusive geographic concentration in this small commune adjacent to Barolo. Castello di Verduno has championed this grape for generations, producing a wine that is simultaneously one of Piedmont's most obscure and most distinctive.
trivia: Pelaverga Piccolo — the specific clone grown in Verduno — is genetically distinct from the Pelaverga grown elsewhere in Piedmont and is considered the finer of the two varieties.
Verduno is the smallest of the Barolo communes, producing wines that are consistently lighter and more perfumed than those from La Morra, Serralunga, or Castiglione Falletto.
The characteristic white pepper aroma of Verduno Pelaverga comes from a compound called rotundone — the same molecule responsible for the peppery character in Syrah and some Grüner Veltliner.

name: Tempranillo, Gran Reserva, 2011
tableside: A 2011 Rioja Gran Reserva, fifteen years old and in a beautiful drinking window. Dried cherry, tobacco, vanilla oak, leather, and cedar — the full secondary complexity that extended aging in American oak produces. Ontañon holds their wines longer than the legal minimum, and it shows.
story: Bodegas Ontañon is a family estate in the Rioja Alta, making wines in a traditional style that emphasizes extended aging over immediate fruit. Their Gran Reserva spends a minimum of two years in American oak and several more in bottle before release — a commitment to patience that produces wines of genuine complexity.
trivia: Rioja Gran Reserva requires a minimum of five years aging — at least two in oak and two in bottle — before release. Ontañon typically holds their wines considerably longer than this minimum.
The 2011 Rioja vintage was marked by excellent growing conditions and is now considered one of the finest of the decade — the wines are drinking beautifully with a decade of additional bottle age.

American oak, traditionally used in Rioja, imparts vanilla, coconut, and dill notes that are distinctly different from the subtler influence of French oak — it's an acquired taste that divides wine drinkers sharply.

name: Tempranillo, 'Malleolus,' Ribera del Duero, 2022
tableside: Emilio Moro's flagship from old-vine Tempranillo in the Ribera del Duero, where high altitude and extreme continental climate produce wines darker and more structured than Rioja. Dark plum, blackberry, graphite, and an iron mineral note that sets it apart. More muscular than anything in the Rioja section.
story: Bodegas Emilio Moro is a family estate that has farmed the Ribera del Duero for three generations, with the Malleolus vineyard being their most prized holding. The name comes from the Latin for 'hammer' — a reference to the rounded river stones that characterize the vineyard's soils.
trivia: Ribera del Duero sits at elevations of 750-900 meters above sea level — significantly higher than Rioja, producing wines with higher natural acidity and more mineral character despite the extreme continental heat.
The Tempranillo clone grown in Ribera del Duero is called Tinto Fino — genetically identical to Rioja's Tempranillo but producing noticeably different wines due to climate and soil differences.

Emilio Moro pioneered single-vineyard Tempranillo in Ribera del Duero at a time when most producers blended across multiple vineyards — Malleolus was one of the first single-site wines to achieve international recognition from the appellation.

name: Cab Sauv/Merlot, Pessac-Léognan, 2020
tableside: An accessible entry into Pessac-Léognan from a château owned by the same family as Smith Haut Lafitte. Black cherry, tobacco leaf, graphite, and a touch of oak. The 2020 vintage was exceptional across Bordeaux, and even at this price it shows the appellation's characteristic precision.
story: Pessac-Léognan was carved out of the broader Graves appellation in 1987, recognizing the superior quality of the wines from this northern sector immediately adjacent to Bordeaux. Château de Cruzeau benefits from the same gravelly, well-drained soils that define the appellation but at a price point accessible to everyday consumption.
trivia: Pessac-Léognan is the only Left Bank Bordeaux appellation where both red and white wines can receive grand cru classé status — the white wines of Haut-Brion and Domaine de Chevalier are among France's finest whites.
Château de Cruzeau is owned by the Cathiard family, who also own the prestigious Château Smith Haut Lafitte — the operational knowledge flows between the two estates.
The 2020 Bordeaux vintage is one of the great modern vintages — consistently praised for wines of exceptional ripeness and structure that will age for decades while drinking well young.

name: 'Hillside' Syrah/Zweigelt/Merlot, Leithaberg DAC, 2022
tableside: Hillinger's entry-level red from the Leithaberg DAC on the western shore of Lake Neusiedl. Red plum, dark cherry, pepper, and a light herbal note. Accessible and food-friendly, built to show what Burgenland can do with a blend beyond Blaufränkisch alone.
story: Leo Hillinger built his estate in the Leithaberg DAC into one of Burgenland's most modern and design-forward operations — the winery itself is an architectural statement. The Hillside blend reflects his philosophy of making wines that appeal beyond the traditional Austrian red wine audience, using international varieties alongside native Zweigelt.
trivia: The Leithaberg DAC designation was created specifically for wines from the limestone and schist soils on the western shore of Lake Neusiedl — a distinct terroir that produces more mineral, structured reds than the eastern lake shore.
Leo Hillinger is one of Austria's most internationally recognized wine producers, as much for his design aesthetic and marketing as for the wines themselves.
Lake Neusiedl — Europe's largest steppe lake — moderates temperatures across the entire Burgenland wine region, reducing the risk of frost and enabling consistent ripening even in difficult vintages.

name: Willamette Valley, 2024
tableside: Elk Cove's Willamette Valley Chardonnay is lean, bright, and entirely unoaked. Green apple, lemon, white flower, and a clean mineral finish. Cool Oregon temperatures keep the wine fresh in a way that California at this price rarely achieves.
story: Elk Cove is one of Oregon's pioneering estates, farming in the Chehalem Mountains since 1974. Their Willamette Valley Chardonnay is made in a restrained style that reflects the region's natural acidity — a deliberate counterpoint to the richer California expressions.
trivia: Elk Cove was founded in 1974, making it one of Oregon's original wave of serious wine estates — established just after Eyrie and Ponzi pioneered the region.
The Willamette Valley's volcanic and sedimentary soils produce Chardonnay with a mineral precision that distinguishes Oregon from California's richer styles.
Oregon Chardonnay has historically been overshadowed by Oregon Pinot Noir, but producers like Elk Cove are increasingly making the case that it deserves equal attention.

name: Blanc de Blancs, Burgenland, NV
tableside: An Austrian Blanc de Blancs made by traditional method — think of it as Austria's answer to Champagne, with the same fine bubbles but the bright, clean character of Burgenland Welschriesling.
story: The Szigeti brothers founded their sparkling wine house in Gols, Burgenland in 1991, abandoning still wine entirely to focus on méthode traditionelle sparkling. They source from the best vineyard sites around Lake Neusiedl, producing some of Austria's most acclaimed Sekt.
trivia: The Szigeti Blanc de Blancs is made using the same traditional method as Champagne — secondary fermentation in the bottle, extended lees aging, and hand disgorgement. The result is a wine that rivals Champagne in quality at a fraction of the price.

Welschriesling — despite the name — has no genetic relation to German Riesling. It's an entirely separate variety that thrives in Austria and produces wines of bright acidity and clean citrus character, ideal for sparkling wine production.
Burgenland's proximity to Lake Neusiedl — Europe's largest steppe lake — moderates temperatures and creates ideal conditions for slow, even ripening of grapes destined for sparkling wine.

name: Cuvée Brut, NV (Split)
tableside: Laurent-Perrier's Brut NV in an individual 187ml split bottle — the perfect single-glass Champagne experience, or a beautiful way to start a meal for one. Same wine as a full bottle, just in a personal format.
story: Laurent-Perrier is one of Champagne's great family-owned houses, headquartered in Tours-sur-Marne. Known for their elegant, Chardonnay-dominant house style, they are also the house behind the iconic Grande Cuvée and the famous Laurent-Perrier Rosé — one of Champagne's most recognized bottles.
trivia: This is a split — a 187ml individual bottle, roughly one generous glass. It is priced as an individual serving rather than a full bottle.
Laurent-Perrier is one of the few remaining large Champagne houses still entirely family-owned — the Nonancourt family has controlled the estate since 1939.
The house's Brut NV is among the most Chardonnay-dominant of the major Champagne house blends — typically around 50-60% Chardonnay — giving it the freshness and precision the style is known for.

name: Turbiana 'Oasi Mantellina,' Lugana, IT
tableside: Turbiana is the local name for a biotype of Trebbiano found only around Lake Garda — this is Allegrini's venture into Lugana, and it shows their typically elegant touch. Crisp, saline, and surprisingly complex.
story: Allegrini is one of the Veneto's most celebrated families — their Amarone is a benchmark of the style. In 2018 they purchased 40 hectares in Pozzolengo in the Lugana appellation, creating a white wine project that reflects the same commitment to quality they bring to their reds.
trivia: Turbiana is a biotype of Trebbiano di Soave found almost exclusively around Lake Garda — the lake's moderating influence gives the wines a freshness and minerality unusual for northern Italy's white wines.

Lugana DOC spans the border between Lombardy and Veneto — the appellation's unique position on glacial moraine soils left by the retreating Garda glacier gives the wines their characteristic saline, mineral quality.
Allegrini purchased their Lugana estate in 2018 after years of searching — the property surrounds a small protected lake called Mantellina, which gives the wine its full name, Oasi Mantellina.

name: Chardonnay, Napa Valley
tableside: The Vice's Chardonnay is the Napa approach — generous, rich, and toasty. If a guest loves California Chardonnay this is the glass for them, and it's available by the bottle as well.
story: The Vice operates across Napa Valley's spectrum, making both Pinot Noir and Cabernet Sauvignon as their primary bottle list wines. The Chardonnay is their white expression — rich, Napa-styled, and designed for guests who want something with presence.
trivia: Napa Valley Chardonnay is often made in a richer, more oaked style than its Burgundian counterpart — the warmer climate produces riper fruit, and extended barrel aging adds the toasty, creamy character the style is known for.

Malolactic fermentation — converting sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid — is standard practice in Napa Chardonnay production and is responsible for much of the buttery, creamy character guests associate with the style.
The Vice's red wines appear three times on the bottle list — the Chardonnay is exclusively a BTG offering, making it a unique expression from a producer guests may already know.

name: Grüner Veltliner, Ried Loisium, Kamptal
tableside: Classic Kamptal Grüner Veltliner — the white pepper note is the giveaway. Dry, mineral, and food-friendly in the way that only Austrian whites can be. Steininger is also known for their Sekt, which appears several times on the sparkling list.
story: Steininger is a Kamptal estate with a dual identity — serious still wines and one of Austria's finest Sekt programs. The Loisium vineyard sits in the heart of Kamptal's best growing zone, producing Grüner Veltliner with the characteristic white pepper and minerality the variety is famous for.
trivia: The Loisium is also the name of a famous wine spa and hotel in Langenlois built around a labyrinth of historic wine cellars — the Steininger estate is closely associated with the cultural life of the Kamptal wine world.
Kamptal's name comes from the Kamp River, whose valley creates the temperature variation that gives the region's wines their freshness and precision.
Steininger's sparkling wines appear multiple times on the sparkling list — having their still Grüner on the BTG list gives guests a way to explore the producer's range beyond the bubbles.

name: Sauvignon Blanc, Val de Loire, FR
tableside: Loire Sauvignon Blanc at its most honest — all grapefruit and fresh herbs over chalk minerality. Clean and direct, it's the style that made Sancerre famous, at a more accessible price.
story: Paris-Simoneau is a Loire Valley producer working in the classic Sauvignon Blanc tradition of the region — focused on freshness, minerality, and varietal purity rather than oak or weight. The Val de Loire designation covers wines from across the broad Loire appellation.
trivia: Loire Valley Sauvignon Blanc is considered the benchmark for the variety in France — the chalky soils of Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé give the wines a mineral, flinty quality that Sauvignon from warmer regions rarely achieves.

The Loire is France's longest river, stretching over 600 miles from the Massif Central to the Atlantic — the wines along its length vary dramatically from the mineral whites of the upper Loire to the sweeter expressions near the coast.

Sauvignon Blanc's characteristic aromas — grapefruit, gooseberry, cut grass, fresh herbs — come from a group of compounds called thiols, which are released during fermentation and give the variety its instantly recognizable profile.

name: Pinot Noir, Loire Valley, FR
tableside: Loire Pinot Noir is one of wine's best-kept secrets — lighter and more food-friendly than Burgundy, with the same red cherry freshness but more herbal lift. Perfect for guests who want something red but not too heavy.
story: The Loire Valley's Pinot Noir tradition is older than most guests realize — Sancerre Rouge has been made from Pinot Noir for centuries, and the region's cool climate produces a distinctly lighter, more aromatic style than Burgundy. Maison de la Villette works across the Loire's white and red expressions.
trivia: Loire Pinot Noir is often overlooked in favor of the region's famous Sauvignon Blancs and Chenin Blancs — but the variety has been grown here for centuries and produces wines of genuine delicacy and food-friendliness.

The Loire Valley's cool climate is ideal for preserving Pinot Noir's natural acidity and freshness — the wines typically run 12-13% alcohol, making them significantly lighter than California or Burgundy expressions.

Sancerre Rouge — the Loire's most famous Pinot Noir — is made from the same grape variety as the white, grown on the same chalk and flint soils, producing wines with a distinctive mineral quality unique to the region.

name: Super Tuscan, Tuscany, IT
tableside: A classic Super Tuscan blend — Sangiovese with Cabernet Sauvignon — that bridges the gap between Italy and Bordeaux. Approachable and food-friendly, it's a reliable choice for guests who want something Italian with familiar structure.
story: Cantina Vini Tipici dell'Aretino is a cooperative based in Arezzo, in the heart of Tuscany. The Super Tuscan category — blending traditional Italian varieties with international grapes like Cabernet Sauvignon — was born in Tuscany in the 1970s and has become one of Italy's most recognizable wine styles internationally.
trivia: The Super Tuscan category was created in the 1970s when producers like Antinori and Sassicaia began blending Cabernet Sauvignon with Sangiovese — a practice prohibited under the existing Chianti DOC rules, forcing them to label their wines as humble table wine despite their exceptional quality.

Arezzo — the province where this producer is based — sits in eastern Tuscany between Florence and Rome, a historically important area for both art and agriculture. Piero della Francesca's famous frescoes are in Arezzo.

The IGT (Indicazione Geografica Tipica) designation, created in 1992, gave legal identity to Super Tuscan wines that had been classified as table wine — allowing producers to use non-traditional varieties while still indicating geographic origin.

name: Tempranillo/Garnacha, Crianza, Rioja, SP
tableside: Rioja Crianza — the entry point into Rioja's aging classification system. At least two years of aging including one in American oak gives it the vanilla and spice that defines the classic Rioja style. A natural partner for red meat.
story: Ontañon has been making wine in Rioja for generations. Their Gran Reserva appears on the bottle list — the Crianza is the younger, more approachable expression from the same house, showing the classic Rioja style of Tempranillo with American oak at an accessible price point.
trivia: Rioja's Crianza designation requires a minimum of two years aging — at least one year in oak barrels. The American oak traditionally used in Rioja imparts the vanilla and dill notes that define the region's classic style.
Garnacha (Grenache) has been blended with Tempranillo in Rioja for centuries — it adds roundness, red fruit, and body to the more structured, tannic Tempranillo base.
Ontañon's Gran Reserva appears elsewhere on the bottle list — having the Crianza as a BTG pour gives guests an opportunity to explore the same producer at different aging levels and price points.

name: Cabernet Sauvignon, North Coast, CA
tableside: Black Stallion's North Coast Cabernet is the house pour — rich, approachable California Cab with the dark fruit and vanilla that guests expect from the style. The estate's Sam Jasper Napa Valley bottling on the bottle list is the prestige tier if they want to step up.
story: Black Stallion Winery occupies a former equestrian facility in Oakville, Napa Valley. Their portfolio spans from the North Coast Cabernet — the accessible BTG tier — to the single-vineyard Sam Jasper Napa Valley Cabernet on the bottle list. The North Coast designation allows fruit sourcing from across a broad region including Napa, Sonoma, Lake, and Mendocino counties.
trivia: The North Coast AVA is one of California's broadest appellations, covering six counties north of San Francisco Bay — Napa, Sonoma, Mendocino, Lake, Marin, and Solano. It allows producers to blend across a wide area while still indicating a California origin.

Black Stallion's two Cabernets on the list — this North Coast BTG pour and the Sam Jasper Napa Valley on the bottle list — illustrate how dramatically appellation specificity affects both character and price in California wine.
The winery building was originally constructed as an equestrian center before being converted to wine production — the stable aesthetic and horse imagery throughout the brand references that history.
